EDCS-09700135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publi Octavi Publi liberti Albani et Octaviae Publi libertae Thaidi Acte et Phyllis libertae\n
EDCS-09700135.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Publius Octavius Albanus, freedman of Publius, and Octavia Thais, freedwoman of Publius, and Phyllis, freedwoman.
EDCS-09700135.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to freed slaves and their patrons, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-09700947.text_edition: \n Luci Laroni Rufi VIviri decurionum decreto Aulo Curio Sacerdoti Laronia Secunda soror viva fecit fratri et filio sibi posterisque suis in fronte pedes XV in agro pedes XX nummi\n
EDCS-09700947.translation: Laronia Secunda, sister, made this for her brother and son, and for herself and her descendants, during her lifetime, by decree of the town council, for Lucius Laronius Rufus, VIvir. 15 feet in front, 20 feet in width of land.
EDCS-09700947.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Laronia Secunda for her brother, son, and herself, honoring Lucius Laronius Rufus, a member of the college of VIviri, and includes land dimensions.

EDCS-09700950.text_edition: \n - Aurelius Sexti filius flamen IIIIvir iure dicundo praefectus fabrum tribunus militum a populo vixit annos LX\n
EDCS-09700950.translation: — Aurelius Sextus, son of Sextus, flamen, IIIIvir with judicial authority, prefect of engineers, tribune of the soldiers elected by the people, lived 60 years.
EDCS-09700950.summary: This inscription records the career and lifespan of Aurelius Sextus, a prominent local magistrate and military officer, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-09700951.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the name and age of Nicanor.

EDCS-09700952.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the name and age of Licinius Eros.

EDCS-09700953.text_edition: \n Scornia Cai filia annorum XXVI optuma femina\n
EDCS-09700953.translation: Scornia, daughter of Caius, aged 26, an excellent woman.
EDCS-09700953.summary: This epitaph praises Scornia, the daughter of Caius, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-09700954.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a funerary or dedication text, identifying Lucius Veratius.

EDCS-09701452.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Arruntio Ianuario Ulpia Successa coniunxs sic quae vixit cum eo annos X menses X dies V Dis M(anibus) Iulius Valens gener socero suo bene merenti fecit \n
EDCS-09701452.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Arruntius Ianuarius. Ulpia Successa, his wife, who lived with him 10 years, 10 months, and 5 days. Julius Valens, his son-in-law, made this for his deserving father-in-law. To the Spirits of the Departed.
EDCS-09701452.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Arruntius Ianuarius, erected by his son-in-law, and also mentions his wife, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-09701453.translation: For Marcus Artidius Justus, the good father.
EDCS-09701453.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary epitaph or dedication, honoring Marcus Artidius Justus.

EDCS-09701454.text_edition: \n Decimus Livius Decimi libertus Gora viva Livia Decimi liberta Truphera sic Decimus Livius Decimi libertus Epaphroditus Decimus Livius Decimi et |(mulieris) libertus Priscus\n
EDCS-09701454.translation: Decimus Livius Gora, freedman of Decimus, and his wife Livia Truphera, freedwoman of Decimus, [set this up] for Decimus Livius Epaphroditus, freedman of Decimus, and Priscus, freedman of Decimus and [his wife].
EDCS-09701454.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by Decimus Livius Gora and his wife Livia Truphera for freedmen associated with their household.

EDCS-09701457.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Herenniae Ianuariae Marcus Herennius coniugi \n
EDCS-09701457.translation: To the divine shades of Herennia Ianuaria, Marcus Herennius [erected this] for his wife.
EDCS-09701457.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Herennius to his wife, Herennia Ianuaria.

EDCS-09701458.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gai Laberi Ligri iuvenum et Cai Rulli et Publius Rullius optim optimorum Veratia I\n
EDCS-09701458.translation: To the divine shades. Veratia I [set this up] for Gaius Laberius Ligur, dearest of youths, and for Gaius Rullius and Publius Rullius, dearest of the dearest.
EDCS-09701458.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Veratia I to several young men, including Gaius Laberius Ligur, Gaius Rullius, and Publius Rullius.

EDCS-09701460.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Tito Minucio Hermae Titus Calpurnius Agatho amico posuit\n
EDCS-09701460.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Titus Calpurnius Agatho erected [this] for his friend Titus Minucius Hermas.
EDCS-09701460.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Titus Calpurnius Agatho for his friend Titus Minucius Hermas.

EDCS-09701461.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sexto Pompeio Martiali Caesennia Felicula coniugi karissimo cum quo vixit annis XXVII item Pompeius Martialis filius patri indulgentissimo\n
EDCS-09701461.translation: To the divine shades. Caesennia Felicula [set this up] for her dearest husband Sextus Pompeius Martialis, with whom she lived for 27 years; also for Pompeius Martialis, their most loving son, for his father.
EDCS-09701461.summary: This funerary inscription is a joint dedication by Caesennia Felicula and her son Pompeius Martialis to Sextus Pompeius Martialis, husband and father respectively.

EDCS-09701462.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sallustiae Ianuariae fecit Iulia Cnome sic filia matri optimae\n
EDCS-09701462.translation: To the divine shades. Iulia Cnome, daughter, made [this] for her excellent mother Sallustia Ianuaria.
EDCS-09701462.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Iulia Cnome for her mother, Sallustia Ianuaria.

EDCS-09701463.text_edition: \n Valeria Decimi liberta Psychario monumentum fecit sibi et viro suo Lucio Quintio Luci liberto Philodamo de suo se viva\n
EDCS-09701463.translation: Valeria Psycharis, freedwoman of Decimus, erected this monument for herself and for her husband Lucius Quintius Philodamus, freedman of Lucius, at her own expense while alive.
EDCS-09701463.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument erected by Valeria Psycharis for herself and her husband Lucius Quintius Philodamus.

EDCS-09701464.text_edition: \n Caius Manneius Quinti filius Ro Caius Manneius Cai libertus Rufus Caius Manneius Cai libertus Chilo Caius Manneius Cai libertus Lupercus monumentum factum est ex testimonio Rufi Chilonis Luperci\n
EDCS-09701464.translation: This monument was erected by testament of Rufus, Chilo, and Lupercus, freedmen of Gaius, sons of Caius Manneius [and freedmen of Gaius].
EDCS-09701464.summary: This inscription records a monument established according to the will of three freedmen: Rufus, Chilo, and Lupercus, all associated with Caius Manneius.

EDCS-09701472.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying Gaius Manneius, son of Quintus.

EDCS-09701473.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying Lucius Anicius, son of Lucius.

EDCS-09701476.text_edition: \n Appia Quinti filia vixit annos LXV\n
EDCS-09701476.translation: Appia, daughter of Quintus, lived for 65 years.
EDCS-09701476.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Appia, recording her age at death.

EDCS-09701478.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Cosconio Longino collegius sic aeneatores\n
EDCS-09701478.translation: To the Divine Spirits. The trumpeters of the guild, from the guild of Cosconius Longinus.
EDCS-09701478.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Divine Spirits made by the trumpeters of a guild associated with Cosconius Longinus.

EDCS-10700698.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Lucius Arrius Titi filius Galeria Titianus IVNI posuit laetus\n
EDCS-10700698.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Lucius Arrius Titianus, son of Titus, of the Galeria tribe, erected this happily.
EDCS-10700698.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Arrius Titianus, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-10700702.text_edition: \n Helenus et Acilia Attica eius dono dederunt\n
EDCS-10700702.translation: Helenus and his wife Acilia Attica gave this as a gift.
EDCS-10700702.summary: This records a gift made by Helenus and Acilia Attica, likely on a monument or object they donated.

EDCS-10700706.text_edition: \n Marcus Gellius procurator Augusti decuria et praet cum fratre suo Altiniano individuus vixit et \n
EDCS-10700706.translation: Marcus Gellius, procurator of Augustus, served in the decuria and praet with his brother Altinianus, lived devotedly and...
EDCS-10700706.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording the service of Marcus Gellius as an imperial procurator and his relationship with his brother.

EDCS-10700708.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Aquilio Cai filio Claudia Verecundo Celeia militi cohortis IIX praetoriae centuria Vetti vixit nnos XX militavit nnos II Censurinus sic Secundus et Aelius Severus\n
EDCS-10700708.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Gaius Aquilius Verecundus, son of Gaius, of the Claudia tribe, a soldier of the 8th Praetorian cohort, Vettius' century. He lived 20 years, served 2 years. Censurinus and Aelius Severus made this.
EDCS-10700708.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Aquilius Verecundus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, detailing his age and service, and listing the dedicator(s).

EDCS-10700709.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelio Saturnino militi torquato legionis primes sic Italicae qui vixsit sic annis XL messibus sic VI diebus X militavit annis XIIII Aurelius Arborius fratri bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-10700709.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. For Aurelius Saturninus, a torquatus soldier of the First Italian Legion, who lived 40 years, 6 months, and 10 days. He served for 14 years. Aurelius Arborius made this for his deserving brother.
EDCS-10700709.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Saturninus, a soldier in the First Italian Legion, detailing his age, service, and dedicating relative.

EDCS-10700711.text_edition: \n Caius Egnatius Cai libertus Inachus Caius Egnatius Marci filius Horatia Maticanus IIIIvir Caius Egnatius Cai libertus Felix\n
EDCS-10700711.translation: Gaius Egnatius Inachus, freedman of Gaius. Gaius Egnatius Maticanus, son of Marcus, of the Horatia tribe, a quaestor. Gaius Egnatius Felix, freedman of Gaius.
EDCS-10700711.summary: This inscription lists several individuals with their names, parentage, tribal affiliations, and/or freedman status, possibly related to a family or collegium.

EDCS-10700720.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus hic posita esit sic Acceta signu sic nomine Firma et fecerunt Cupressenia Amabilis idemque nomine Hermesiana \n
EDCS-10700720.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Here was placed Acceta, who was called Firma, and she made it; and Cupressenia Amabilis, also known by the name Hermesiana, made it.
EDCS-10700720.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Acceta (also known as Firma) and identifies Cupressenia Amabilis (also known as Hermesiana) as the maker(s) of the monument.

EDCS-10700721.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Gaius Attius, with illegible text following.

EDCS-10700722.text_edition: \n Marcus Cincius Rufus Cincia Marci liberta Aucta\n
EDCS-10700722.translation: Marcus Cincius Rufus and Aucta, freedwoman of Marcus Cincius.
EDCS-10700722.summary: This inscription names Marcus Cincius Rufus and his freedwoman Aucta, likely a funerary or memorial text.

EDCS-10700724.text_edition: \n ------] s Lucius Clodius Luci filius Arnensis Lucius Clodius\n
EDCS-10700724.translation: Lucius Clodius, son of Lucius, of the Arnensis tribe; Lucius Clodius.
EDCS-10700724.summary: This inscription names Lucius Clodius, son of Lucius, of the Arnensis tribe, with a possible repetition of his name, potentially a funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-10700728.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Firmiae Marci libertae Salutari coniugi bene merenti Titus Flavius Secundus posuit\n
EDCS-10700728.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Secundus set this up for Firmia Salutaris, freedwoman of Marcus, his deserving wife.
EDCS-10700728.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Titus Flavius Secundus to his deserving wife, Firmia Salutaris, a freedwoman, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-10700731.text_edition: \n [Dis Manibus] Cai Iuni Hilari vixit annos LXXXV Caius Iunius Priscus patri bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-10700731.translation: [To the departed spirits.] Caius Iunius Hilarius lived 85 years. Caius Iunius Priscus made this for his deserving father.
EDCS-10700731.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Caius Iunius Hilarius at age 85, with the monument erected by his son Caius Iunius Priscus.

EDCS-10700733.text_edition: \n Publius Marius Luci libertus Hospes Maria Fausta uxor\n
EDCS-10700733.translation: Publius Marius Hospes, freedman of Lucius, and his wife Maria Fausta.
EDCS-10700733.summary: This inscription names Publius Marius Hospes, freedman of Lucius, and his wife Maria Fausta, possibly a funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-10700735.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Optatus coniunx Phoebenis sororis Aprionis bene merenti fecit vixit nnos XXVIII\n
EDCS-10700735.translation: To the departed spirits. Optatus made this for Phoebene, sister of Aprionis, his deserving wife, who lived 28 years.
EDCS-10700735.summary: This funerary inscription records that Optatus dedicated this to his deserving wife Phoebene, sister of Aprionis, who died at the age of 28.

EDCS-10700736.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Pisivanae |(mulieris) libertae Vitalini vixit annis XXX mensibus II Caius Lutatius Cai filius Horatia Velitius Ianuarius Pisivana |(mulieris) liberta Felicula patrona Caius Caesonius Cai filius Priscus vir coniugi dulcissimae fecerunt\n
EDCS-10700736.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Pisivana, freedwoman, Vitalini, lived 30 years and 2 months. Caius Lutatius, son of Caius, of the Horatia tribe; Velitius Ianuarius and Felicula, freedwoman of Pisivana, patrona, Caius Caesonius, son of Caius, Priscus, made this for their most sweet wife and patrona.
EDCS-10700736.summary: This complex funerary inscription honors Pisivana, a freedwoman who lived 30 years and 2 months, and her patrona Felicula, with dedications from Caius Lutatius and Caius Caesonius Priscus.

EDCS-10700739.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Successae filiae dulcissimae quae vixit nnis IIII mensibus VIII diebus XXIII Hyacinthus et Dionysias parentes fecerunt\n
EDCS-10700739.translation: To the departed spirits. Hyacinthus and Dionysias made this for their most sweet daughter Successa, who lived 4 years, 8 months, and 23 days.
EDCS-10700739.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by parents Hyacinthus and Dionysias to their daughter Successa, who died at the age of 4 years, 8 months, and 23 days.

EDCS-10700741.summary: This is a short memorial dedication to Victoria.

EDCS-10700742.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus fecit Urbica filibus sic suis dulcissimis Eutychiano qui vixit annis VI mensibus XI diebus VII item Sophos sic qui vixit annis V mensibus IIII diebus XIX\n
EDCS-10700742.translation: To the departed spirits. Urbica made this for her most sweet sons Eutychianus, who lived 6 years, 11 months, and 7 days, and Sophos, who lived 5 years, 4 months, and 19 days.
EDCS-10700742.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Urbica to her two sons, Eutychianus and Sophos, detailing their ages at death.

EDCS-10700752.text_edition: \n SECUND ex fundo Sueti Caesetiano D pequnia sic DA \n
EDCS-10700752.translation: From the Suetius Caesetianus estate, money given, thus recorded.
EDCS-10700752.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of payment or distribution of money from a specific estate, likely related to administrative or financial matters, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-10900471.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the burial of an individual named Geminianus in the month of January, dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10900481.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely refers to a period of three days related to the month of March (Martias), possibly concerning a religious observance or event, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-10900499.text_edition: \n Secunda Aurelius Victor Ulpiae Secundae coniugi carissimae fecit Vibes sic in Deo\n
EDCS-10900499.translation: Secunda Aurelius Victor made this for his dearest wife Ulpia Secunda, Vives thus in God.
EDCS-10900499.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelius Victor to his beloved wife Secunda, indicating a late Roman or early Christian context due to the phrase 'in Deo', with an uncertain date.

EDCS-11600071.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Chresimus, a colonist of Lunensis, and Geminia, parents, placed this for Geminia Caviana and her family, a well-deserving person. The burial plot, bought from Claudius Saturninus and Claudius Ferminus, which Chresimus, a colonist of Lunensis, purchased. Burial plot.
EDCS-11600071.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the purchase and dedication of a burial plot for Geminia Caviana and her family by Chresimus and Geminia, colonists of Lunensis, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11600077.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Vibius [name missing] lived for [one?] year, [space missing] Pr[...]
EDCS-11600077.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Lucius Vibius, mentioning his lifespan and possibly part of his name or origin, dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11600085.translation: Gaius Luxsius Euphemus, freedman of Gaius, [a member] of the Six, [a member] of the Augustales, and from two decuries, [a] decurial lictor and town crier for the curule aediles, [made this] for himself, and for Gaius Luxsius Epaphroditus, patron, and for his dearest wife Luxsia Rhodope, and for his freedman Gaius Luxsius Hermetus, well-deserving, and for his freedman Gaius Luxsius Atimetus, and for his freedman-born Gaius Luxsius Verus, and for his freedman Gaius Luxsius Synistor, and for his freedwoman Luxsia Mae, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-11600085.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription dedicated by Gaius Luxsius Euphemus, a prominent freedman holding various civic and religious offices, for himself and his family including freedmen and their descendants, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100852.text_edition: \n Impperatores Caessares Flavius Valerius Constantius Galerius Valerius Maximianus Pii Felices Invicti Augusti pontifices maximi Germanici maximi V Sarmatici maximi III Persici maximi II Britannici maximi II Carpici maximi V Armeniaci maximi Medici maximi Adiabenici maximi tribunicia potestate XVI sic consules VI patres patriae proconsules ddomini nnostri Diocletianus et Maximianus patres Auggustorum et Caessarum ddomini nnostri Severi(us) et Maximinus nobilissimi Caessares nomina militum qui militaverunt in cohortibus praetoriis Auggustroum et Caessarum decem I II III IIII V VI VII VIII VIIII X piis vindicibus qui pie et fortiter militia functi sunt ius tribuimus conubii dumtaxat cum singulis et primis uxoribus ut etiamsi peregrinis iuris feminas in matrimonio suo iunxerint proinde liberos tollant acxi sic ex duobus civibus Romanis natos ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias ddominis nnostris Constantio Augusto VI et Maximiano Augusto VI conssulibus cohors VIIII praetoria Augustorum et Caesarum Valerius Clemens natione Italus descriptum et reconnitum sic ex tabul<a> aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro pos<t> templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Impperatores Caessares Flavius Valerius Constantius Galerius Valerius Maximianus Pii Felices Invicti Augusti pontifices maximi Germanici maximi Sarmatici maximi III Persici maximi II Britannici maximi II Carpici maximi V Armeniaci maximi Adiabenici maximi tribunicia potestate XVI sic consules VI patres patriae domini nostri Diocletianus Maximianus patres Auggustorum et Caessarum domini nostri Severi(us) et Maximinus nobilissimi Caesares ante diem VII Idus Ianuarias ddominis nnostris Constantio Augusto VI et Maximiano Augusto VI conssulibus cohors VIIII praetoria Auggustorum et Caessarum Valerius Clemens natione Italus Valeri Gaiani Valeri Albini Valeri Vitalis Valeri Valentini Valeri Victoris Valeri Traiani Valeri Ianuari\n
EDCS-12100852.translation: The Emperors Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantius, Galerius Valerius Maximianus, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augusti, chief priests, conquerors of the Germans, conquerors of the Sarmatians thrice, conquerors of the Persians twice, conquerors of the Britons twice, conquerors of the Carpatians five times, conquerors of the Armenians, conquerors of the Medes, conquerors of the Adiabenes, with tribunician power sixteen times, consuls six times, fathers of the country, proconsuls, our lords Diocletian and Maximian, fathers of the Augusti and Caesars, our lords Severus and Maximinus, most noble Caesars, with tribunician power sixteen times, consuls six times, fathers of the country. Our lords Diocletian and Maximian, fathers of the Augusti and Caesars, our lords Severus and Maximinus, most noble Caesars, on the day before the Ides of January, in the consulship of our lords Constantius Augustus VI and Maximian Augustus VI, the ninth praetorian cohort of the Augusti and Caesars. Valerius Clemens, of Italian nationality, has been registered and recognised according to the bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall near the temple of the divine Augustus, by Minerva. The Emperors Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantius, Galerius Valerius Maximianus, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augusti, chief priests, conquerors of the Germans, conquerors of the Sarmatians thrice, conquerors of the Persians twice, conquerors of the Britons twice, conquerors of the Carpatians five times, conquerors of the Armenians, conquerors of the Medes, conquerors of the Adiabenes, with tribunician power sixteen times, consuls six times, fathers of the country. Our lords Diocletian and Maximian, fathers of the Augusti and Caesars, our lords Severus and Maximinus, most noble Caesars, on the day before the Ides of January, in the consulship of our lords Constantius Augustus VI and Maximian Augustus VI, the ninth praetorian cohort of the Augusti and Caesars. Valerius Clemens, of Italian nationality, of Valerius Gaianus, of Valerius Albinus, of Valerius Vitalis, of Valerius Valentinus, of Valerius Victor, of Valerius Traianus, of Valerius Ianuar[ius].
EDCS-12100852.summary: This is a rescript of the emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, Galerius, Severus, and Maximinus, issued in 303 CE, granting the right of legal marriage to soldiers of the ninth praetorian cohort, recorded on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-12700155.text_edition: \n Genio coloniae Hebae Quintus Peternius Amphio VIvir Augustalis de sua pecunia dedicavit idem populo crustulum et mulsum dedit\n
EDCS-12700155.translation: To the genius of the colony of Heba. Quintus Peternius Amphio, a Six, an Augustalis, dedicated this from his own money; he also gave cakes and mulsum to the people.
EDCS-12700155.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Quintus Peternius Amphio, a local magistrate and priest, to the genius of the colony of Heba, also mentioning a public offering of food and drink, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800369.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Diocletiano seniori Augusto Maximianus senior Augustus frater et Constantius et Maximianus Augusti et Severus et Maximinus \n
EDCS-12800369.translation: To our lord Diocletian, the elder Augustus; Maximian, the elder Augustus, his brother; and Augusti Constantius and Maximian; and Caesars Severus and Maximinus.
EDCS-12800369.summary: This inscription is a dedication or acclamation of the reigning emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, Galerius, Severus, and Maximinus, likely from the period of their joint rule in the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-12800387.text_edition: \n ------ Se]cundus publicus Russellanorum fecit publicum coloniae Russellanorum pu[blicum coloniae Russellanorum ------ publicum coloniae Russellanorum E S P R fudit Secundus publicus Russellanorum fecit publicum [coloniae Russellanorum ------ Secundus pu[blicus Russellanorum fecit ------\n
EDCS-12800387.translation: Secundus publicus, freedman of the Russellani, made the public [building] for the colony of Russellani... Secundus publicus, freedman of the Russellani, made the public [building] for the colony of Russellani... Secundus publicus, freedman of the Russellani, made the public [building] for the colony of Russellani...
EDCS-12800387.summary: This inscription records the construction of a public building for the colony of Russellani by Secundus, a freedman.

EDCS-12800722.text_edition: \n Titia Luci filia vixit annos XIto data est XIIX Kalendas Octobres Cnaeo Pisone Lucio Sestio consulibus\n
EDCS-12800722.translation: Titia, daughter of Lucius, lived for 11 years and was given in marriage on the 18th day before the Kalends of October, during the consulship of Gnaeus Piso and Lucius Sestius.
EDCS-12800722.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and marriage date of Titia, daughter of Lucius, during the consulship of Piso and Sestius.

EDCS-13300446.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Octavia Thermuthis made this for her most devoted husband, Marcus Octavius Chrysanthus, well deserving.
EDCS-13300446.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Octavia Thermuthis for her husband Marcus Octavius Chrysanthus.

EDCS-13302337.translation: To Marcus Agrippa, son of Marcus?
EDCS-13302337.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely commemorating Marcus Agrippa.

EDCS-13302351.text_edition: \n ------] Sexti libertus Niger Cai libertus Hylas curatores seviri Augustales \n
EDCS-13302351.translation: ... Niger, freedman of Sextus, and Hylas, freedman of Caius, were curators and seviri Augustales.
EDCS-13302351.summary: This inscription records the service of Niger and Hylas as curators and seviri Augustales, likely overseeing a public project.

EDCS-13302358.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Eubiae Zmaragdidi Hermetis Eubi Eubi fili libertae fecit Eubius Rusellanus matri suae feminae bene merenti quae vixit annis XXVIIII\n
EDCS-13302358.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Eubius Russellanus made this for his mother, Eubia, freedwoman of Zmaragdus of Hermetis, a well-deserving woman who lived 29 years.
EDCS-13302358.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Eubia and was dedicated by her son, Eubius Russellanus.

EDCS-13302360.text_edition: \n onio Arnensi ex testamento Sexti fili \n
EDCS-13302360.translation: ... from the will of Sextus, son of Arnius.
EDCS-13302360.summary: This fragment indicates a bequest or execution of a will, related to Sextus, son of Arnius.

EDCS-13302361.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus fili- qui vixit nnis VIIII LInus Iulia iva posuerunt\n
EDCS-13302361.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Linus and Iulia Iva set up [this monument] for their son, who lived 9 years.
EDCS-13302361.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Linus and Iulia Iva for their deceased son.

EDCS-13302516.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sexto Mintio Aquilino Vetui sic equiti Romano filio\n
EDCS-13302516.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Sextus Mintius Aquilinus Vetustus, son of Equiti Romano.
EDCS-13302516.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Sextus Mintius Aquilinus Vetustus, son of Equiti Romano.

EDCS-13400029.text_edition: \n Manius Silius Epaphroditus amphitheatrum a solo pecunia sua fecit coloniae Iuliae Felici Lucoferoniensium idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-13400029.translation: Manius Silius Epaphroditus built the amphitheater from his own funds for the colony of Iulia Felici Lucoferoniensium, and he also dedicated it.
EDCS-13400029.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of an amphitheater by Manius Silius Epaphroditus for the colony of Lucoferonensium.

EDCS-13400030.text_edition: \n ------ Au]gustales [ Manius S]ilius Epaphroditus Consius Lucius Vallius Lucius Cuspius Titus Flav[ius ------\n
EDCS-13400030.translation: To the Augustales: Manius Silius Epaphroditus, Consius, Lucius Vallius, Lucius Cuspius, Titus Flavius.
EDCS-13400030.summary: This inscription lists members of a collegium, likely the Augustales, in an uncertain location.

EDCS-13400207.summary: This is a very short inscription possibly referring to the allied status of the inhabitants of Tarquinia.

EDCS-13500533.text_edition: \n Marco Agrippae Luci filio consuli III tribunicia potestate O \n
EDCS-13500533.translation: To Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, consul for the third time, with tribunician power.
EDCS-13500533.summary: This is an honorary inscription dedicated to Marcus Agrippa, likely from the late 1st century BCE given his consulships and tribunician power.

EDCS-13600245.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cnaei Domiti Modesti fili vixit nnum I menses X Cnaeus Domitius Paris delicato et liberto suo fecit\n
EDCS-13600245.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cnaeus Domitius Paris made this for his son, Cnaeus Domitius Modestus, who lived for 1 year and 10 months, his beloved freedman.
EDCS-13600245.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a freedman for his son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13600246.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Petroniae Edis feminae innocentissimae quae vixit annos menses VI dies XI Aurelius? --- miles praetorianus stationarius Hebae coniugi karissimae et Aurelio filio qui vixit annum I menses II dies crudelissimo Dis Manibus Caio Petisio Cai filio Sabatina o Petisii Legitimus et Secunda filio dulcissimo qui vixit annos VII parentes infelicissimi filio fecerunt\n
EDCS-13600246.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Petronia Edis, most innocent woman, who lived 6 years, 6 months, and 11 days; Aurelius [---], a praetorian guardsman, made this for his most beloved wife and Aurelius, their son, who lived 1 year, 2 months, and a cruel [fate]. To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Gaius Petisius, son of Gaius, from the Sabina tribe, and Secunda, his sweetest son, who lived 7 years, his most unhappy parents made this.
EDCS-13600246.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription commemorating multiple family members, likely from the Roman Imperial period, highlighting the grief of the parents.

EDCS-13600247.text_edition: \n Aulus Caecina Auli filius S Caius Caecina Auli filius \n
EDCS-13600247.translation: Aulus Caecina, son of Aulus, [son of] Caius Caecina, son of Aulus.
EDCS-13600247.summary: This inscription appears to be a formal declaration of lineage or ownership, likely from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-13600248.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely naming an individual with possible Etruscan connections (Vibenna), from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-13800147.text_edition: \n Tito Nasidio Messori veterano ex equitibus speculatoribus donis donato militaribus ab Augusto adlecto ex decreto decurionum remissa honoraria aedilitate IIviro colonia Iulia Felici Luco Feroniae Hedia Verecunda uxor locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-13800147.translation: To Titus Nasidius Messor, veteran, formerly an eques speculator (bodyguard), honored with military gifts by Augustus, enrolled by decree of the decurions, with remission of his second aedileship and his office of IIvir, in the colony Iulia Felici of Lucius Feronia. Hedia Verecunda, his wife, was granted this place by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13800147.summary: This honorary inscription, likely from the Augustan period, details the career and honors of Titus Nasidius Messor in the colony of Iulia Felici.

EDCS-13800148.text_edition: \n Caio Musano Cai filio primo pilo bis tribuno militum praefecto stratopedarchi IIviro quinquennali ex decreto decurionum publice\n
EDCS-13800148.translation: To Gaius Musanus, son of Gaius, primus pilus, twice military tribune, prefect of the camp, quinquennial IIvir, publicly [honored] by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13800148.summary: This is a public honorary inscription from a Roman colony, likely from the early to mid-Imperial period, recognizing the civic and military achievements of Gaius Musanus.

EDCS-13800149.text_edition: \n Iuliae Augustae matri castrorum Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi fortissimi felicissimi patris patriae et Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Parthici Britannici maximi matri Capenates foederati c(ura) Manili Crescentis praetoris Dedicata XIIII Kalendas Octobres Saturnino et Gallo conssulibus\n
EDCS-13800149.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the camps of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, the strongest, happiest, father of the fatherland, and of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Parthicus Britannicus Maximus. The allied people of Capena [dedicated this] under the care of Manilius Crescentis, praetor. Dedicated on the 14th day before the Kalends of October, when Saturninus and Gallus were consuls.
EDCS-13800149.summary: This dedication, from 203 CE, was made by the allied people of Capena to Julia Domna (referred to as Julia Augusta) and likely Caracalla, honoring their roles as imperial patrons.

EDCS-13800150.text_edition: \n Iuliae Tiberi filiae Paulinae sacerdoti Cereris municipii Capenatium foederatorum ob honorem sacerdotalem honestissimis caerimoniis praebitum decuriones item municipes et postea sacerdoti Veneris bis epulum et sportulas decurionibus et municipibus praebuit locus datus decreto decurionum dedicata VI Kalendas Martias Glabrione II consule curatore Lucio Mucio Muciano marito eius\n
EDCS-13800150.translation: To Iulia Paulina, daughter of Tiberius, priestess of Ceres of the federated municipality of Capena, for the honor of priesthood provided with the most distinguished ceremonies, the councilors and also the townspeople, and later as priestess of Venus, she twice provided a banquet and gifts to the councilors and townspeople. A site was granted by decree of the councilors. Dedicated on the sixth day before the Kalends of March in the second consulship of Glabrio, with Lucius Mucius Mucianus, her husband, as curator.
EDCS-13800150.summary: This is a dedication honoring Iulia Paulina, a priestess in Capena, for her services and generosity, likely dating to the consulship of Glabrio.

EDCS-13800151.text_edition: \n Variae Italiae Pacati Fausti sacerdoti et cultrici Cereris et Veneris dignissimae ob merita eius decuriones Augustales honorati et vicani pecunia collata posuerunt eique locum publice censuerunt\n
EDCS-13800151.translation: To Varia Vitalis, wife of Pacatus, most worthy priestess and devotee of Ceres and Venus, for her merits, the honored councilors, Augustales, and villagers, with money collected, have set this up and officially decreed a place for her.
EDCS-13800151.summary: This inscription honors Varia Vitalis, a priestess of Ceres and Venus in Capena, for her merits, set up by local officials and villagers.

EDCS-13800152.text_edition: \n Lucio Valerio Luci filio Fabia Timiniano equo publico omnes honores consecuto aedilique alimentorum quaestori pecuniae publicae reciperendae succuratori muneris aedili Capenatium quinquennali municipii Capenatium foederatorum perpetuo qqaestores ex decreto ordinis conlatione facta decurionum et Augustalium item vicanorum ob merita eius curantibus Plotio Saturnino et Caio Licinio Secundino\n
EDCS-13800152.translation: To Lucius Valerius Timinianus, son of Lucius, of the Fabia tribe, who, having received a public horse, achieved all honors, and served as aedile of the grain dole, quaestor for receiving public funds, helper in the games, quinquennial aedile of the federated municipality of Capena, and perpetual quaestor, from a decree of the council and contributions made by the councilors and Augustales, and also the villagers, for his merits, with Plotus Saturninus and Gaius Licinius Secundinus overseeing.
EDCS-13800152.summary: This is a dedication honoring Lucius Valerius Timinianus of Capena for his many public offices and contributions, likely a senatorial or equestrian figure.

EDCS-13800153.text_edition: \n Marco Gellio Servando Seniori seviro Augustali municipio Capeneatium sic foederatorum ludos edenti ob merita eius collatione facta decurionum et Augustalium item vicanorum hoc sic honorem sibi oblatum HS V milia nummum in aerarium rei publicae Capenatium foederatorum contulit ut ex eo die natali sui V Kalendas Apriles de |(denariis) LXXV usurae decurionibus et Augustalibus et vicanis dividatur praesentibus et ex ea divisione iubeo statuae meae coronas emi |(denariis) III loco dato ex decreto decurionum curantibus Caio Naevio Proculo et Marco Gellio Servando Iuniore et Vatinio Prisco scriba publico rei publicae Capenatium foederatorum Dedicata est III Kalendas Decembres Maximo et Orfito conssulibus\n
EDCS-13800153.translation: To Marcus Gellius Servandus Senior, Augustalis of the federated municipality of Capena, who, while presenting games, for his merits, with contributions made by the councilors and Augustales, and also the villagers, contributed HS 5,000 from this honor offered to himself into the treasury of the federated municipality of Capena, so that from that day on his birthday, the fifth day before the Kalends of April, interest of 75 denarii should be divided among the councilors, Augustales, and villagers, and with those present, and from that division, I order that crowns for my statue be bought for 3 denarii. A site was granted by decree of the councilors, with Gaius Naevius Proculus, Marcus Gellius Servandus Junior, and Vatinus Priscus, public scribe of the federated municipality of Capena, overseeing. It was dedicated on the third day before the Kalends of December in the consulship of Maximus and Orfitus.
EDCS-13800153.summary: This inscription records a generous endowment by Marcus Gellius Servandus Senior, an Augustalis in Capena, to fund games and benefits for local officials on his birthday, with a dedication in the consulship of Maximus and Orfitus.

EDCS-13900535.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Augusti liberto Philocalo Leonidiano ex testamento arbitratu Iuliae Aureliae et Titi Claudi Leonidiani\n
EDCS-13900535.translation: To Gaius Julius Philocallus Leonidius, freedman of Augustus, by the will and at the arbitration of Iulia Aurelia and Titus Claudius Leonidius.
EDCS-13900535.summary: This is a record of a testamentary disposition, possibly a funerary dedication or endowment, involving freedmen and their patrons.

EDCS-13900577.text_edition: \n Quintus Cossutius Publi filius IIIIvir iure dicundo de sua pecunia pro ludis\n
EDCS-13900577.translation: Quintus Cossutius, son of Publius, chief magistrate with judicial power, from his own money for games.
EDCS-13900577.summary: This inscription records a donation by Quintus Cossutius, a local magistrate, to fund public games.

EDCS-13900582.summary: This is a short dedication to the deified Augusta Iustitia, likely from a public official or beneficiary.

EDCS-14000171.translation: Titus Baebius, son of Titus, from a vision.
EDCS-14000171.summary: This is a very brief inscription indicating a dedication made by Titus Baebius, possibly in fulfillment of a vow seen in a vision.

EDCS-14000187.translation: Sacred to holy Apollo; Eros, procurator of Augustus.
EDCS-14000187.summary: This is a dedication to Apollo made by Eros, an imperial freedman serving as a procurator, likely in an official capacity.

EDCS-14000192.translation: To Venantius, who lived 1 year and 11 months. He sleeps in peace.
EDCS-14000192.summary: This is a Christian funerary epitaph for a child named Venantius, indicating the date of death and the concept of 'sleeping in peace'.

EDCS-14000194.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Callinoeni, who lived for 16 years. Successa, her mother.
EDCS-14000194.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Callinoeni, recording her age and dedicating her memory by her mother, Successa.

EDCS-14000195.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For two twin girls, Primaria and Secundaria, who lived for five years and seven months. Their mother made this.
EDCS-14000195.summary: A funerary epitaph for twin sisters Primaria and Secundaria, recording their ages at death and commissioned by their mother.

EDCS-15200067.text_edition: \n Antoniae Marci filiae Valentinae coniugi castissimae et piissimae Marcus Ulpius Augusti libertus Mithres tabularius hereditatium\n
EDCS-15200067.translation: To Antonia Valentina, daughter of Marcus, most chaste and dutiful wife, Marcus Ulpius Mithres, freedman of Augustus, administrator of inheritances, erected this.
EDCS-15200067.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Ulpius Mithres, an administrator, to his wife Antonia Valentina.

EDCS-15300305.translation: To Hilarius, son of Quintus Fuficius.
EDCS-15300305.summary: A very brief epitaph, likely funerary, identifying Hilarius, son of Quintus Fuficius.

EDCS-15300306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ianuario qui vixit annos LXX cultores Culianienses posuerunt\n
EDCS-15300306.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Ianuarius, who lived 70 years. The cultores of Culium placed this.
EDCS-15300306.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ianuarius, recording his age and dedication by a group identified as cultores Culianenses.

EDCS-15400773.text_edition: \n Manius Maecius Cai filius Sabatina Varus eques salve et tu veive vale hic fuit ille bonus multa multa probitate quoius neque mors satis laudari neque vita potest Maecia Mani filia\n
EDCS-15400773.translation: Manius Maecius Varus, son of Caius, of the Sabatina tribe, an equestrian, greetings, and may you too farewell. Here was that good man, of great integrity, whose death can no more be sufficiently praised than his life. Maecia, daughter of Manius.
EDCS-15400773.summary: A tombstone inscription from Maecia praising her deceased husband Manius Maecius Varus for his integrity, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-15800313.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Divi filio Augusto pontifici maximo Quintus Lucretius Eros Murdianus Lucius Volumnius Eros magistri Augustales\n
EDCS-15800313.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the Divine One, Pontifex Maximus. Quintus Lucretius Eros Murdianus and Lucius Volumnius Eros, masters of the Augustales, [dedicated this].
EDCS-15800313.summary: An inscription recording a dedication by two magistri Augustales, Quintus Lucretius Eros Murdianus and Lucius Volumnius Eros, to Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-15900046.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Umbriciae Pyramidi coniugi bene merenti heres vivus posuit\n
EDCS-15900046.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Umbricia Pyramis, a deserving wife. Her heir, while still alive, placed this.
EDCS-15900046.summary: A funerary epitaph for Umbricia Pyramis, dedicated by her heir.

EDCS-15900047.text_edition: \n Caius Aquillius Teophilus Lucius Aetrius Surus Caius A magistri Mercuriales dono dant de pecunia\n
EDCS-15900047.translation: Caius Aquillius Teophilus, Lucius Aetrius Surus, and Caius A[---], the Mercuriales masters, give this as a gift from their funds.
EDCS-15900047.summary: An inscription recording a dedication of funds by three individuals identified as magistri Mercuriales.

EDCS-16000010.text_edition: \n Lucius Munatius Luci libertus Heraclida Herculi vovit\n
EDCS-16000010.translation: Lucius Munatius Heraclida, freedman of Lucius, vowed [this] to Hercules.
EDCS-16000010.summary: A dedication to Hercules by Lucius Munatius Heraclida, a freedman, possibly a votive offering.

EDCS-16100258.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae Iuliae sanctissimae ex genere Mustiolae sanctae Asiniae Felicissimae quae vixit annis XXXVII Pomponius Felicissimus coniugi incomparabili deposita XIII Kalendas Ianuarias die Solis\n
EDCS-16100258.translation: To the good memory of Julia, most holy, from the family of holy Mustiola, Asinia Felicissima, who lived 37 years, to her incomparable wife, Pomponius Felicissimus, placed on the 13th day before the Kalends of January, the day of the Sun.
EDCS-16100258.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Julia, set up by her husband Pomponius Felicissimus, recording her death date.

EDCS-16100420.text_edition: \n Vivus Quintus Geganius Luci filius Scaptia haruspex sexvir decreto decurionum gratis sibi et suis et Vibiae Cai filiae Tertullae uxori et @(obitae) Vettiae Auli filiae matri in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes XII\n
EDCS-16100420.translation: Quintus Geganius, son of Lucius, a Scaptian haruspex, while alive, by decree of the decurions, free of charge for himself and his family, and for Vettia, daughter of Aulus, his mother, and for Tertulla, daughter of Cai, his wife, in front 12 feet, in the field 12 feet.
EDCS-16100420.summary: This is a funerary boundary inscription by Quintus Geganius, a haruspex, for himself, his wife, and his mother.

EDCS-16200651.text_edition: \n Cultorum Larum ossa Terenti Lascivi in fronte pedes CI in agro pedes LVII\n
EDCS-16200651.translation: The remains of Terentius Lascivus, worshippers of the Lares, in front 101 feet, in the field 57 feet.
EDCS-16200651.summary: This inscription marks the burial plot of Terentius Lascivus, identified as a worshipper of the Lares.

EDCS-16201534.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Luci libertus Mocapor piscator salve\n
EDCS-16201534.translation: Greetings, Lucius Valerius Mocapor, freedman of Lucius, the fisherman.
EDCS-16201534.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucius Valerius Mocapor, a fisherman and freedman.

EDCS-16201535.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Caesaris Nervae Traiani Augusti Germanici Dacici sub cura Hebri Augusti liberti procuratoris Tuendus servus fecit V\n
EDCS-16201535.translation: Tuendus, a slave, made this under the supervision of Hebrus, procurator, freedman of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus.
EDCS-16201535.summary: This is a dedication by a slave named Tuendus, supervised by a procurator, likely for a public work or building.

EDCS-16201608.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Iuni Saturni Marci Iuni Potiti filii et Valeriae Canaviae Rutaeni ex Aquitanica sic vixit nnos XIX diebus XXX\n
EDCS-16201608.translation: To the spirits of the underworld of Marcus Junius Saturnus, son of Marcus Junius Potitus, and of Valeria Canavia, from Rutaeni, from Aquitanica; he lived 19 years and 30 days.
EDCS-16201608.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Junius Saturnus, son of Marcus Junius Potitus, and Valeria Canavia, recording his age at death.

EDCS-16300050.text_edition: \n Publius Clodius Venerandus fecit Publius Clodius Venerandus fecit Publius Clodius Venerandus fecit\n
EDCS-16300050.translation: Publius Clodius Venerandus made this. Publius Clodius Venerandus made this. Publius Clodius Venerandus made this.
EDCS-16300050.summary: This is a repetitive dedication by Publius Clodius Venerandus, possibly for a building or monument.

EDCS-16600332.text_edition: \n Aulus Sergius Polybius Augustalis donum dedit\n
EDCS-16600332.translation: Aulus Sergius Polybius Augustalis gave this gift.
EDCS-16600332.summary: This is a dedication of a gift by Aulus Sergius Polybius Augustalis.

EDCS-16700070.text_edition: \n Aulus Vennonius Felicissimus decurialis decuriae IX acceptis ex arca IIIDsestertiis nummis reliquum sua pecunia fecit ob cuius dedicationem universo numero singulis dedit IIIsestertios binos nummos\n
EDCS-16700070.translation: Aulus Vennonius Felicissimus, decurialis, made the ninth decuria with 2000 sesterces received from the treasury, and completed the rest with his own money, for the dedication of which he gave to each member of the whole group two sesterces.
EDCS-16700070.summary: This inscription records a financial contribution and distribution by Aulus Vennonius Felicissimus, a decurialis, for his decuria.

EDCS-17300630.translation: To the Holy God Mars / Quintus Fabius Eutychianus, patron and quinquennial for life, set this up at his own expense.
EDCS-17300630.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mars by Quintus Fabius Eutychianus, who held patron and perpetual quinquennial positions.

EDCS-17800456.translation: Quintus Sanquinius, son of Quintus, of the Stellatina tribe, quaestor, tribune of the plebs, praetor, proconsul.
EDCS-17800456.summary: This is a funerary epitaph or an inscription on a monument listing the offices held by Quintus Sanquinius, a Roman official.

EDCS-18600411.text_edition: \n Imperator Titus Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XIIII pater patriae consul VII designatus VIII censor fecit\n
EDCS-18600411.translation: Emperor Titus Caesar Vespasian Augustus, high priest, in his ninth year of tribunician power, supreme commander for the fourteenth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the seventh time, designated for the eighth, censor, built this.
EDCS-18600411.summary: This inscription records a public work commissioned by Emperor Vespasian, likely dating to between 79 and 81 AD based on his consular and tribunician powers.

EDCS-19700216.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Fadius Cornutus, of Titus Messianus, prefect of the 5th Cohort Equitata Hispana, military tribune of the 5th Legion Macedonica; his heirs, by the will of Lucius Fadius Cornutus, his father.
EDCS-19700216.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Fadius Cornutus, detailing his military career and established by his heirs, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-20400539.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Alexander, administrator for Faustina Augusta, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-20400539.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus by Alexander, an administrator for Faustina Augusta, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20401518.text_edition: \n ------] optimo et indulgentissimo principi quod aquam usibus iet salubritati publicae necessariam per longum spatium structis operibus impensa fisci sui duxit Claudiani \n
EDCS-20401518.translation: ------ to the best and most indulgent princeps, who led water necessary for uses and public health, over a long distance through structures with the expense of his treasury, Claudianus------
EDCS-20401518.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription praising an emperor (likely Hadrian or Antoninus Pius) for his public works, specifically the construction of an aqueduct.

EDCS-20401519.translation: Burial of Pisinna, who lived for two days and [...]
EDCS-20401519.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription recording the very short life of an individual named Pisinna.

EDCS-20402765.translation: To Bellona. Stephanus, servant of Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20402765.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Bellona, dedicated by Stephanus, a servant of Emperor Vespasian, likely from the Flavian period (69-96 AD).

EDCS-20402766.translation: Titus Aebutius, son of Caius, willingly fulfilled his vow to Fortuna.
EDCS-20402766.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Fortuna, dedicated by Titus Aebutius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20402767.translation: Lucius Suetius Amphio, freedman of Lucius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Fortuna.
EDCS-20402767.summary: This is a votive inscription to Fortuna, dedicated by Lucius Suetius Amphio, a freedman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20402768.translation: Marcus Vinisidius Epaphra, freedman of Marcus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Spring.
EDCS-20402768.summary: This is a votive inscription to a spring deity, dedicated by Marcus Vinisidius Epaphra, a freedman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20402769.translation: Our workshop made this. Philocalus, slave, dedicated it to Hercules the Helper for the health of Quintus Pomponius Romanus.
EDCS-20402769.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules the Helper, set up by a slave for his patron's health, likely from a workshop.

EDCS-20402771.translation: Lucius Titinius Memno, freedman of Lucius and Quintus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Hercules.
EDCS-20402771.summary: A freedman named Memno dedicates a votive offering to Hercules, expressing gratitude for a favor received.

EDCS-20402772.translation: Marcus Firmidius Spectatus, a frumentarius of Legion II Italica Pia Fidelis and an optio, dedicated and consecrated this to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, and [for that of Geta] and Julia Augusta, Mother of the Camps, under the supervision of Flavius Mucianus, a centurion of the frumentarii. He also dedicated it on the 11th of April, when Severus and Victorinus were consuls.
EDCS-20402772.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a military officer (frumentarius) for the health of the emperors and empress, dated to April 11th under the consulship of Severus and Victorinus.

EDCS-20402773.translation: Statius Mettius Zethus willingly gave this gift to Jupiter Sabazius. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20402773.summary: A dedication of a gift to Jupiter Sabazius by Statius Mettius Zethus, with the location provided by local council decree.

EDCS-20402774.translation: Cleanthus, freedman of Prixus Helle, gives this gift to our Just Juno for her health, having undertaken a vow. They also give gifts to the Lares.
EDCS-20402774.summary: A dedication to Juno Iusta by a freedman for her health, also including offerings to the Lares.

EDCS-20402775.translation: Marcus Hortorius Philodamus, freedman of Marcus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Luna.
EDCS-20402775.summary: A freedman named Philodamus dedicates a votive offering to Luna, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-20402776.translation: Caius Lepidius Secundus, son of Caius, of the Palatina tribe, prefect of the engineers, prefect of a cohort, military tribune, promagister of the XX (legion?), augur of inheritances, gave this gift to Luna.
EDCS-20402776.summary: This inscription details the career of Caius Lepidius Secundus and records his dedication of a gift to Luna.

EDCS-20402777.translation: Felix, the vilicus, consecrated this to Mens Bona.
EDCS-20402777.summary: A dedication to Mens Bona (Good Mind) by a vilicus (estate manager) named Felix.

EDCS-20402778.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording a vow fulfilled by someone named Cornelianus (?).

EDCS-20402781.translation: Lucius Titinius Glaucus Lucretianus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, priest of Rome and of the Augusti, IIvir, patron of the colony, sevir of the Roman knights, curio, prefect of the engineers, military tribune of Legion XXII Primigenia, prefect acting as legate of the Balearic Islands, military tribune of Legion VI Victrix, fulfilled his vow for the health of Emperor Nero, which he had vowed for the Balearic Islands in the year when Aulus Licinius Nerva was consul, and when Lucius Saufeius Vegetus and Quintus Aburius Nepos were IIvirs, and placed it where he wished. He also placed gifts for Jupiter, Juno, Minerva, Felicitas, Rome, and the deified Augustus. Emperor Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power 13 times, imperator 11 times, consul 4 times. Lucius Titinius Glaucus Lucretianus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, priest of Rome and of the Augusti, IIvir, patron of the colony, sevir of the Roman knights, curio, prefect of the engineers, military tribune of Legion XXII Primigenia, prefect acting as legate of the Balearic Islands, military tribune of Legion VI Victrix, fulfilled his vow for the health of Emperor Nero, which he had vowed for the Balearic Islands in the year when Aulus Licinius Nerva was consul, and when Lucius Saufeius Vegetus and Quintus Aburius Nepos were IIvirs, and placed it where he wished. He placed gifts for Jupiter, Juno, Minerva, Felicitas, Rome, and the deified Augustus.
EDCS-20402781.summary: This is a lengthy and complex inscription by Lucius Titinius Glaucus Lucretianus, detailing his extensive career and his fulfillment of vows made for the health of Emperor Nero and for the Balearic Islands, including dedications to various deities and the deified Augustus, dated to the consulship of Aulus Licinius Nerva and during the rule of Nero.

EDCS-20402786.translation: To Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, wife of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.
EDCS-20402786.summary: This inscription honors Fulvia Plautilla, wife of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely a dedication on a monument.

EDCS-20402791.translation: To Marcus Turtellius Rufus, son of Caius, twice duovir and twice military tribune, from the colonists and inhabitants.
EDCS-20402791.summary: This is a dedication from the colonists and inhabitants to Marcus Turtellius Rufus, who held significant local magistracies.

EDCS-20402793.translation: Gaius Calvius, son of Caius, and Titus Lurius, son of Titus, serving as duovirs.
EDCS-20402793.summary: This inscription records Gaius Calvius and Titus Lurius serving as duovirs, likely on a public building or monument.

EDCS-20402794.translation: To the departed spirits of Fabia Fortunata, who lived 8 years and 2 days.
EDCS-20402794.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Fabia Fortunata, recording her age at death.

EDCS-20402795.translation: To Marcus Minatius Sabellus, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe, serving as duovir for the second time.
EDCS-20402795.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Minatius Sabellus, a local official who served as duovir twice.

EDCS-20402796.translation: To Aulus Octavius Eronius the elder, from the colonists and lodgers.
EDCS-20402796.summary: This dedication is from the colonists and lodgers to Aulus Octavius Eronius the elder.

EDCS-20402797.translation: To Lucius Titinius Petrinianus, son of Lucius, serving as duovir for the second time, from the colonists and inhabitants.
EDCS-20402797.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the colonists and inhabitants to Lucius Titinius Petrinianus, who held the office of duovir twice.

EDCS-20402803.translation: Marcus Aulius Eros, freedman of Marcus, gave the steps and repaired the wall in his magistracy and dedicated it.
EDCS-20402803.summary: This inscription records a public works project by Marcus Aulius Eros, a freedman, who funded and dedicated repairs to steps and a wall.

EDCS-20402804.translation: Lucius Titius Philargurus, freedman of Lucius, gives the base.
EDCS-20402804.summary: This inscription records a donation of a base by Lucius Titius Philargurus, a freedman.

EDCS-20402805.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Augustus for the third time and Gallienus Augustus for the second time, on the [3rd?] of February. In the assembly, when the numerous fullers were present, and there, with the registrars [3?] Mirus and Flavius Festus the younger serving as magistrates, a resolution was made that it be forever within the protection of our guild if we co-opt patrons endowed with illustrious honors and full of good character and gentleness. Therefore, since Lucius Cottius Proculus is a splendid man, rich in the prestige of our splendid city of Luna, a man of simple life, by which we believe our number would be greatly increased if we co-opted him as our patron, what do you think should be done about it? It was decreed that it should please all and everyone to consent to such a sound report of our magistrates, especially since he is both enriched by dignity and filled with the honor of office, by which our number can sufficiently and abundantly rejoice if we take him as our patron and ask of his kindness and his goodwill, that he deign to receive this votive decree of our consent with that spirit, by which we offer it gloriously and joyfully, and that he command this bronze tablet of our decree, written down, to be affixed where he shall order, to be a witness in the age of our consent. It was decreed.
EDCS-20402805.summary: This inscription is a decree from the guild of fullers in Luna, dated to 257 CE, electing Lucius Cottius Proculus as their patron in recognition of his status and character, and ordering the decree to be inscribed on bronze.

EDCS-20402807.translation: In the consulship of Decimus Haterius Agrippa and Gaius Sulpicius Galba, Hilarus, the overseer and magistrate, erected this. In the consulship of Sisenna and Statilius Lucius Scribonius, Hilarus [son of] Vaccius Scaripus Nervius died. In the consulship of Lucius Pontius Flaccus and Gaius Caecilius, Tiburtinus Philo died. In the consulship of Tiberius Caesar Germanicus (second consulship), Tigranus Felicio [and] Hermippus Primus died. In the consulship of Marcus Silio and Lucius Norbanus, Gabinus Optatus [and] Apollonius Calo died. Tela[...
EDCS-20402807.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of consuls and notable deaths, possibly part of a public record or a funerary monument.

EDCS-20402808.translation: Thalamus made this. Thalamus made this. Thalamus made this.
EDCS-20402808.summary: A very brief inscription indicating that Thalamus made this object or structure, repeated three times.

EDCS-20402812.translation: To the departed spirits. Abascantus, the Emperor's horrearius (storeroom keeper), made this for his most devoted wife Claudia Benedicta and for himself and [for others...]
EDCS-20402812.summary: A funerary dedication by Abascantus for his wife Claudia Benedicta, mentioning his own and possibly other's future use of the tomb.

EDCS-20402813.translation: To the departed spirits. Fortunatus, from among the well-deserving stewards, made this for Ulpia Florentina, his incomparable wife, with whom he lived for 30 years and [some] months without any fault.
EDCS-20402813.summary: A funerary dedication by Fortunatus for his wife Ulpia Florentina, praising her character and stating their years of marriage.

EDCS-20402814.translation: To the departed spirits. Annianus, son of Marcus Flavianus, from Syria Palaestina, for his dearest wife Antonia Ag[...], who lived [for X years...], left children and grandchildren.
EDCS-20402814.summary: A funerary dedication by Annianus for his wife Antonia, mentioning her origin and that she left children and grandchildren.

EDCS-20402815.translation: [...]via, freedwoman of Lucius, with the approval of Atticus, the freedman.
EDCS-20402815.summary: This fragmentary inscription records an action taken by the freedwoman Ammia, likely concerning property or a legal matter, under the supervision of Atticus.

EDCS-20402816.translation: To Appuleius, son of Sextus, grandson of Sextus, great-grandson of Sextus, [and] Fabia Numantina, the last of his family.
EDCS-20402816.summary: A dedication or funerary monument honoring Appuleius and Fabia Numantina, emphasizing the lineage and designating Appuleius as the last of his family.

EDCS-20402817.translation: To Atilia Secunda, daughter of Gaius, [from] her maternal grandmother.
EDCS-20402817.summary: A dedication or funerary inscription from a maternal grandmother to her granddaughter, Atilia Secunda.

EDCS-20402818.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that seems to record a vow to Jupiter made by someone named Lucius.

EDCS-20402820.translation: ] Gaius Aufidius, son of Lucius, [dedicated this gift] to Hercules [...]
EDCS-20402820.summary: A dedication of a gift to Hercules by Gaius Aufidius, son of Lucius.

EDCS-20402821.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudia Restuta to her most excellent husband, Tiberius Claudius Callistus, with whom she lived for 28 years.
EDCS-20402821.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband.

EDCS-20402822.translation: To Lucius Cosseius. His parents, Cosseius and Melissa, [set this up] for a son of incredible devotion, and Lucius Poppaienus Philumenus for his dearest brother.
EDCS-20402822.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by parents for their son and by a brother for his sibling.

EDCS-20402823.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tedia Salbilla [set this up] for her most devoted daughter, Eppia Demetrias, who deserved well.
EDCS-20402823.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her devoted daughter.

EDCS-20402824.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Syntropus for his [---] Litorius Rufus, who deserved well.
EDCS-20402824.summary: A funerary epitaph with a partially lost recipient and dedicant.

EDCS-20402825.translation: Eros set this up for his beloved wife Piecasa.
EDCS-20402825.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife.

EDCS-20402826.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [---]grammus, [freedman] of Emperor Augustus, and his dearest wife Nonia Euhodia, [set this up]; and their daughter Nonia Tyche for her most devoted father.
EDCS-20402826.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a freedman of the emperor and his wife for each other, and by their daughter for her father.

EDCS-20402828.translation: Lucius Helvius Potinianus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe.
EDCS-20402828.summary: An epitaph recording a man's name, filiation, and tribal affiliation, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-20402830.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Curtius Propinquus for his wife Iulia Tethys.
EDCS-20402830.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife.

EDCS-20402831.translation: [Munatia Prima, freedwoman of Gaius] Dama, [set this up] [---] 10 feet in the field, 10 feet in the front.
EDCS-20402831.summary: A boundary inscription marking a plot of land, with the owner's name and the dimensions of the plot.

EDCS-20402832.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Nunnus Mahes and Nunnia Successa, parents, [made this] for their most devoted son, Quintus Nunnio Felicioni. May the earth lie lightly on him.
EDCS-20402832.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by parents for their devoted son.

EDCS-20402837.translation: Lucius Terentius Pelorus made this for himself and for his mother, Tettia Erotis, and for his freedman, Terentius [?].
EDCS-20402837.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Terentius Pelorus for himself and his mother and freedman.

EDCS-20402838.translation: To the departed spirits. Tettia Clara lived for 24 years. Tettius Secundus made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-20402838.summary: A funerary dedication by Tettius Secundus for his wife, Tettia Clara, who died at age 24.

EDCS-20402839.translation: Phileros and Pharnacas set this up for their mother, Titia Successa.
EDCS-20402839.summary: A funerary dedication set up by Phileros and Pharnacas for their mother, Titia Successa.

EDCS-20402841.translation: Titus(?) Valerius Si[---] with his sons [---]cus for [his] deserving [relative].
EDCS-20402841.summary: A funerary dedication, with significant lacunae, for a deserving relative, possibly by Titus Valerius Si--- and his sons.

EDCS-20402842.translation: To the departed spirits. For Vettia Aphrodisia, his most sweet wife, deserving of all good, Sextus Annaeus Liberalis erected this. She lived 20 years, 11 months, and 20 days.
EDCS-20402842.summary: A funerary dedication by Sextus Annaeus Liberalis for his wife, Vettia Aphrodisia, who died at age 20.

EDCS-20402843.translation: To the departed spirits. Boielius Fortis made this for his deserving wife, Vibia Priscilla.
EDCS-20402843.summary: A funerary dedication by Boielius Fortis for his wife, Vibia Priscilla.

EDCS-20402844.translation: Hail, Gaius Umbrius, son of Titus.
EDCS-20402844.summary: A very brief funerary or commemorative epitaph for Gaius Umbrius.

EDCS-20402881.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-20402881.translation: In memory of Felicis. If anyone wishes to place another's body here, they will pay fifty solidi to the treasury.
EDCS-20402881.summary: A funerary monument inscription that sets a penalty for unauthorized burials, likely from the later Roman period.

EDCS-20402883.translation: Sulpicia Saturnina, freedwoman of Caius, dedicated this gift to Bona Dea.
EDCS-20402883.summary: A religious dedication by Sulpicia Saturnina to the goddess Bona Dea.

EDCS-20402886.translation: Sacred to [---]. Salvius [---], Philotimus [---], Hilarus [---], [---]nus, [---] Merc(uriales).
EDCS-20402886.summary: A dedication to Mercury or related deities, with significant lacunae, mentioning several individuals and a group identified as 'Merc(uriales)'.

EDCS-20402888.translation: Eros, freedman of Marcus, [built] the small shrine of the Pisana aqueducts.
EDCS-20402888.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a small shrine by Eros, a freedman of Marcus, likely related to the Pisana aqueducts.

EDCS-20402889.translation: Corinna, freely and deservedly, pays her vow.
EDCS-20402889.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Corinna to an unnamed deity, dated by the context of other inscriptions to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20402891.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-20402891.translation: Having assisted in the recording: Quintus Sertorius Atilius Tacitus, son of Quintus; Publius Rasinius Bassus, son of Lucius; Lucius Lappius Gallus, son of Publius; Quintus Sertorius Alpius Pica, son of Quintus; Gaius Vettius Virgula, son of Lucius; Marcus Herius Priscus, son of Marcus; Aulus Albius Gutta, son of Aulus; Tiberius Petronius Pollio, son of Tiberius; Lucius Fabius Bassus, son of Lucius; Sextus Aponius Creticus, son of Sextus; Gaius Canius Saturninus, son of Gaius; Lucius Otacilius Panthera, son of Quintus. Because, when magistrates were not being elected in our colony due to disputes among candidates, the following matters were transacted, which are written below: when on April 2nd a message arrived that Gaius Caesar, son of the divine Augustus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, guardian of the entire Roman empire and ruler of the whole world, grandson of a god, after successfully completing the consulship which he had carried on beyond the furthest borders of the Roman people, waging war, had been taken from the Roman people by a cruel fate due to wounds received in the service of the state, while governing the republic well and having conquered or received into allegiance the most warlike and greatest peoples, and this ruler, who was most just and most like his father in virtue, was already designated for our colony, its unique protection, and this event had not yet ended the grief which, after the death of his brother Lucius Caesar, the designated consul and augur, our patron, prince of youth, had caused the whole colony to undertake, it renewed and multiplied the sorrow of all, individuals and the community. Therefore, because in this situation there were neither aediles nor prefects in the colony, nor anyone presiding in legal matters, all the decurions and colonists agreed that, because of the greatness of such a sudden calamity, from the day his death was announced until the day his bones were brought back and interred and the rites for his Manes were completed, all should abstain from feasting, changing their clothes, and all temples of the immortal gods, public baths, and shops should be closed, and the matrons in our colony should mourn. And that day, the day Gaius Caesar died, which is February 21st, should be recorded with grief and mourning in the presence of all, and it should be decreed that no public sacrifice or supplication, no betrothal or public banquet, shall take place or be arranged or announced on or after that day, which is February 21st, nor shall any theatrical games or chariot races be held or witnessed on that day. Furthermore, that every year on that day, the day of his death, his Manes shall be honored publicly by the magistrates or those who preside in legal matters in Pisa, in the same place and in the same manner as it was established to honor Lucius Caesar. Furthermore, that a gate should be established in the most frequented place of our colony, adorned with spoils of the peoples conquered or received into allegiance by him, with a pedestrian statue of himself in triumphal attire above it, and around it two gilded equestrian statues of Gaius and Lucius Caesar should be placed. Furthermore, as soon as we are able to elect and have decemvirs according to the law of the colony, the first decemvirs to be elected shall refer this matter, which was agreed upon by the decurions and all the colonists, to the decurions, and with their public authority, it shall be legally decreed and their authorization shall be entered into the public records. In the meantime, Titus Statulenus Iuncus, flamen Augustalis, minor pontifex of the public sacred rites of the Roman people, should be asked to indicate this public duty and the will of all, by submitting a petition to the Emperor Caesar Augustus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 26th time. And that Titus Statulenus Iuncus, chief of our colony, flamen Augustalis, minor pontifex of the public sacred rites of the Roman people, having submitted his petition as written above to the Emperor Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 26th time, father of the fatherland, it should be decreed that all that was done, enacted, and established on April 2nd, in the consulship of Sextus Aelius Catus and Gaius Sentius Saturninus, shall be done, enacted, held, and observed in this way forever, by Lucius Titius, son of Aulus, and Titus Allius Rufus, son of Titus, the duovirs, and by whomever shall be duovirs, prefects, or any other magistrates in our colony thereafter. And that Lucius Titius, son of Aulus, and Titus Allius Rufus, son of Titus, the duovirs, shall ensure that all that is written above, by our decree, is entered into the public records by the public scribe as soon as possible, in the presence of the quaestors. They decreed.
EDCS-20402891.summary: This lengthy inscription from Pisa records a senatorial decree passed in 4 AD establishing public mourning and perpetual honors for Gaius Caesar Germanicus, detailing sacrifices, games, statues, and public processions in his memory following his death, and confirming these measures for future magistrates. It was enacted during the consulship of Sextus Aelius Catus and Gaius Sentius Saturninus.

EDCS-20402904.translation: Of the Luciorum, of Montanus and Apronianus.
EDCS-20402904.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly referring to a property or dedication belonging to members of the Lucius family named Montanus and Apronianus.

EDCS-20402907.translation: To the Shades Below. Venuleia Pelagia rests here, the son [dedicated this] to his most dutiful mother. Marcus Naevius Restitutus, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe, a soldier of the 10th praetorian cohort, rests here. He left in his will 4000 sesterces to the college of Pisan shipbuilders, the oldest and most dutiful station, from whose income memorial rites and flower offerings should be celebrated annually at his tomb. If this is not done by them, then, under the same condition, the college of carpenters, upon receiving 4000 sesterces from the shipbuilders, will themselves have to celebrate them.
EDCS-20402907.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Venuleia Pelagia and Marcus Naevius Restitutus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, who left a bequest to the Pisan shipbuilders' college for the upkeep of his tomb.

EDCS-20402912.translation: While alive, they made [this] for Quintus Anquirinnus Severus, son of Quintus, of the Galeria tribe, a decurion elected by vote, aged 15 years. For themselves and their descendants.
EDCS-20402912.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Quintus Anquirinnus Severus and his wife Occia Agile for themselves and their descendants, and specifically mentions Severus's status as a young decurion.

EDCS-20402914.translation: Lucius Lollius Commodus, freedman of Lucius, made [this] while alive for himself and [---] Asinius [---] Augustalis [---] Volusenus [---] for Aebutia Sabina [---]ebia Am[---]. In the field [---] paces, in the front [---] paces.
EDCS-20402914.summary: This is a funerary inscription indicating that Lucius Lollius Commodus, a freedman, erected this monument for himself and other individuals, including Asinius Augustalis and Aebutia Sabina, and provides measurements for a plot of land.

EDCS-20402916.translation: To the Shades Below. Mettia Ianuaria rests here. For her well-deserving husband, Quintus Obsequientius Severinus, Augustalis and curator of the calendar of Florentia, for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-20402916.summary: This inscription records the tomb of Mettia Ianuaria and her husband Quintus Obsequientius Severinus, who held the prestigious position of Augustalis and curator of the calendar in Pisa, intended for their family.

EDCS-20402918.translation: For Sextus Octavius Felix, perpetual Augustalis.
EDCS-20402918.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sextus Octavius Felix, honoring his perpetual position as Augustalis.

EDCS-20402919.translation: [---] for Gaius Scandilius Civi[---], decurion of Pisa. Gaius Scandilius Philetus [his father] and Scandilia Prima [his mother].
EDCS-20402919.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Gaius Scandilius Civi, a decurion of Pisa, and identifies his parents, Gaius Scandilius Philetus and Scandilia Prima.

EDCS-20402920.translation: To the shades below. To Annia Iucunda, mother of Marcus Annius Proculus. To the shades below. To Marcus Annius Proculus, son of Marcus, of the Palatine tribe, decurion of the colony of Ostia, flamen of the deified Vespasian, patron of the shipwrights of Ostia. He lived 25 years, 6 months, 28 days, 4 hours.
EDCS-20402920.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Marcus Annius Proculus, a prominent citizen of Ostia, who died at a young age, with a dedication to his mother.

EDCS-20402923.translation: Lucius Apisius Pollio, son of Lucius, a garland-maker, is buried here.
EDCS-20402923.summary: A simple burial marker for Lucius Apisius Pollio, identified by his profession as a garland-maker.

EDCS-20402924.translation: To the shades below. Publius Aelius Diogenes made this for himself and his wife Attia Ionica and his daughter Attia Moschis, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants. This monument will not be followed by an heir.
EDCS-20402924.summary: A tomb inscription set up by Publius Aelius Diogenes for himself, his family, and descendants, stipulating that the property is entailed.

EDCS-20402945.text_edition: \n Publius Ferrarius Hermes Caeciniae Dignae coniugi karissimae Numeriae Maximillae coniugi bene merenti et Publio Ferrario Proculo filio et posterisque suis\n
EDCS-20402945.translation: To Publius Ferrarius Hermes, for his most beloved wife Caecinia Dignae, for his deserving wife Numeria Maximilla, and for his son Publius Ferrarius Proculus and their descendants.
EDCS-20402945.summary: A funerary dedication by Publius Ferrarius Hermes for his wives and son.

EDCS-20402953.translation: To the shades below. To Titus Murtio Glycon. His wife Murtia Adiecta and his daughter Murtia, daughter of Glycon, made this for him, a deserving man.
EDCS-20402953.summary: A tomb inscription for Titus Murtio Glycon, set up by his wife and daughter.
