EDCS-20402955.translation: To Gnaeus Octavius Lupercus, son of Gnaeus. Gnaeus Octavius Epituncanus, his father, made this while alive for himself and his family, and for his wife Anneia Quarta, daughter of Quintus. In front 10 feet, in depth 10 feet.
EDCS-20402955.summary: A family tomb inscription, set up by a father for himself, his family, and his wife, specifying the dimensions of the burial plot.

EDCS-20402956.translation: To the shades below. Tertia Agathonis, freedwoman, made this for her most devoted son Lucius Ovinus Amandus.
EDCS-20402956.summary: A tomb inscription for Lucius Ovinus Amandus, set up by his freedwoman mother.

EDCS-20402960.translation: To the shades below. Publius Cornelius Felix to his sweetest wife Pompeia Agathonicene, who lived 24 years, 4 months, 13 days.
EDCS-20402960.summary: A tomb inscription for Pompeia Agathonicene, who died young, dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-20402961.translation: To the shades below. To Pompeia Primitiva, who lived 28 years, 4 months, 12 days. Titus Pompeius Primitivos and Quintus Maecius Sedatus made this for their most beloved and chaste wife.
EDCS-20402961.summary: A tomb inscription for Pompeia Primitiva, set up by her father and her husband.

EDCS-20402963.translation: To the shades below. For Rafidia Chyrsis, freedwoman of Publius. Made for himself. In front 7.5 feet, in depth 4 feet.
EDCS-20402963.summary: A tomb inscription for Rafidia Chyrsis, a freedwoman, with dimensions for the burial plot.

EDCS-20402971.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Veianius Helittas made this for himself, while alive, and for his wife Claudia Euploeae. Whoever, after our death, wishes to place a body above or disturb the sarcophagus, shall pay 25,000 sesterces to the treasury of the Roman people.
EDCS-20402971.summary: A funerary碑 (gravestone) erected by Gaius Veianius Helittas for himself and his wife, with a penalty for desecration of the tomb.

EDCS-20402987.translation: To Silvana, who sleeps here, well-deserving in peace. She lived 21 years, 3 months, 4 hours, 6 scruples. Buried on the 9th day before the Kalends of July. [She rests] in [peace?].
EDCS-20402987.summary: A tombstone for Silvana, recording her lifespan and burial date, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20402990.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius C[---] Pisanus, with his sons, made this for his well-deserving wife Fabia Procla with his own money.
EDCS-20402990.summary: A funerary inscription from a husband and sons for a wife and mother, Fabia Procla.

EDCS-20403008.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Attilius Candidus.
EDCS-20403008.summary: A simple dedication to the departed spirits for Publius Attilius Candidus.

EDCS-20403012.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Speratus lived 36 years. His dearest wife Aelia Sabina made this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-20403012.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Aelia Sabina to her husband Titus Flavius Speratus.

EDCS-20403019.translation: To Lord Osiris. Gaius Gargennius Maximus, veteran, son of Spurius, of the Scaptia tribe, [erected this] in the name of his brother Marcus Gargennius Macrinus, veteran, son of Spurius, of the Scaptia tribe.
EDCS-20403019.summary: A dedication to the god Osiris by a veteran, Gaius Gargennius Maximus, on behalf of his veteran brother Marcus Gargennius Macrinus.

EDCS-20403038.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Tettius Probus [erected this] for his most cherished wife Vibia Proba, well-deserving, and for his mother Tettia Maxima, most dutiful, well-deserving.
EDCS-20403038.summary: A funerary inscription from Lucius Tettius Probus for his wife Vibia Proba and his mother Tettia Maxima.

EDCS-20403053.translation: To Isis Regina. Gaius Adulnius Natalis, for Valens his son, fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-20403053.summary: A votive inscription from Gaius Adulnius Natalis to the goddess Isis Regina, dedicated for his son Valens.

EDCS-20403054.summary: A fragmentary inscription with very little legible text, likely a dedication or funerary marker.

EDCS-20403055.translation: [---] Sextus Calpurnius Ianvarius fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-20403055.summary: A votive inscription from Sextus Calpurnius Ianvarius, recording the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-20403056.translation: Quintus D. Trop[himus?] willingly paid his vow for Quintus D. Secundinus, his son.
EDCS-20403056.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, recording a father's vow fulfilled for his son.

EDCS-20403057.translation: Lucius Otacilius Favor willingly paid his vow for Severus, his freedman colleague.
EDCS-20403057.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, where a patron fulfills a vow for a freedman.

EDCS-20403058.translation: Publius Otacilius Super willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Isis Regina.
EDCS-20403058.summary: A votive inscription from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, dedicating a payment to the goddess Isis Regina.

EDCS-20403061.translation: Gaius Subulinius Faustinus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Isis Regina for the unlawful matter concerning the people remitted by the greatest emperors Severus and Antoninus Augusti.
EDCS-20403061.summary: A votive inscription from the reigns of Severus and Caracalla (late 2nd or early 3rd century CE) by a citizen thanking the goddess Isis Regina for a legal matter resolved by imperial decree.

EDCS-20403070.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Aurelius Diocletian, the Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, pontiff, Germanic champion, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. Titus Aelius Marcianus, a man of senatorial rank, corrector of Italy, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-20403070.summary: A dedication to Emperor Diocletian (late 3rd century CE) by Titus Aelius Marcianus, a high-ranking official, acknowledging the emperor's power and his own devotion.

EDCS-20403071.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For the memory of Quintus Petronius Melior, a man of senatorial rank.
EDCS-20403071.summary: A funerary dedication to the deceased of senatorial rank, Quintus Petronius Melior, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20403075.translation: Satrius Albanus, son of Aulus, of the Scaptia tribe, from Florentia, a soldier of the 1st Praetorian cohort, century of Martialis. He served for 10 years and lived for 33 years.
EDCS-20403075.summary: A military tombstone from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, recording the service and lifespan of a Praetorian guardsman.

EDCS-20403092.translation: Quintus Vibius Maximus, son of Lucius, of the Scaptia tribe, from Smintium, aerarius (a type of civic official), sexvir, made this while alive for himself and for his most beloved wife Maeminia Maxima, daughter of Quintus; and for his son Quintus Vibius Verus, son of Quintus; and for his brother Lucius. This stone will also be a protection after death, a tomb, and will give evidence that burials have taken place here, if indeed we believe there is anything in the underworld; what is the point of living unless we take care after death? Fame flies away, only the body is burned, and we seek an eternal home and the end of labors. As you read this, learn to provide for yourself too.
EDCS-20403092.summary: A complex funerary epitaph from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, including philosophical reflections on death and the afterlife by a local official for his family.

EDCS-20403096.translation: Versinia Tyche, his freedwoman, made this for Publius Alfius Erastus, a merchant, her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-20403096.summary: A funerary epitaph from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, dedicated by a freedwoman to her deceased husband, a merchant.

EDCS-20403098.translation: For Gaius Allius Rufus, son of Quintus, and Gaius Allius Aptus, son of Gaius. 10 feet in front, 12 feet in the rear.
EDCS-20403098.summary: A funerary boundary marker, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, indicating the dimensions of a burial plot for two individuals.

EDCS-20403115.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Quadratilla, her father Tampius.
EDCS-20403115.summary: A funerary dedication for Quadratilla, set up by her father Tampius, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20403121.translation: Aurelia Secunda made this for herself while alive and for her family.
EDCS-20403121.summary: A funerary monument self-funded by Aurelia Secunda for herself and her relatives, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20403126.translation: Made by a living person. Quintus Crispius Ajax, freedman of Quintus, for himself and for Cispia Storgini, freedwoman of Quintus, and for Quintus Cispius Narcissus, son of Quintus, [son?]. In the front 12 feet, in the field 12 feet.
EDCS-20403126.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Crispius Ajax, his wife Cispia Storgini, and their son Quintus Cispius Narcissus, detailing the dimensions of the plot, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20700400.translation: Sacred to the Spring [God?]. Baebius Nymphodotus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the return [of...], by the divine will of Jupiter the Victorious.
EDCS-20700400.summary: A votive inscription to a water deity, possibly the god Fontus, dedicated by Baebius Nymphodotus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20700403.translation: Hermes Ac..., freedman of Nymphodotus, servant of Epinicus, son of Julius, made [this] for Silvanus Sanctus from our workshop.
EDCS-20700403.summary: A dedication to Silvanus Sanctus, made by Hermes, a freedman of Nymphodotus and slave of Epinicus, for the deity, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20700404.translation: [...] to Victor [?], sacred to Silvanus. Hermes, slave of Baebius Nymphodotus, the overseer, made [this]. Cla[...], freedman of Julius Epinicus, made [this] from our workshop.
EDCS-20700404.summary: A dedication to Silvanus, likely by Hermes, an overseer and slave of Baebius Nymphodotus, and possibly another individual named Epinicus, from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20700406.translation: Publius Servilius Priamus, freedman of Publius, to Silvanus Sanctus.
EDCS-20700406.summary: A dedication to Silvanus Sanctus by Publius Servilius Priamus, a freedman, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20700408.translation: Philodamus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his son.
EDCS-20700408.summary: A votive inscription by Philodamus who fulfilled a vow for his son, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20700411.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus.
EDCS-20700411.summary: An inscription honoring Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely referring to the emperor Claudius, as pontifex maximus, possibly from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-20700412.text_edition: \n Poppaeae Augustae Neronis Caesaris Augusti Germanici Neroni Claudio divi Claudii filio Germanici Caesaris nepoti Tiberi Caesaris Augusti pronepoti divi Augusti abnepoti Caesari Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VIIII imperatori VIIII consuli IIII D N Lucius Titinius Luci filius Galeria Glaucus Lucretianus duovir IIII quinquennalis primus creatus beneficio divi Claudii praefectus Neronis Claudi Caesaris Augusti patronus coloniae sevir equitum Romanorum curio sacrorum faciundorum flamen perpetuus Romae flamen Augusti beneficio Caesaris creatus tribunus militum legionis XXII Primigeniae praefectus insularum Balearum\n
EDCS-20700412.translation: To Poppaea Augusta, wife of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus; to Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, grandson of the deified Claudius, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator for the 9th time, consul for the 4th time. Lucius Titinius Lucius' son Galeria Glaucus Lucretianus, elected duovir for the 4th time and quinquennalis for the first time by the favor of the deified Claudius, prefect by Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus, patron of the colony, sevir of Roman knights, curio for performing sacred rites, perpetual flamen, flamen Augusti created by Caesar's favor, military tribune of the 22nd Legion Primigenia, prefect of the Balearic Islands.
EDCS-20700412.summary: A lengthy inscription detailing the dignities and offices held by Lucius Titinius Glaucus Lucretianus, honoring Emperor Nero and his family, likely from the period of Nero's reign (54-68 CE).

EDCS-20700414.translation: To the Great and Invincible Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the council of the people of Luna, devoted to his divine majesty. Marcus Pescennius, son of Quintus, duovir, was set up by Phryx, his freedman. To Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, the council of the people of Luna, devoted to his divine majesty. To Magnea Urba, Augusta, wife of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, the council and people of Luna, devoted to their divine majesty.
EDCS-20700414.summary: This inscription honors multiple emperors and empresses, including Maxentius and Maximian, and appears to be a dedication by the municipal council of Luna, likely dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-20700416.translation: Lucius Titinius, son of Lucius Petrinianus, duovir, restored and replaced the bronze public insignia and the base which were missing, at his own expense.
EDCS-20700416.summary: This inscription records Lucius Titinius Petrinianus, a duovir, who funded the repair and replacement of public bronze insignia and a base, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20700417.summary: A simple dedication to Titinia, daughter of Lucius, likely a tomb inscription from the Roman period.

EDCS-20700418.translation: Marcus Pescennius Eros, freedman of Marcus, provides the base.
EDCS-20700418.summary: This inscription indicates that Marcus Pescennius Eros, a freedman, provided a base, probably for a statue or dedication, in the Roman period.

EDCS-20700419.translation: Lucius Titius Philomusus, freedman of Lucius, provides the base.
EDCS-20700419.summary: This inscription records that Lucius Titius Philomusus, a freedman, provided a base, likely for a statue or dedication, in the Roman period.

EDCS-20700420.translation: Gnaeus Turellius Salvius, freedman of Gnaeus, provides the base.
EDCS-20700420.summary: This inscription states that Gnaeus Turellius Salvius, a freedman, provided a base, likely for a statue or dedication, in the Roman period.

EDCS-20700421.translation: To Lucius Pontius Strabo, son of the son of Lucius, duovir for the third time, tribune of the soldiers for the third time, prefect of the cavalry and fleet.
EDCS-20700421.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Pontius Strabo, detailing his significant civic and military career, including multiple terms as duovir and tribune, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20700422.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Licinius Super erected this for his well-deserving Marcus Epidius Celer, a sailor in the praetorian fleet at Ravenna, who served for 19 years and lived for 44 years, a Dalmatian by birth.
EDCS-20700422.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Epidius Celer, a Dalmatian sailor in the Ravenna praetorian fleet, erected by Licinius Super, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20700423.translation: To Pietas, [dedicated by her] brother. [She was] a sweet sister, [erected] a memorial. Murrasius Ision, soldier of the 2nd cohort of vigiles, century of Herodes, erected this for his most unhappy sister.
EDCS-20700423.summary: This inscription is a memorial erected by Murrasius Ision, a soldier, for his beloved sister, likely a tomb inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20700428.translation: To Publius Amaedius, son of Publius, of the Arnensian tribe, [as a memorial] from his maternal grandfather.
EDCS-20700428.summary: This inscription is a dedication from a maternal grandfather to his grandson, Publius Amaedius, likely a tomb inscription from the Roman period.

EDCS-20700457.translation: To the departed spirits. To Aninia Tigris, who deserved well. Terentius Ca[...
EDCS-20700457.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Aninia Tigris by Terentius Ca.

EDCS-20700459.translation: Cissus, freedman of Antonius, made this in his lifetime for himself and his family.
EDCS-20700459.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Cissus, a freedman, for himself and his family.

EDCS-20700460.translation: To the departed spirits. To Claudia Sabina, for whom Marcus Vinisidius Restitutus was husband, most beloved.
EDCS-20700460.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Claudia Sabina by her husband, Marcus Vinisidius Restitutus.

EDCS-20700461.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Cosconius Tacitus and Cosconius Nepos to their most beloved mother, Valeria Procula. Philippus placed [this monument] for the one who deserved well, most beloved.
EDCS-20700461.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Valeria Procula by her sons Titus Cosconius Tacitus and Cosconius Nepos, with a further dedication by Philippus.

EDCS-20700464.translation: ... Genialis, his heirs, for the one who deserved well.
EDCS-20700464.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to an individual (possibly Genialis) by his heirs.

EDCS-20700465.translation: To the departed spirits. Syceni Herennia. Chresimus, her husband, and Aiecta, her daughter, and her son-in-law Alexander, erected [this monument] for her. She lived 70 years.
EDCS-20700465.summary: A funerary epitaph for Syceni Herennia, erected by her husband, daughter, and son-in-law, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-20700469.translation: Octavius Romulus, an honest boy. Here lie his buried bones.
EDCS-20700469.summary: An epitaph for Octavius Romulus, a young boy, recording the burial of his remains.

EDCS-20700471.translation: To the departed spirits. To Petronius Donatus, who lived 25 years. Ariniade Ignatia, his mother, and Velutia Procla, his sister, placed [this monument] for the one who deserved well.
EDCS-20700471.summary: A funerary epitaph for Petronius Donatus, who lived 25 years, erected by his mother and sister.

EDCS-20700472.summary: An incomplete inscription that names Gaius Sabinus Isoc[h]rys[us.

EDCS-20700475.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph that begins to record the name and lifespan of an individual.

EDCS-20700476.translation: To Nae and to Marcus, son of [---], son of [---], son of [---], son of [---].
EDCS-20700476.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication to several individuals, possibly family members or local deities, with incomplete details about the dedicants and recipients.

EDCS-20800248.translation: Gaius Pompilius Cerialis, IIvir for judicial matters, quinquennial magistrate, prefect of Drusus Caesar's legion XXI Rapax, first centurion, and Laelia Brocchilla, daughter of Marcus, and Gaius Pompilius Proculus, son of Gaius, of the Sabatina tribe, perpetual pontiff, served with equestrian status and lived for [---] years.
EDCS-20800248.summary: This epitaph honors Gaius Pompilius Cerialis, a military and civic official, along with his wife and son, detailing their public service and lifespan.

EDCS-20800251.translation: Caecina, son of Tlabonius, lived 20 years.
EDCS-20800251.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age of Caecina, son of Tlabonius.

EDCS-20800263.translation: Tiberius Claudius Glyptus willingly gave this gift.
EDCS-20800263.summary: This is a dedication of a gift offered willingly by Tiberius Claudius Glyptus.

EDCS-20800264.translation: [---] to the most excellent patrons; the procurators Flavi Secundinus and Perelianus commissioned [the work] according to their vow, under the care of [---].
EDCS-20800264.summary: This inscription records a commission by two procurators, Flavi Secundinus and Perelianus, for a public work vowed to patrons.

EDCS-20800265.summary: This inscription simply records the name Gaius Gellius Modestus Antonianus, likely an epitaph or a record of ownership.

EDCS-20800267.translation: Lucius Volusenus, haruspex, sacred to the departed spirits, 12 feet in front, 10 feet in depth.
EDCS-20800267.summary: This is a funerary marker for Lucius Volusenus, a haruspex, specifying the dimensions of his plot.

EDCS-20800268.translation: 10 feet in front, 10 feet in depth. Vibia Salvia, freedwoman of Gaius.
EDCS-20800268.summary: This funerary inscription marks the plot dimensions and names Vibia Salvia, a freedwoman, as the deceased.

EDCS-20800270.translation: To the departed spirits. To Quintus Velesinius Sabinus, worshippers of Saturn.
EDCS-20800270.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Quintus Velesinius Sabinus by his worshippers of Saturn.

EDCS-20800271.translation: To the departed spirits. To Cosconia Kapriola, his wife; Cosconius Montanus and his sons and daughters, to her well-deserving.
EDCS-20800271.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Cosconius Montanus and his children to his wife, Cosconia Kapriola.

EDCS-20800274.translation: Festius, the administrator, dedicated this gift to the Lares and the household.
EDCS-20800274.summary: This is a dedication to household deities by an administrator, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800276.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IIII consuli III Lucius Velius Luci filius Clustumina Prudens |(centurio) legionis X fretensis CCCtrecenarius princeps castrorum |(centurio) cohortis X praetoriae |(centurio) cohortis X urbanae |(centurio) cohortis IIII vigilum evocatus Augusti testamento poni iussit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-20800276.translation: Lucius Velius Prudens, son of Lucius, of the Clustumina tribe, a centurion of the Tenth Legion Fretensis, a three-hundred-man commander, chief of the camp, centurion of the Tenth Praetorian Cohort, centurion of the Tenth Urban Cohort, centurion of the Fourth Vigil Cohort, a recalled soldier of the Emperor, ordered this to be set up by his will; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20800276.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Velius Prudens, a high-ranking military officer, likely dating to the Hadrianic or Antonine period, indicating his career progression and posthumous honors.

EDCS-20800277.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aulus Munatius Hypnus and Aufidia Faustina set this up for their dearest son, Aulus Munatius Faustinus, a soldier of the Fourth Praetorian Cohort, who lived 19 years and 10 months.
EDCS-20800277.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young soldier of the Praetorian Guard, set up by his parents, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20800288.summary: This is a very brief funerary inscription for Cornelia Avonia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20800292.translation: To Gaetulica, farewell. To the spirits of the underworld. Her father, mother, and Marcus Callidius Saturninus, her uncle, made this for Gnaeus Postumius Gaetulicus, who lived 13 years, 2 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-20800292.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, Gnaeus Postumius Gaetulicus, set up by his parents and uncle, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20800296.translation: To the healing Hercules. Tiberius Claudius Dento, freedman of the Emperor, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20800296.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Hercules, dedicated by an imperial freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20800315.text_edition: \n Aulus Vensius Auli filius Arnensi Constans Acutia natus IIIIvir iterum II\n
EDCS-20800315.translation: Aulus Vensius Constans, son of Aulus, of the Arnensian tribe, previously a magistrate twice.
EDCS-20800315.summary: This is a brief inscription recording the name and civic offices of Aulus Vensius Constans, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20800566.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aulus Volumnius Pudens and Sallustia Ismirna (his parents) set this up for Aulus Volumnius Rufus, son of Aulus, of the Arnensian tribe, from Clusium, who lived 21 years, served in the Third Praetorian Cohort for 3 months and 15 days. May your bones rest well, may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-20800566.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aulus Volumnius Rufus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, set up by his parents, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20800570.translation: Lucius Thorius Tappo, son of Sextus.
EDCS-20800570.summary: This is a very brief inscription recording the name and parentage of Lucius Thorius Tappo, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20800571.text_edition: \n Publius Acilius Attianus praefectus praetorio Herculi sancto donum dedit\n
EDCS-20800571.translation: Publius Acilius Attianus, the prefect of the praetorium, dedicated this gift to the holy Hercules.
EDCS-20800571.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Hercules, dedicated by a high-ranking prefect of the Praetorian Guard, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20800572.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Encolpius erected this for his patron, Lucius Silius Gratus, procurator of the XX hereditatium.
EDCS-20800572.summary: A funerary dedication by Encolpius for his patron, a procurator managing inheritance taxes, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20800574.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Felix, his fellow slave, made this for Hilara Crescentis Domitiae, a dispensator (financial administrator) of the vicaria (district/ward), as deserving.
EDCS-20800574.summary: A funerary dedication by a slave for a female financial administrator, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20800590.text_edition: \n Caio Didio Cai filio Sabatina Saturnino primo pilo donato bello Parthico a divo Vero item bello Germanico a divo Marco torquibus et armillis item divis Severo et Magno Antonino corona aurea civica et hasta pura argentea ob insignia eius in rem publicam merita Saturnienses municipes patrono curam agente Caio Mintio Ur<b=S>o \n
EDCS-20800590.translation: To Gaius Didius Sabatinus Saturninus, son of Gaius, a primipilar (centurion) honored with torcs and armbands by the divine Verus in the Parthian War and by the divine Marcus in the Germanic War, and with a golden civic crown and a silver hasta pura by Divus Severus and Magnus Antoninus for his merits in public service, the townsfolk of Saturnia, honoring their patron, with Gaius Mintius Urbius acting as curator.
EDCS-20800590.summary: An honorary inscription for Gaius Didius Sabatinus Saturninus, a distinguished military officer and patron, who received numerous honors for his service and merits, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20800591.text_edition: \n Sexto Mecio Sexti filio Sabatina Marcello IIviro quinqquennali Saturniensium quaestori kalendarii rei publicae et alimentorum curatori pagi Lucretiani iuveni optimo et amantissimo ob multa eius beneficia pagani pagi supra scripti in se conlata dignissimo posuerunt locus datus ex decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-20800591.translation: The villagers of the aforementioned pagi erected this to Sextus Mecius Marcellus, son of Sextus, a former duovir and quinquennalis of the Saturnians, quaestor of the public treasury and administrator of the alimenta (welfare fund) for the pagi Lucretiani, an excellent and beloved young man, for his many benefits conferred upon them; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20800591.summary: A posthumous honorific inscription dedicated by the villagers to Sextus Mecius Marcellus, a local official and benefactor, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20800593.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Lucius Densius Lesinus, son of Lucius, erected this; his wife Valeria Bria with her son, their own [---] siuus, for him deserving well, who lived 35 years, 9 months.
EDCS-20800593.summary: A funerary inscription by a wife and son for Lucius Densius Lesinus, providing his age at death, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21000196.translation: Flavia Epictesis gave this gift to Fortuna.
EDCS-21000196.summary: A dedication of a gift to the goddess Fortuna by Flavia Epictesis, likely a votive offering from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21000206.translation: To Lucius Aconius Callistus, son of Lucius, of the Laurentes Lavinate [tribe/family].
EDCS-21000206.summary: An inscription likely identifying a person, Lucius Aconius Callistus, possibly related to the Laurentes Lavinate tribe, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21000260.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Gaius Avilius Profuturus, son, most beloved, who lived 6 years, 9 months, 7 (?) hours, 1 uncia.
EDCS-21000260.summary: A funerary inscription for a young boy, Gaius Avilius Profuturus, detailing his age at death, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21000266.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Allia Legitima [erected this] for her son, Lucius Didius Legitimus, deserving well, who lived 22 years.
EDCS-21000266.summary: A funerary inscription by a mother for her son, Lucius Didius Legitimus, stating his age at death, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21000267.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Sabinus, his fellow member, [erected this] for Lucius Didius Legitimus, deserving well.
EDCS-21000267.summary: A funerary inscription by a colleague or associate for Lucius Didius Legitimus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21000273.translation: A golden coin from Danae, [wife] dear to her husband, nor was it permitted [to her] living. My sweet Danae, [our] souls cannot [be parted]. Our [love] is not divided, for placed above is [the memory] of an exceptional wife, Herennius.
EDCS-21000273.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epigram or epitaph from an unknown context, possibly related to a Roman named Herennius and his wife Danae.

EDCS-21000279.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Larcia Primitiua, his dearest wife. Ulpius Adventus, her husband, and Larcius [her father/son?] dedicated this.
EDCS-21000279.summary: This is a dedication on a tombstone for Larcia Primitiua, set up by her husband Ulpius Adventus and another individual, likely a relative, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000286.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Caius Pomponius Substitutus. Pomponius Anicetus, his most dutiful son, dedicated [this].
EDCS-21000286.summary: This is a dedication on a tombstone for Caius Pomponius Substitutus, erected by his devoted son Pomponius Anicetus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000290.translation: To the spirits. [By] the author and protector. [Dedicated to] Sosia, freedwoman of Quintus, [from] Inturcius.
EDCS-21000290.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription possibly dedicating a monument to Sosia, a freedwoman, with a connection to Inturcius.

EDCS-21000332.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, sacred. For Marcus Furius Restitutus, who held the office of a college of four men and quaestor, Furius Optatus, his most dutiful father, saw to its construction.
EDCS-21000332.summary: This is a tombstone inscription dedicated to Marcus Furius Restitutus, a local magistrate, by his father Furius Optatus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000351.translation: [Publius] Cornelius Lentulus Marcellinus, with Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of a god, in his sixth consulship, and Marcus [Agrippa II] as consuls. Titus Septimius Sabinus, [city official?], Lucius [...], [abdicated?], Lucius Licinius Stolo, Titus S[...], [...], Egnatius Val[...], [...], Quintus Fiscilius?, A[...], [Marcus] Junius Silanus [...].
EDCS-21000351.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription listing names of consuls and other officials, possibly related to a public record or dedication from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-21000352.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto Titus Rufilius Priscus IIIIvir aedilis ex decreto decurionum pro ludis sua pecunia posuit\n
EDCS-21000352.translation: Titus Rufilius Priscus, holding the office of aedile for the fourth time and having served as quaestor, placed this at his own expense for the games, by decree of the decurions, in honor of Mars Augustus.
EDCS-21000352.summary: This is a dedication by Titus Rufilius Priscus, a local magistrate, for games honoring Mars Augustus, likely from a Roman provincial city.

EDCS-21000366.text_edition: \n Lucio Pomponio Luci filio Lupo IIIIviro iure dicundo quinquennali iterum tribuno militum legionis IIII Macedonicae praefecto cohortis equitatae Macedonum et cohortis Lusitanorum et Baliarium insularum ex senatus consulto ob munificentiam eius\n
EDCS-21000366.translation: By decree of the Senate, Lucius Pomponius Lupus, son of Lucius, who twice held the office of quinquennial judge and served as tribune of the fourth Macedonian legion, prefect of an equestrian cohort of Macedonians, and of the cohort of Lusitanians and the Balearic islanders, set this up at his own expense because of his generosity.
EDCS-21000366.summary: This is an inscription honoring Lucius Pomponius Lupus for his public service and generosity, detailing his military and administrative career, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21000370.text_edition: \n Sextus Hortensius Auli filius Stellatina Sextus Hortensius Clarus magister Larum accensus Germanici Caesaris consulis forum et Augusteum cum statuis LVII circa porticus et lacus et cloacas solo privato sua pecunia faciendum curavit idem dedicavit crustulum et epulum dedit\n
EDCS-21000370.translation: Sextus Hortensius Clarus, son of Aulus, of the Stellatina tribe, magister of the Lares, an attendant of Germanicus Caesar, when he was consul, constructed the forum and the Augusteum with 57 statues, the surrounding porticoes, lakes, and sewers at his own expense from private funds; he also dedicated it and provided a feast.
EDCS-21000370.summary: This is a dedication of a public building complex, including a forum and Augusteum, by Sextus Hortensius Clarus, a local official and attendant to Germanicus Caesar, funded privately.

EDCS-21000371.translation: [Dedicated to] Augus[tus?]. [Publius?] Cincius, son of Lucius, [served as] aedile [...]. Publius Cincius, son of Publius [...].
EDCS-21000371.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription mentioning individuals named Cincius, one of whom held the office of aedile, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21000374.text_edition: \n Lucio Cornelio Latino medico hic publice honoratus est et Corneliae Saturninae matri\n
EDCS-21000374.translation: Lucius Cornelius Latinus was honored here publicly as a physician, and his mother Cornelia Saturnina.
EDCS-21000374.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Lucius Cornelius Latinus, a physician, and his mother, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000429.translation: Postumia Tertia made this for her husband, patron, and son, Postumius Iustinus, who lived 38 years.
EDCS-21000429.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, who was also her patron and the father of her children, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000440.translation: Rufinus (dedicated this) to his freedman Logismus. From Pontus, the muse Terpsichore taught me my art. I lived for twenty years, in the time when life and the gods allowed. Now I lie here.
EDCS-21000440.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedman named Logismus, who was taught his art by the muse Terpsichore and lived for twenty years, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000450.translation: Gaius Metilius Saturninus, son of Spurius.
EDCS-21000450.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Gaius Metilius Saturninus, son of Spurius, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000453.translation: Gaius Attius Celsus, son of Lucius, of the Pomptina tribe, procurator of the 20% inheritance tax in Italy, and Gaius Lollius Autronianus, son of Gaius, of the Pomptina tribe, and Quintia Felicula, freedwoman of Titus, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-21000453.summary: This is an inscription recording a dedication of gifts by several individuals, including a tax official and a freedwoman, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000458.translation: To Publius Clitius Gallus, son of Publius, consul, IIIvir for money, silver, and gold, Salian priest of the Palatine, quaestor of Caesar, praetor, Augustalis priest crowned with the hasta pura, honored by the censorship by the emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus, father of the country, and by Titus Emperor Caesar Augustus' son; public land was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-21000458.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Publius Clitius Gallus for his various offices and honors, including his connection to emperors Vespasian and Titus, and records the granting of public land, likely from the Flavian period.

EDCS-21000460.text_edition: \n rnius Luci filius Horatia Asiaticus IIIIvir tribunus militum a populo \n
EDCS-21000460.translation: Renius Asiaticus, son of Lucius, of the Horatia tribe, IIIIvir, tribune of the soldiers, chosen by the people.
EDCS-21000460.summary: This inscription honors Renius Asiaticus, a prominent local official and military tribune, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000461.translation: Marcus Cincius Eros, freedman of Marcus, (dedicated this) to Marcus Cincius, son of Lucius, of the Horatia tribe, IIIIvir with the right to judge, tribune of the soldiers of the Gemella legion.
EDCS-21000461.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedman for his patron, who held significant local political and military offices, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000506.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Here lies Tyche, called 'queen', a servant. Her mother was Sabina, her nurse the earth of Tibur. At the end of her life, she is covered by the earth of Falisca.
EDCS-21000506.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a servant woman named Tyche, who is poetically described as a 'queen', with a contrast between her nurturing origins and her place of burial, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000507.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Veturius Clemens, son of Lucius, of the Horatia tribe, son of [Lucius Veturius Clemens].
EDCS-21000507.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Lucius Veturius Clemens, identifying his father and tribal affiliation, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21000518.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Livia Na, recording her age at death.

EDCS-21000530.text_edition: \n Publilio Luci filio Falerna Memoriali praefecto fabrum praefecto cohortis III Cyreneicae sic sagittariorum tribuno militum legionis X Fretensis praefecto gentis Numidarum dilictatori sic tironum ex Numidia lectorum legionis Augustae in Africa item E item ferrat[ae ------\n
EDCS-21000530.translation: To Publilius Memorialis, son of Lucius, of the Falerna tribe, prefect of the engineers, prefect of the 3rd cohort of Cyrenean sagittarii, military tribune of the 10th Fretensis legion, prefect of the Numidian people, dictator of the selected Numidian legionaries of the Augustan legion in Africa, likewise of the Ferrata [legion? ---]
EDCS-21000530.summary: This is a fragmented inscription honoring Publilius Memorialis, detailing his military and administrative career.

EDCS-21000531.translation: To Publius Aelius Agathoclianus, son of Publius, of the Palatina tribe, pontiff, praetor, scribe of the Laurentes Lavinates, senior quaestorian scribe, curule aedile's scribe, decurial pullarius maior, prefect of engineers three times, accredited velatus; the Foroclodians, by decree of the decurions, honored him as patron for his merits and because he was the first to donate marbles and columns to the public baths, on the occasion of whose dedication, distributions were given to the decurions and IIIIviris. Dedicated on the 7th day before the Ides of August, in the consulship of Gnaeus Claudius Severus II and Tiberius Claudius Pompeianus II.
EDCS-21000531.summary: This is a dedication by the people of Foroclodia to Publius Aelius Agathoclianus, honoring him as patron for his public benefactions, including the donation of materials for the public baths, with details of the accompanying feast and the date.

EDCS-21000532.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-21000532.translation: Dedicated on the 12th day before the Kalends of June, in the consulship of Quintus Volusius Flaccus and Lucius Aurelius Gallus. For the occasion of its dedication, he provided a banquet and 20 sesterces to each individual. To Lucius Cascellius Probus, son of Lucius, of the Voltinia tribe, quinquennial official, enrolled quaestor of provisions, curator of the grain supply; the decurions and the people of Foroclodia, of the Claudian prefecture, granted him a place due to his merits and dignity, by decree of the decurions. Under the supervision of Tiberius Julius Antistianus, Titus Volcasius Mercator, Titus Volcasius Ianvarius, Lucius Fufius Fructus. Here, he was the first of all to be enrolled in the order of quinquennial officials on the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Pudens and Orfitus. Because of the honor offered to him, he donated to the treasury of the prefecture of Claudia a silver image of the Genius of the prefecture, weighing six pounds, with a bronze base, at his own expense. For the occasion of its dedication, he gave 100 sesterces to each patron and decurion, likewise 50 sesterces to the public ministries, and provided a banquet with distributions to his fellow townsmen.
EDCS-21000532.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a silver image of the Genius of the Claudian prefecture by Lucius Cascellius Probus, who was honored by the people of Foroclodia for his public service and benefactions, including banquets and donations, with specific dates and officials mentioned.

EDCS-21000533.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Avillius Petronianus and Popilia Rufiana made this for their dearest son, who lived 7 years, 7 months, and 5 days.
EDCS-21000533.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their young son, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-21100440.translation: Marcus Aeteius Iucundus, freedman of a woman, lived 60 years.
EDCS-21100440.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the name and lifespan of Marcus Aeteius Iucundus.

EDCS-21100456.translation: To Sextus Congenius Verus, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, of Bessian origin, lived 42 years, served 22 years, on the trireme Asclepius. Atilius Carus and Vettius Longinus, heirs, made this for him, a deserving man.
EDCS-21100456.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Congenius Verus, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet, detailing his origin, age, and length of service.

EDCS-21100458.translation: Erclanius Gregarius lived 40 years, killed by the wicked.
EDCS-21100458.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Erclanius Gregarius, stating his age at death and the cause of his demise.

EDCS-21100466.summary: This is a fragmented inscription identifying Aulus T[---] as a freedman.

EDCS-21100474.text_edition: \n Lucio Censorino Caio Calvisio consulibus heisce magistri aram Silvano Martio faciendam curaverunt Marcus Appius Marci libertus Apollodorus Publius Caprilius Publi libertus Diocles Lucius Veturius |(mulieris) Luci libertus Antiochus Aulus Laelius Auli libertus Salvi Marcus Magilius Marci libertus Protogenes Caius Antestius |(mulieris) libertus Bithus Aulus Antestius |(mulieris) libertus Eros Aulus nnus Auli libertus Philodamus Marcus Thorius Marci libertus Nestor Neicepor sic Capreili Publi servus Lucrio Memmi Luci servus Asclepiades Memmi Luci servus NI Marcus Gargilius Marci libertus Hipponicus\n
EDCS-21100474.translation: In the consulship of Lucius Censorinus and Caius Calvisius, the following masters had the altar of Silvanus Martius made: Marcus Appius Apollodorus, freedman of Marcus; Publius Caprilius Diocles, freedman of Publius; Lucius Veturius Antiochus, freedman of a woman; Aulus Salvi, freedman of Aulus Laelius; Marcus Protogenes, freedman of Marcus; Caius Bithus, freedman of a woman; Eros, freedman of Aulus nnus; Philodamus, freedman of Aulus; Nestor, freedman of Marcus Thorius; Lucrio, slave of Capreilius Publius; Asclepiades, slave of Memmius Lucius; NI, slave of Memmius Lucius; Hipponicus, freedman of Marcus Gargilius.
EDCS-21100474.summary: This inscription records the construction of an altar to Silvanus Martius, supervised by several freedmen and slaves, during the consulship of Lucius Censorinus and Caius Calvisius.

EDCS-21100567.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Quintus, son of Somnius.

EDCS-21100598.summary: This inscription records the names of Aulus Coponius and Titus Titinius, likely as dedicator(s) or honoree(s).

EDCS-21100605.translation: To the August Lares, to Diana of Fregenae, by Marcus Avillius, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-21100605.summary: This is a dedication to the Lares Augusti and Diana Fregenae by Marcus Avillius, a freedman.

EDCS-21100606.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lenaeus and Primitiva made this for their most dutiful son, Secundinus, who lived for 3 years and 5 months.
EDCS-21100606.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Secundinus, erected by his parents Lenaeus and Primitiva, who lived for 3 years and 5 months.

EDCS-21100608.translation: To Cassia Paternus, from Iulius Aspri. Iulius Camilius Asper, Iulius Severus Calidus, and Camilia Aequa, children.
EDCS-21100608.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Cassia Paternus by her children, Iulius Camilius Asper, Iulius Severus Calidus, and Camilia Aequa, mentioning Iulius Aspri.

EDCS-21100613.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gnaeus Arrius [---], his [son?] Gnaeus Arrius [---] made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-21100613.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Gnaeus Arrius to another Gnaeus Arrius, likely his father or relative.

EDCS-21100614.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. To Marcus Aurelius, freedman of Augustus, and his wife Valeria Quieta. Aurelius Leo, from his uncle, and Terina, his parents.
EDCS-21100614.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Marcus Aurelius, freedman of Augustus, and his wife Valeria Quieta, by their relatives.

EDCS-21100630.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Atia Calybes.
EDCS-21100630.summary: This is a funerary epitaph mentioning Atia Calybes.

EDCS-21100631.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudia Tyche made this for her well-deserving husband, Tiberius Claudius Epithychanus.
EDCS-21100631.summary: This funerary epitaph records Claudia Tyche dedicating a monument to her husband, Tiberius Claudius Epithychanus.

EDCS-21100632.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelius Antoninus to his most sacred wife, Claudia Gaiana Lepida.
EDCS-21100632.summary: This funerary epitaph records Aelius Antoninus dedicating a monument to his wife, Claudia Gaiana Lepida.

EDCS-21100634.translation: To the memory of Lucius Numitorius, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, from his father; and Minicia, daughter of Publius, his mother; and Fulvia, his wife. Sextus Numitorius, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe; and Sextus Numitorius Virgula.
EDCS-21100634.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication for Sextus Numitorius, listing his father, mother, and wife, and also mentioning Sextus Numitorius Virgula, likely another family member or the deceased himself.

EDCS-21100636.translation: Consecrated to the departed spirits. Seia Musa made this for herself and for her dearest husband, Gnaeus Numisius Primus, who lived 75 years, 3 months, and 15 days; and for her most dutiful daughter, Numisia Felicitas, who lived 18 years, 10 months, and 12 days; and for Publius Aelius Iulianus, who deserved well; and for her freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-21100636.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Seia Musa for herself and her family, including her husband, daughter, and a deserving individual named Publius Aelius Iulianus, along with their freedmen and descendants.

EDCS-21100645.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Quintus Caecilius Amandus scriba librarius quaestorius III decuriarum et Quintus Tullius Quinti filius Falerna Caudio Amandus donum dederunt\n
EDCS-21100645.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Quintus Caecilius Amandus, a secretary of the quaestorian rank for three decades, and Quintus Tullius Caudio Amandus, son of Quintus, of the Falerna tribe, gave this offering.
EDCS-21100645.summary: This inscription records an offering dedicated to Silvanus by two individuals, Quintus Caecilius Amandus, a secretary, and Quintus Tullius Caudio Amandus.

EDCS-21100648.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Augusti libertus Pardalas Apollinis parasitus ego sum bene tibi sit qui me legis et tibi Apolausti maioris condiscipulus Apolausti iunioris doctor Tiberius Claudius Pardalas Augusti libertus Apollinis parasitus aego sic sum bene tibi sit qui me legeris Quisquae sic me vexsaverit sic quisquae sic meo corpori manus intulerit quartanas dabo ex viso magno imperio iussus sum cum viverem\n
EDCS-21100648.translation: I, Tiberius Claudius Pardalas, freedman of Augustus, parasite of Apollo, am here. May you be well, who reads me, and you, Aplaustus the elder's fellow student and Aplaustus the younger's teacher. Tiberius Claudius Pardalas, freedman of Augustus, parasite of Apollo, am as I am. May you be well, who reads me. Whoever troubles me thus, whoever lays hands on my body thus, I will give him quartan fever, by great command, ordered when I was alive.
EDCS-21100648.summary: This unusual inscription is a curse formula and a boast by Tiberius Claudius Pardalas, a freedman of Augustus and a parasite of Apollo, intended to deter anyone who disturbs him or his monument, potentially dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-21100668.text_edition: \n Domninus patri dulcissimo Antonia Castorino coniugi bene merenti qui vixit annis LX diebus V recess<i=E>t post consulatum Limeni Catulini pridie Nonas Martias \n
EDCS-21100668.translation: To Domninus, my dearest father; to Antonia Castorina, my deserving wife, who lived 60 years and 5 days. She passed away on the day before the Nones of March, after the consulship of Limenus Catulinus.
EDCS-21100668.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to a father named Domninus and a wife named Antonia Castorina, noting the wife's lifespan and the date of her death in relation to the consulship of Limenus Catulinus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-21100669.translation: To Martius Firmissimus, the presbyter, and to Decimia Aproniana, his most beloved wife, who deserved well and lived with me for 31 years, 7 months, and 13 days. Laid to rest on the 3rd Ides of August, in peace.
EDCS-21100669.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Martius Firmissimus, a presbyter, and his wife Decimia Aproniana, recording her lifespan and burial date, likely from the 4th or 5th century CE.

EDCS-21100671.translation: On the Kalends of January, let no one allow Julianetes or Lucentius to open.
EDCS-21100671.summary: This is a prohibition inscription, likely related to access or opening of a tomb or property, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21100672.text_edition: \n Evvangelia sic deposita IIII Kalendas Iulias Leontio et Sallustio\n
EDCS-21100672.translation: The Gospels thus deposited on the 4th day before the Kalends of July for Leontius and Sallustius.
EDCS-21100672.summary: This inscription records the deposition of the Gospels on a specific date for two individuals, Leontius and Sallustius, likely in a Christian context from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-21300789.translation: By the authority of Quintus Iunius Rusticus, prefect of the city.
EDCS-21300789.summary: This is an official endorsement or authorization by Quintus Iunius Rusticus, the urban prefect, indicating a decree or legal action.

EDCS-21800414.translation: Mummius Niger and Valerius Vegetus, the consular, brought their own Vegetian water, which rises in the Antonianum Maius estate of Publius Tullius Varro, along with the place where this spring is located, to his Calvisiana villa, which is near the Passerian waters. They acquired and transferred ownership of places and routes for this water from the owners of each respective estate through which the above-mentioned water was led, for a width of ten feet in structures, for six feet in width through pipes, through the estates Antonianum Maius and Antonianum Minus of Publius Tullius Varro, and Baebianum and Philinianum of Avileius Commodus, and Petronianum of Publius Tullius Varro, and Volsonianum of Herennius Polybius, and Fundanianum of Caetennius Proculus, and Cuttolonianum of Cornelius Latinus, and Serranum Inferius of Quintinus Verecundus, and Capitonianum of Pisibanius Celsus, and along the left-hand base of the public road Ferentiensis, and Scirpianum of Pisibania Lepida, and along the Cassian Way to his Calvisiana villa, likewise along public roads and boundaries, by permission of a senatorial decree.
EDCS-21800414.summary: This inscription details a significant engineering project by Mummius Niger and Valerius Vegetus, a consular, who constructed an aqueduct over five miles long to supply water from an estate belonging to Publius Tullius Varro to their villa, meticulously listing the estates and lands traversed and the legal permissions obtained via a senatorial decree.

EDCS-22000013.translation: Alive, Titus Marius Laetus, freedman of Titus, made [this] for himself and for Senatia Galla, freedwoman of Lucius, his fellow soldier, 15 feet in the field and 15 feet in the front.
EDCS-22000013.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Marius Laetus, specifying the dimensions of his burial plot.

EDCS-22000015.translation: To the departed spirits. Narenia Hermione... [untranslatable text] ...
EDCS-22000015.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary dedication to Narenia Hermione.

EDCS-22000016.translation: Lucius Nunnedius, son of Gaius, [for himself] and his freedmen, 10 feet in the front and 10 feet in the field.
EDCS-22000016.summary: This inscription records the dimensions of a plot of land purchased by Lucius Nunnedius for his burial.

EDCS-22000028.translation: ...to his most dutiful comrade Tigris, [name] placed [this] for [him], well-deserving, 7 feet in the field and 4 feet in the front.
EDCS-22000028.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a comrade named Tigris, specifying the dimensions of the burial plot.

EDCS-22000078.translation: To Quenia Audentia, his most beloved wife, well-deserving, Gaius Papirius Constantius [dedicates this].
EDCS-22000078.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Gaius Papirius Constantius to his wife, Quenia Audentia.

EDCS-22000084.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Lauselio Maxsimo sic et Cauliae Thyche sic Marcus Lauselius Tacitus et Lauselia Tacita parentibus et Faesonia Sabina et Marcus Lauselius Sabinus neptes bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-22000084.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Lauselius Maxsimus, and for Caulia Thyche, Marcus Lauselius Tacitus and Lauselia Tacita, parents, and Faesonia Sabina and Marcus Lauselius Sabinus, grandchildren, have set [this] up for the well-deserving.
EDCS-22000084.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by grandchildren to their deceased parents, Marcus Lauselius Maxsimus and Caulia Thyche.

EDCS-22000086.translation: Alive, Gaius Gavius Asper, son of Lucius, made [this] for Lucius Gavius, son of Quintus, his father; for Graecia, daughter of Aulus, Quintus, his mother; for Aulus Gavius, son of Quintus, his paternal uncle; for Lucius Gavius Mansuetus, son of Lucius, his brother, a soldier of the 12th urban cohort, who lived 36 years and served 5 years.
EDCS-22000086.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Gaius Gavius Asper for his deceased family members, including his brother who was a soldier.

EDCS-22000091.translation: To the departed spirits. For his most dutiful father, Aulus Resius Sabinus, son of Lucius, of the Sabatina tribe.
EDCS-22000091.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to a father, Aulus Resius Sabinus.

EDCS-22000092.translation: [For] Paenius Restitutus, son of Lucius... [and for] Saenius Romanus, soldier of the 11th urban cohort, [his] century... [daughter] ...cia, freedwoman of Lucius Romanus, to her husband, [she] made [this]. // To the departed spirits. For Varia Severa, freedwoman of Lucius, Sextus Axius Philadespotus [dedicated this] to his most excellent wife.
EDCS-22000092.summary: This monument contains two separate funerary inscriptions, one for a soldier and one for a wife, set up by their respective families or partners.

EDCS-22000095.translation: Gaius Celtus Severus, son of Gaius, of the Sabatina tribe, quaestor, aedile, pontifex.
EDCS-22000095.summary: This is a funerary epitaph listing the public offices held by Gaius Celtus Severus.

EDCS-22000115.translation: Lucius Caecina, son of Lucius, from the Tlabon family, lived 30 years.
EDCS-22000115.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Lucius Caecina.

EDCS-22000124.translation: On the third day before the Ides of November. To the departed spirits. Lucius Vetina Priscus erected this for his most excellent wife, Didia Quintina, daughter of Quintus, who lived 27 years.
EDCS-22000124.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Didia Quintina by her husband, Lucius Vetina Priscus.

EDCS-22000154.translation: To Caius Tanusius Balbinus, son of Titus, of the Anician family.
EDCS-22000154.summary: A dedication, likely funerary, to Caius Tanusius Balbinus.

EDCS-22000155.translation: To Iulius Martialis, optio of the 3rd Praetorian Cohort, in the century of Valens, who lived 34 years. His heir erected this for one who deserved well.
EDCS-22000155.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Martialis, an optio in the Praetorian Guard.

EDCS-22000157.translation: Caius Laetilius Lupus, son of Lucius, from the Oufentina tribe, for himself and his family. Soldier of the 4th Praetorian Cohort. Satria Venusta, his wife, [dedicated this to] Laetilius Phil[...].
EDCS-22000157.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Laetilius Lupus and his wife Satria Venusta, both associated with the Praetorian Guard.

EDCS-22000158.translation: To the departed spirits. For Aulus Vicirius, son of Aulus, from the Arnensis tribe, [a soldier] of the 4th Scythian Legion, aedile of Etruria, a flamen Augusti. By decree of the decurions, a public funeral with [...] for his merits.
EDCS-22000158.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Aulus Vicirius, a veteran of the 4th Scythian Legion and a local magistrate, for whom a public funeral was decreed.

EDCS-22000164.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Perperna Agathopus, Saturnina, and Fortunata, heirs and freedmen, erected this for Caius Perperna Geminus, son of Caius, of the Sergia tribe, who lived 68 years.
EDCS-22000164.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Perperna Geminus, erected by his heirs and freedmen.

EDCS-22000167.translation: To the departed spirits. For Caius Vibius Clemens, son of Caius, of the Oufentina tribe, of the equestrian order, who lived 18 years, 4 months, and 18 days. [Erected by...] P[...]
EDCS-22000167.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Vibius Clemens, a young man of equestrian rank.

EDCS-22000175.translation: Quintus Letinius Lupus, also called Cau/cadion, who is the son of Sallustia Veneria or Veneriosa, I entrust and dedicate and sacrifice him to your divinity here among you, that you may kill him, may kill him within this same year.
EDCS-22000175.summary: A magical curse tablet calling upon deities to inflict harm or death upon Quintus Letinius Lupus.

EDCS-22000180.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-22000180.translation: Appius Claudius Caecus, son of Caius, censor, consul twice, dictator, interrex three times, praetor twice, curule aedile twice, quaestor, military tribune three times, conquered many towns from the Samnites, routed the army of the Sabines and Tuscans, prohibited peace from being made with King Pyrrhus, paved the Appian Way in his censorship and brought water into the city, made the temple of Bellona.
EDCS-22000180.summary: An inscription detailing the extensive political and military career and public works of Appius Claudius Caecus, a prominent Roman statesman.

EDCS-22000181.translation: To Quintus Fabius Maximus, son of Quintus, twice dictator, consul five times, censor, interrex twice, curule aedile, quaestor twice, military tribune twice, pontifex, augur. In his first consulship, he subdued the Ligurians and triumphed over them; in his third and fourth years, by shadowing Hannibal, ferocious from many victories, he kept him in check as dictator, came to the aid of the army routed under his master of the horse, Minucius, whose command the people had made equal to that of the dictator, and for this reason was called 'father' by Minucius' army. As consul for the fifth time, he captured Tarentum and triumphed. He was considered the most cautious leader of his age and the most skilled in military affairs. He was chosen as princeps senatus for two five-year terms.
EDCS-22000181.summary: This is a laudatory epitaph or biographical inscription for Quintus Fabius Maximus, detailing his extensive military and political career in the Roman Republic, likely dating to the late 3rd century BCE.

EDCS-22000189.translation: To Lucius Martius Macro, son of Lucius, of the Pomptina tribe, Salian priest of the Palatine, decemvir of judicial struggles, triumvir for striking gold and silver, quaestor, and legate of the province of Achaia, tribune of the plebs, praetor, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22000189.summary: This is a funerary or honorific inscription honoring Lucius Martius Macro, listing his priesthoods and magistracies, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-22000191.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Vettius Florentinus, a most distinguished man, formerly a governor, of singular innocence, lived 28 years, 7 months, and 6 days.
EDCS-22000191.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Vettius Florentinus, recording his name, status as a former governor, virtues, and lifespan, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000192.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aegnatius Diogenes, from the trireme Capricorn, of Alexandrian origin, lived 21 years, served 3 years. His heir, Camulius Claudianus, to one who deserved well.
EDCS-22000192.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aegnatius Diogenes, a sailor from Alexandria, detailing his age and length of service, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000193.translation: To Sextus Satrius Iustus, son of Sextus, of the Palatina tribe, prefect of the guilds of Augustus, quaestor, aedile, duovir. The urban plebs, with their own money.
EDCS-22000193.summary: This is a dedication by the urban plebs for Sextus Satrius Iustus, honoring his public offices including prefect of guilds and duovir, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000194.translation: To the departed spirits. To Quintus Volcacius Celer, son of Quintus, soldier of the 9th praetorian cohort, beneficiary of Tribune Atticus, served 8 years, lived 25 years.
EDCS-22000194.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Volcacius Celer, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, detailing his unit, rank, and lifespan, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000195.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Corpennius Sabinus, son of Aulus, of the Pomptina tribe, formerly sevir. Apolinaris [dedicates this].
EDCS-22000195.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Corpennius Sabinus, a former sevir, with the dedicator named as Apolinaris, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000200.translation: To Lucius Valerius Iunior, son of Lucius, of the Pomptina tribe, of equestrian rank, sworn to the sacred rites of Etruria, pontifex, quaestor, aedile, duovir, accensus velatus. The urban plebs. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22000200.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Lucius Valerius Iunior, detailing his priesthood and magistracies, dedicated by the urban plebs, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000202.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Marcus Terentius Sabinianus. Marcus Terentius Secundus [dedicates this] to his most devoted son, a haruspex.
EDCS-22000202.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Terentius Sabinianus, dedicated by his father Marcus Terentius Secundus, mentioning his profession as a haruspex, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000203.translation: Lucius Aelius, son of Marcus, of the Pomptina tribe.
EDCS-22000203.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that appears to be the beginning of a funerary or honorific text for Lucius Aelius, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000204.translation: To Publius Avillius Phileros, 12 feet in front, 12 feet in the field.
EDCS-22000204.summary: This is a boundary inscription measuring land for Publius Avillius Phileros.

EDCS-22000206.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Cadrinnius Crescentianus, foster son, to his well-deserving Marcus Cadrinnius Clemens, with his own money.
EDCS-22000206.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a foster son to his deceased foster father, Marcus Cadrinnius Clemens.

EDCS-22000209.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Cilnia Iustina, his incomparable wife, who lived 30 years, 29 days. Flavius Annianus (set this up).
EDCS-22000209.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Flavius Annianus for his wife, Cilnia Iustina, noting her age at death.

EDCS-22000210.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Cilnia Severa, wife to Marcus Caurisinius Successus.
EDCS-22000210.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cilnia Severa, wife of Marcus Caurisinius Successus.

EDCS-22000212.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Claudia Galla, an excellent mother. From her sons Gallus and Postuma.
EDCS-22000212.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by two sons for their excellent mother, Claudia Galla.

EDCS-22000215.translation: Crispinia Firma, daughter of Lucius, (belonging to) Valerius Festus.
EDCS-22000215.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or relation of Crispinia Firma to Valerius Festus.

EDCS-22000216.translation: Lucius Valerius Festus, son of Aulus, of the tribe Pomptina.
EDCS-22000216.summary: This is a simple funerary or identification inscription for Lucius Valerius Festus.

EDCS-22000217.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Briatia Tecumen, freedwoman. From her freedmen Diadumenus and Agaratus.
EDCS-22000217.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by two freedmen for their former mistress, Briatia Tecumen.

EDCS-22000223.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Gargilius Rufi Marianus, son of Publius, of the tribe Quirina, who lived 57 years, 5 months, 13 days. His sister Castricia Maura made this for her most devoted brother, who deserved well of her.
EDCS-22000223.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Lucius Gargilius Rufi Marianus and was set up by his sister, Castricia Maura.

EDCS-22000224.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Grata, his excellent wife. From Gaius Caesius Callinicus.
EDCS-22000224.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Gaius Caesius Callinicus for his excellent wife, Grata.

EDCS-22000225.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Helvia Tigris, freedwoman and wife, dedicated this to her well-deserving patron, Publius Helvius Crescentes.
EDCS-22000225.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a freedwoman and wife to her patron, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22000226.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Helvia Tertulla, daughter of Publius. Crescens, freedman.
EDCS-22000226.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Helvia Tertulla, dedicated by her freedman Crescens.

EDCS-22000229.translation: For Lucius Laronius Diogenes. Priscus, freedman. Frontage 12 feet, land 10 feet.
EDCS-22000229.summary: This is a funerary epitaph that also records the dimensions of a plot of land for Lucius Laronius Diogenes, set up by his freedman Priscus.

EDCS-22000230.translation: Marcus Volasenna Ferox made this for his pious mother, Lucceia Nymphe, freedwoman of Aulus. Frontage 10 feet, land 12 feet.
EDCS-22000230.summary: This is a funerary epitaph and boundary marker for Lucceia Nymphe, a freedwoman, made by her son Marcus Volasenna Ferox.

EDCS-22000233.translation: To... Caius Memmius Felix... to his fellow student and friend, who deserved excellently of him... Restitutus... Felix grateful...
EDCS-22000233.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Caius Memmius Felix, commemorating a deceased fellow student and friend, with significant portions missing.

EDCS-22000249.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a funerary epitaph or a dedication, for Marcus Ulattius.

EDCS-22000686.translation: Marcus Sertorius Verecundus, son of Marcus, of the Stellatina tribe, from Cortona, soldier of the 1st Praetorian Cohort, century of Annius. He lived 21 years, served 5 years. He ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-22000686.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Sertorius Verecundus, a Praetorian guardsman, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22000687.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Gellius Hesychus, freedman of Lucius, VIvir Augustalis. Crescen[s?]
EDCS-22000687.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Lucius Gellius Hesychus, a VIvir Augustalis, likely dedicated by Crescens.

EDCS-22000689.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Octavius Venustus, cultor of Minerva.
EDCS-22000689.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Octavius Venustus, a worshipper of Minerva.

EDCS-22000691.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For the very dear father... Cocceius Festus... Aprylia... Cocceius...
EDCS-22000691.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a father, likely belonging to the Cocceius family.

EDCS-22000694.translation: To the divine shades of Aulus Gellius Myrinus.
EDCS-22000694.summary: A funerary dedication to Aulus Gellius Myrinus, likely a tombstone inscription from an unknown date.

EDCS-22000703.translation: Titus Annius Largus, son of Lucius, fulfilled his vow to the household gods for military service willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-22000703.summary: A votive inscription by Titus Annius Largus fulfilling a vow to the Penates for military service, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000707.translation: Under the consulship of Marcus Vibius Liberalis and Publius Martius Verus, on the 10th day before the Kalends of April, in Augusta Perusia. In the Laelian school, while writing, Publius Casinerius Clemens, IIvir, in his own name and in the name of his colleague Lucius Petilius Nepos, spoke. He asked Annian Leona that, according to the terms of the will of Egnatius Festus, a site be assigned to him for the statue he was going to place for the deified Pius. What it would please to do concerning this matter, they decreed as follows: it is pleasing that, according to the wish of Egnatius Festus, the site for the statue to be placed for the deified Pius be granted next to Naevius Libanus at the entrance of the curia. For the deified Antoninus Pius. Gaius Egnatius Festus, aedile and IIvir, to whom the urban plebs offered money for public games he was presenting and for the statue to be placed for himself, was content with that honor, remitted the expense, and having obtained permission from the council of Perusia, placed it with his own money. On account of its dedication, he ordered to be given from his heir to the decurions 4000 sesterces, to the plebs 2000 sesterces. Site granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22000707.summary: A decree of the decurions of Perusia granting a site for a statue of the deified Antoninus Pius, commissioned by Gaius Egnatius Festus, who funded it himself after the populace offered to pay for games and the statue; it also records donations from Festus to the decurions and plebs upon the statue's dedication, dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-22000708.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holder of tribunician power for the second time, proconsul, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, the bravest, most fortunate, father of the country, grandson of the deified Marcus Aurelius Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, and great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, by public decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22000708.summary: An inscription dedicating a public monument or building to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla) and listing his extensive titulature, emphasizing his lineage and imperial authority, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22000710.translation: Dedicated on the Ides of July, when Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix was consul for the second time [[and Publius Septimius Geta Nobilissimus Caesar was consul]]. To Gaius Vibius Gallus Proculeianus, son of Gaius, nephew of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, patron of the Perusines, patron and curator of the Republic of the Vettonenses, judge from the V decuriae, aedile, patron of the guild of felt-makers. Vibius Veldumnianus to his dearest grandfather, on whose account. For its dedication, he gave to the decurions 2 denarii, to the plebs 1 denarius. Site granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22000710.summary: A dedication of a site for a statue or monument to Gaius Vibius Gallus Proculeianus, a prominent local figure and patron of several communities, including Perusia and the Vettonenses, dating to the joint consulship of Caracalla and Geta (208 CE).

EDCS-22000718.text_edition: \n Caius Atilius Auli filius Glabrio IIIIvir quinquennalis praefectus fabrum delatus a consule praefectus cohortis corum sagittariorum Titia Auli filia minore natus\n
EDCS-22000718.translation: Gaius Atilius Glabrio, son of Aulus, quinquennial duumvir, prefect of the armourers, appointed by the consul, prefect of the cohort of archers, Titia daughter of Aulus, born younger.
EDCS-22000718.summary: A fragmented inscription listing the career and family of Gaius Atilius Glabrio, a Roman official who held various military and administrative posts, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22000720.translation: To the divine shades. For Publius Pacilius Alcaeus, soldier of the 10th urban cohort, century of Verus. He served for 2 years, lived for 22 years. Publius Pacilius Leo made (this) for his most dutiful son and for himself.
EDCS-22000720.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Pacilius Alcaeus, a soldier in the 10th Urban Cohort, who died at age 22 after 2 years of service, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000721.translation: To the divine shades. [---] of the Versenus family, son of Lucius, of the Lemonia tribe, Granianus, tribune of the 32nd cohort of volunteers, tribune of the 16th Flavian Firm legion, IIvir of the Hispellates, patron of the municipium of Arna(tes). L(ucius) Versenus Aper (made this) for his most dutiful brother. He lived 32 years.
EDCS-22000721.summary: A funerary inscription for Granianus, a soldier and officer who served in auxiliary and legionary units, and also held civic positions including IIvir and patron, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22000725.translation: To Gaius Betuus Cilo Minucianus Valens Antonius Celer, son of Gaius, of the Tromentina tribe, to Publius Liguvius Rufinus Liguvianus, aedile, quinquennial duumvir, priest of the three groves, praetor of Etruria XV populorum, patron of the municipium. Betua Respectilla, daughter, to her most dutiful father. Site granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22000725.summary: A funerary dedication honoring Gaius Betuus Cilo Minucianus Valens Antonius Celer, a prominent local official with various civic and religious roles, including patron of the municipium, by his daughter Betua Respectilla, with the site granted by decree of the decurions, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000733.translation: Tessera. The paganal association made (it). Lucius Farusanus Favor donated (it) to his magistracy publicly. In the Paetinian pagus, from the estate of Gaius Terentius Iunior. Willingly (he) placed (it), servant.
EDCS-22000733.summary: A tessera (token or marker) donated by Lucius Farusanus Favor to his paganal association in the Paetiniana pagus, from the land of Gaius Terentius Iunior, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22000738.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Grania Urbana made this for her well-deserving husband, Nomius, the actor of the Postimii.
EDCS-22000738.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a wife to her husband, who was an actor for the Postimii family.

EDCS-22100001.text_edition: \n Kharisia Cai filia Magnina sibi et Caio Caecilio Victori |(centurioni marito et Caio Caecilio Victori filio equiti Romano et Tito Flavio Felici filio frumentario legionis VI victricis et libertis libertabus posterisque suis eorum cum filiis sua pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-22100001.translation: Magnina, daughter of Caius, gave this herself and for Caius Caecilius Victor, her husband, a centurion, and for Caius Caecilius Victor, her son, a Roman cavalryman, and for Titus Flavius Felix, her son, a grain official of the VI Victrix legion, and for her freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, with their children, with her own money.
EDCS-22100001.summary: A funerary monument erected by Magnina for herself and her family, including her husband and sons holding military and administrative positions, funded by her own money.

EDCS-22100002.translation: To Quintus Atrius Iucundianus, a most excellent man, honored bisellarius, with decurional ornaments, the Senate and People of Pisana, by contributed money, because he increased with his own funds the sum for the grain supply by 80,000 sesterces and dedicated the curtains in the theater with all decoration at his own expense. His place was granted by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-22100002.summary: A public dedication honoring Quintus Atrius Iucundianus for his financial contributions to the grain supply and the theater, funded by public subscription and his own money, with a seat granted to him by the Senate.

EDCS-22100193.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Claudia Olympiadis.
EDCS-22100193.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Olympiadis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100200.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Volusia Sabina for her well-deserving husband, Caius Hosidius Sigerianus.
EDCS-22100200.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a wife to her husband, Caius Hosidius Sigerianus.

EDCS-22100203.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Blaesius Festus for Iulia Isiadis.
EDCS-22100203.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Lucius Blaesius Festus to Iulia Isiadis.
