EDCS-22200536.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Cominius Crescentis, who lived 14 years, 10 months, and 8 days. To a most beloved son from his parents.
EDCS-22200536.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, Lucius Cominius Crescentis, set up by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200537.translation: Gaius Considius, son of Gaius, of the Voltinia tribe.
EDCS-22200537.summary: This is a brief inscription recording the name and tribal affiliation of Gaius Considius, likely a citizen, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200538.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cosinia Nice, freedwoman, made this for Gaius Cosinius Primitivus, well-deserving.
EDCS-22200538.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by a freedwoman for her patron, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200553.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Modia Leontis made this for her husband Modius Leon.
EDCS-22200553.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her deceased husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200556.translation: Aulus Olsadius Firmu, son of Aulus, of the Voltinia tribe.
EDCS-22200556.summary: This is a brief inscription recording the name and tribal affiliation of Aulus Olsadius Firmu, likely a citizen, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200561.translation: To the departed spirits. To Petronia Iusta, my excellent wife, Larcius Priscianus and Petronius Firmus and Caecinia Rufina [dedicate].
EDCS-22200561.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a husband and possibly other relatives to Petronia Iusta.

EDCS-22200567.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelia Veneria dedicated this to Sergius Iulianus, her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22200567.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelia Veneria to her husband Sergius Iulianus.

EDCS-22200585.translation: To the departed spirits. Vibennius Adiectus made this for his well-deserving wife, Vibennia Cogitata.
EDCS-22200585.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Vibennius Adiectus to his wife Vibennia Cogitata.

EDCS-22200588.translation: To the departed spirits of Lucius Volsinius Primigenius.
EDCS-22200588.summary: This is an incomplete funerary dedication to Lucius Volsinius Primigenius.

EDCS-22200816.translation: To the curator Sextus Campanius Reditus, by decree of the decurions, for Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, designated emperor.
EDCS-22200816.summary: This is a dedication to Sextus Campanius Reditus, curator, for the future emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, authorized by the town council.

EDCS-22200832.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Severus, most noble Caesar, the council and the people of Vulcentium, devoted to his divine majesty and power.
EDCS-22200832.summary: This is a dedication by the civic authorities of Vulcentium to the Caesar Flavius Valerius Severus.

EDCS-22200834.translation: Gaius Septimius, son of Gaius, of the Sabatina tribe, quaestor with judicial authority.
EDCS-22200834.summary: This inscription records the career of Gaius Septimius, a local magistrate.

EDCS-22200839.translation: Gaius Claius Venerius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe; Volumnia Hilara, freedwoman of Lucius.
EDCS-22200839.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording two individuals, possibly a couple or patrons and clients.

EDCS-22200841.translation: To the departed spirits. Octavia Civica's daughter Coelia Pardalis and Octavius Epaphroditus, her husband, who deserved well of her.
EDCS-22200841.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Coelia Pardalis and her husband Octavius Epaphroditus.

EDCS-22200844.translation: [To] Ianuarius [who lived] approximately [3] years, Murrhia [to her husband, who lived] approximately [3] years with whom she lived, made this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-22200844.summary: This is an incomplete funerary dedication by Murrhia to her husband Ianuarius.

EDCS-22200857.translation: Gaius Aunius Aper willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-22200857.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Aunius Aper dedicating something to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-22200858.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions Natalis and the use of personal funds, possibly for a building project.

EDCS-22200861.translation: To the departed spirits. Tutilia Maxima made this for her husband, Lucius Aemilius Festus, son of Lucius, a decurial haruspex, a most excellent and most dutiful husband, who lived 44 years. He deserved well.
EDCS-22200861.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Aemilius Festus, a haruspex, set up by his wife Tutilia Maxima.

EDCS-22200862.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Gavius Priscus and Scribonius Restitutus, heirs, made this for their deserving kinsman, Gaius Coponius Crescens, son of Crescens, a decurion of the Tuscans, quaestor of public affairs, veteran of the Emperor's 14th Gemina legion, standard-bearer, who lived 50 years. He deserved well.
EDCS-22200862.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Gaius Coponius Crescens, a decurion and former military man, made by his heirs.

EDCS-22200865.translation: To the departed spirits. His son made this for his dear and deserving father, Lucius Numisius Proculus, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe, who served as a duumvir with judicial power, was designated quinquennial, and had fulfilled his duties, who lived 70 years.
EDCS-22200865.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Numisius Proculus, a former magistrate, erected by his son.

EDCS-22200866.translation: Sextus Scantius, son of Sextus, a duumvir, at his own expense.
EDCS-22200866.summary: This inscription records Sextus Scantius, a duumvir, who financed a public work at his own expense.

EDCS-22200872.translation: To the departed spirits. He made this for his deserving wife, Aelius Secundus, who lived 32 years.
EDCS-22200872.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelius Secundus, set up by her husband.

EDCS-22200875.translation: Calia Annia, daughter of Lucius, lived 30 years.
EDCS-22200875.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph for Calia Annia, daughter of Lucius.

EDCS-22200876.translation: Asclepiodotus and Filumene, sons, lived 9 years, 8 months, 7 days, and 6 years, 9 months, 5 days respectively.
EDCS-22200876.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for two young brothers, Asclepiodotus and Filumene, mentioning their ages at death.

EDCS-22200877.translation: Lucius Atinius, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe, aged 50.
EDCS-22200877.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph for Lucius Atinius, son of Lucius, mentioning his age and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-22200879.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Sextus Campanus.

EDCS-22200883.translation: Gaius Septimius Eutychus made this for his wife Cornelia Arescusa, who lived 35 years, as a most excellent and deserving wife, and for himself and his descendants.
EDCS-22200883.summary: A funerary monument erected by Gaius Septimius Eutychus for his wife Cornelia Arescusa and their descendants.

EDCS-22200888.translation: To the Divine Shades. Baebia Anthis and Tiberius Claudius Hermes made this for their adopted daughter Lacaena, who lived 1 year and 6 months, a well-deserving child.
EDCS-22200888.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lacaena, paid for by her adoptive parents Baebia Anthis and Tiberius Claudius Hermes.

EDCS-22200889.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Licinia Minucius.

EDCS-22200895.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Rufatius Saturninus made this for his deserving son Lucius Rufatius Caecenninus, who lived 14 years, and for himself.
EDCS-22200895.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Lucius Rufatius Saturninus to his son Lucius Rufatius Caecenninus.

EDCS-22200896.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Gaius Terentius Clemens.

EDCS-22200897.translation: Publius Tettius Menocrates and Tettia Arethusa, parents, for Publius Tettius Philonis, son of Publius.
EDCS-22200897.summary: A funerary dedication by parents Publius Tettius Menocrates and Tettia Arethusa for their son Publius Tettius Philonis.

EDCS-22200899.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Sextus Arm[---].

EDCS-22200902.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-22200902.translation: The place of Ianuarius and Victories.
EDCS-22200902.summary: An inscription possibly marking a burial plot or location belonging to Ianuarius and Victories.

EDCS-22200903.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-22200903.translation: Marcellinus the father made this for his deserving son, against a vow, a double burial; for his innocent daughter Iunia Marcelina, who lived 13 years and 3 months.
EDCS-22200903.summary: A funerary monument erected by Marcellinus for his son and daughter, Iunia Marcelina.

EDCS-22200906.translation: Sacred to the household gods of Augustus. Lucius A[---]ius Clemens and Quadratus, sons of Caius, of the Stellatina tribe, made and paid for this and dedicated it themselves. For the dedication, they also provided a feast for the villagers.
EDCS-22200906.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a sacred monument to the Laribus Augustiis by two individuals, Lucius A[---]ius Clemens and Quadratus, who also provided a celebratory feast for the local community.

EDCS-22300168.translation: To Publius Tullius Varro, his most excellent father, son of Publius Tullius [Varro?], of the Stellatina tribe, who was a member of the board of fifteen men judging legal disputes, military tribune of the Eighth August Legion (served twice), urban quaestor, proquaestor of the province of Crete and Cyrene, plebeian aedile, praetor, legate of the divine Vespasian of the Thirteenth Gemini Legion, proconsul of the province of Macedonia.
EDCS-22300168.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a son to his father, Publius Tullius Varro, detailing his extensive list of distinguished public offices and military service, suggesting a date in the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-22300170.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Mettio Feroci evocato Augusti Briseis Caesaris fecit\n
EDCS-22300170.translation: To the departed spirits. Briseis, wife of Caesar, made this for Quintus Mettius Ferox, an evocatus (recallable soldier) of Augustus.
EDCS-22300170.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Briseis, wife of Caesar, dedicated this monument to Quintus Mettius Ferox, a former soldier.

EDCS-22300171.translation: To the departed spirits. Ulpius Protogenes, freedman, made this for his patron Marcus Ulpius Sporus, son of Marcus, a doctor of the horse-borne auxiliary units Indianae and Tertiae Astorum, and a salariarius (salaried official) of the most splendid community of the Ferentines.
EDCS-22300171.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a freedman to his patron, Marcus Ulpius Sporus, who was a physician serving in auxiliary cavalry units and a financial official for the community of Ferentines.

EDCS-22300173.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Marcellus, son of Elainus, who was pontiff with the right to declare law for the Sorrinenses Novenses, quaestor of the public treasury, patron of the college of artisans and of the centonarii (firefighters/decorators). The most splendid council, recognizing his merits, first resolved to erect a statue to him from their own funds. For its dedication, he gave bread, wine, and ten denarii to the decurions. Having increased this out of respect for the honor offered him, he gave five thousand sesterces to the people in perpetuity for the grain supply. The site was granted by decree of the decurions, with the care managed by Titus Varius Severus.
EDCS-22300173.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue in honor of Marcus Aurelius Marcellus, a local magistrate and patron, who was celebrated for his public service and subsequently made a significant donation to the community's grain supply.

EDCS-22300177.translation: To Severus, son of Caius, of the Stellatina tribe. He served twice as a praefectus fabrum (commander of engineers), twice equo publico (on public horses), and as a quindecemvir iure dicundo quinquennalis (member of a board for five-year judicial terms). When the decurions, with the consent of the Augustales and the plebs, offered him statues in bronze due to his singular integrity, he himself ordered them to be erected, remitting the cost of the contribution. For their dedication, his heirs, according to his will, gave four denarii to each decurion, three denarii to each Augustalis, and two denarii to each resident plebeian within the walls. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22300177.summary: This inscription honors Severus, a distinguished local official, who, despite being offered statues for his integrity, chose to fund their erection himself and provided generous donations to various social groups upon their dedication, as stipulated in his will.

EDCS-22300178.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-22300178.translation: To Lucius Valerius Tullius Tuscianus, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe, Roman knight, priest of Lanuvium. The Sorrinenses Novenses [dedicate this] to their patron, because he contributed the work of the market [building?] with its grounds and all maintenance to their republic, and for its dedication, he honored all the inhabitants. Care was managed by Tiberius Rusticus Rufus.
EDCS-22300178.summary: This dedication by the community of Sorrinenses Novenses honors their patron, Lucius Valerius Tullius Tuscianus, a Roman knight and priest, for his generous contribution to the construction and maintenance of the public market.

EDCS-22300186.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Caecilius Primus. Euterpe, his wife, [made this] for her husband who deserved well of her.
EDCS-22300186.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a wife, Euterpe, to her deserving husband, Marcus Caecilius Primus.

EDCS-22300188.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Sex. Gavi Proculus, son of Sex. He lived for 16 years and was honored with the public equestrian status.
EDCS-22300188.summary: This funerary inscription records the early death of Sex. Gavi Proculus at the age of sixteen, noting that he had attained the status of an eques (knight).

EDCS-22300189.translation: To the departed spirits. From Valeria Marcellina, wife of Caius Julius Macr.
EDCS-22300189.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription from a wife, Valeria Marcellina, to her deceased husband, Caius Julius Macr.

EDCS-22300201.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tullius Metiochus, his most beloved son, sadly erected this for Tullius Onesimus, who lived 11 years, 5 months, and 24 days.
EDCS-22300201.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy named Onesimus, erected by his father Tullius Metiochus.

EDCS-22300204.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-22300204.translation: To Agrippa Iulius, son of Augustus, grandson of the deified [Caesar]. To Tiberius Iulius, son of Augustus, grandson of the deified [Caesar], pontiff, twice consul, twice imperator, with tribunician power for the sixth time. To [Gaius] Caesar, son of Augustus, grandson of the deified [Caesar], pontiff, designated consul, prince of the youth. To Imperator Caesar, son of the deified [Augustus], chief pontiff, consul for the twelfth time, with tribunician power for the twentieth time, imperator for the fourteenth time. To Lucius Caesar, son of Augustus, grandson of the deified [Caesar]. Publius Sergius Rufus, son of Publius, and Titus Braetius Rufus, son of Titus, as magistrates for the second time, have restored the temple and statues with their own money for the pagus of Stellatini.
EDCS-22300204.summary: This is a dedication by two magistrates for the pagus of Stellatini, commemorating several members of the imperial family, likely from the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-22300214.translation: Quintus Vibius Rufus, son of Quintus, of the Arnensis tribe. Vibia Arbuscula, freedwoman of Quintus. Quintus Vibius Faustus, freedman of Quintus.
EDCS-22300214.summary: This inscription lists three individuals, possibly family members, with their names, patronymics, tribal affiliations, and status as freedpeople.

EDCS-22300224.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Of Marcus Aurelius Hilarianus, freedman of the Augusti, an excellent man.
EDCS-22300224.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Hilarianus, a freedman of the emperors.

EDCS-22300228.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Munatius Polibianus and Gaius Munatius Polibius erected this for their most pious parents, Gaius Munatius Hermetus and Numisia Tycheni.
EDCS-22300228.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by two sons for their parents.

EDCS-22300240.text_edition: \n Fortunae imperio aram posuit Selia et aedem dedicavit\n
EDCS-22300240.translation: Selia placed an altar to the command of Fortuna and dedicated the temple.
EDCS-22300240.summary: This is a dedication of an altar and temple to the goddess Fortuna by a woman named Selia.

EDCS-22300248.translation: For the honor of the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified [Augustus], chief pontiff, father of the country, and of the municipality. Gaius Egnatius Glyco, freedman of Marcus, Gaius Egnatius Mus, freedman of Gaius, Gaius Iulius Isochrysus, freedman of Caesar, and Quintus Floronius Princeps, freedman of Quintus, as Augustales, have paved the Via Augusta from the Via Annia outside the gate to Ceres with stone at their own expense, for the games.
EDCS-22300248.summary: This is a public works dedication by Augustales, a group of freedmen honoring the emperor, to pave a road in honor of the emperor and municipality, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22300257.translation: To Publius Licinius Cornelius Saloninus Valerianus, the most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, from the senate and people of the colony of Falerii.
EDCS-22300257.summary: This is a dedication to Publius Licinius Cornelius Saloninus Valerianus, a young imperial prince, by the colony of Falerii.

EDCS-22300261.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Maximila Agnatia placed this for her excellent husband, Publius Glitius, tribune of the soldiers of the legion [X], praetor, IIIvir capitalis, OP VEHIS P Q.
EDCS-22300261.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Glitius, a military officer and magistrate, erected by his wife.

EDCS-22300265.translation: Marcus Pontius [---]tius, military tribune of the III Cyrenaica legion, prefect of the IIII Raetorum cavalry cohort, prefect of the II Ituraeorum cohort, prefect of the fabra twice, scribe of the curule aediles, having held the honor, scribe librarian of the curule aediles twice, erected this for himself and his family.
EDCS-22300265.summary: This is a funerary epitaph and career summary for Marcus Pontius [---]tius, detailing his military and civil offices, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22300266.translation: To Lucius Tutilius Lupercus, son of Sulpicius Avitus.
EDCS-22300266.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Tutilius Lupercus, son of Sulpicius Avitus, likely to a deity.

EDCS-22300272.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-22300272.translation: To Quintus Albius Felix, son of Quintus, of the Horatia tribe, centurion of the 20th Valeria Victrix legion, decorated with gifts by the divine Trajan Augustus with torques, armillae, and phalerae in the Parthian war, and by the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus with a hasta pura and a golden crown; by Avillia Soteris, his mother, for her most excellent and most dutiful son. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22300272.summary: This is a funerary monument for Quintus Albius Felix, a decorated centurion, erected by his mother, likely dating to the reign of Hadrian.

EDCS-22300273.translation: Titus Lucilius Olianus, son of Stator, centurion, father, by will and by the arbitration of Titus Thor[...].
EDCS-22300273.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument for Titus Lucilius Olianus, a centurion and father, established by his will and testament.

EDCS-22300274.translation: Lucius Numisius Viator, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, from Eporedia, an evocatus Augusti, saw to its placement by his will.
EDCS-22300274.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument for Lucius Numisius Viator, an evocatus Augusti, arranged by his will.

EDCS-22300280.text_edition: \n Senatus municipesque Caio Aconio Cai filio Porru sic IIIIviro iure dicundo senatus consulto pontifici praefecto perpetuo\n
EDCS-22300280.translation: The Senate and the townspeople, by decree of the Senate, to Gaius Aconius Porrus, son of Gaius, former holder of the office of quaestor, perpetual pontiff, prefect, and twice holder of the office of IIIIvir iure dicundo.
EDCS-22300280.summary: This is an honorary inscription from the Senate and townspeople honoring Gaius Aconius Porrus for his various public offices, including IIIIvir iure dicundo and pontiff.

EDCS-22300283.text_edition: \n Caius Aconius Luci filius Porrus quaestor Xvir senatus consulto pro IIIIviro\n
EDCS-22300283.translation: Gaius Aconius Porrus, son of Lucius, quaestor, Xvir, by decree of the Senate, for the office of IIIIvir.
EDCS-22300283.summary: This honorary inscription records Gaius Aconius Porrus's public service as quaestor, Xvir, and his designation for the IIIIvir office by senatorial decree.

EDCS-22300285.translation: The townspeople and inhabitants to Publius Annianus Senicio, son of Publius, Xvir, [...]
EDCS-22300285.summary: This is an honorary inscription by the townspeople and inhabitants for Publius Annianus Senicio, a Xvir.

EDCS-22300287.translation: Gaius Julius Severus, son of Gaius, of the Horatia tribe, priest of Isis and the Mother of the Gods, quaestor of the grain supply for Caesar, quaestor of the public treasury, curator of the youth, IIIIvir aedilis, IIIIvir iure dicundo, IIIIvir quinquennalis, here, on account of the honor of the aedileship, restored this portico which had collapsed from age, towards which he donated 27,300 sesterces.
EDCS-22300287.summary: This is a public benefaction inscription by Gaius Julius Severus, a prominent local official, who restored a portico at his own expense.

EDCS-22300288.translation: Gaius Mammius, son of Gaius, [...], IIIIvir iure dicundo by decree of the Senate, pontifex [...], temples, porticoes which [...], and ornaments [...]
EDCS-22300288.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Mammius for his service as IIIIvir iure dicundo and pontifex, and mentions his contributions to public buildings and ornaments.

EDCS-22300292.translation: To Gaius Artin[...], Lucius Plotidius [...], aged [...], priest (?) of Juno (?).
EDCS-22300292.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely honoring a priest, possibly of Juno, for individuals named Gaius Artin[...] and Lucius Plotidius.

EDCS-22300328.translation: To Quintus Fulvius Charetus, banker and tax collector from the upper wine port, as a most excellent and most indulgent patron, by his freedmen Doctus and Festus.
EDCS-22300328.summary: This is a dedication by freedmen to their patron, a banker and tax collector, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22300346.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Isias, to my most excellent wife. Primigenius Vestinianus, slave of Emperor Augustus, lived 15 years.
EDCS-22300346.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Isias, who died at age 15, set up by her husband, a slave of the emperor, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22300362.translation: Gaius Villius Plocamus, freedman of Gaius, made this while alive for himself and for Villia Tertia, freedwoman of Gaius, his co-freedwoman, and for Arruntia Erotis, freedwoman of [a woman], and for Antonia Pieris, freedwoman of Publius.
EDCS-22300362.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Gaius Villius Plocamus for himself and several other individuals, including his co-freedwoman, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22300373.text_edition: \n Compotenti Dianae Publius Mummaeius sic Atticus et Mummeia Felicla dono dederunt\n
EDCS-22300373.translation: Publius Mummaeus Atticus and Mummeia Felicia have given this gift to Diana Compotentia.
EDCS-22300373.summary: A dedication to the goddess Diana by Publius Mummaeus Atticus and Mummeia Felicia, probably from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22300374.translation: Hermeros, slave of Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, the Theamidian, master craftsman, has given five altars to Feronia as a gift from his own funds.
EDCS-22300374.summary: A dedication by Hermeros, a slave of Emperor Claudius and a master craftsman, of five altars to the goddess Feronia, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-22400113.translation: To Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine [Julius Caesar], pontifex maximus, consul for the 11th time, holding tribunician power for the 11th time. The chief Augustales: Philippus, freedman of Augustus; Marcus Aebutius Secundus; Marcus Gallius Anchialus; Publius Fidustius Antigonus.
EDCS-22400113.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Augustus and lists the names of the chief Augustales, officials responsible for the imperial cult, likely from the period of Augustus' reign (27 BCE - 14 CE).

EDCS-22400118.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Propinquus, most distinguished man, to his dearest wife, Velia Pumidia, most distinguished woman.
EDCS-22400118.summary: A funerary epitaph for Velia Pumidia, dedicated by her husband Aurelius Propinquus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22400121.translation: To the departed spirits. To Quintus Petronius Urbanus, centurion of the Praetorian Guard, a most rare husband, and to Quintus Petronius Pertinax, [his] son.
EDCS-22400121.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Petronius Urbanus, a Praetorian centurion, and his son, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22400123.translation: To the departed spirits. To Quintus Albius Felix, son of Quintus of the Horatia tribe, who lived 19 years and 8 months, most dutiful towards his parents, whom cruel fate suddenly snatched away. [Quintus] Albius Felix, father, made [this] for Quintus Albius Felix, son of Quintus of the Horatia tribe, [lost]... master of the college of lint-makers which is located in Faleri. Quintus Albius [son of Quintus...] lived [...].
EDCS-22400123.summary: A complex funerary monument commemorating Quintus Albius Felix, who died young, set up by his father, who also names himself as master of a college in Faleri, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22400125.translation: To Gnaeus Corellius Frontinus, duovir for the fourth time, aedilician duovir with power of jurisdiction, quaestor of the emperor's grain supply. The people of Nepete, from a collected fund, because of his generosity, to whom his wife Otacilia Comice erected a statue, gave sportulas to the decurions and Augustales, and a feast to the people. The place was assigned by decree of the decurions, under the care of Lucius Turpilius Felix.
EDCS-22400125.summary: A dedication by the people of Nepete to Gnaeus Corellius Frontinus, a local magistrate and benefactor, who also provided for a feast and gifts, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22400129.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Sulpicius Severus and Sulpicia Iustina erected this for their father Lucius Sulpicius Clemens, who served as IVvir iure dicundo (with twofold authority), IVvir with aedilic power, quaestor of the treasury of the Nepesine republic, magistrate of youth, sevir of knights, praetor of youth, and an excellent father.
EDCS-22400129.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by children for their father, who held several important municipal offices in Nepesia.

EDCS-22400144.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Gaius Fulvius Lunensis, son, made this for his most dutiful father, Gaius Fulvius Lunensis, a primipilaris.
EDCS-22400144.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a son for his father, a primipilaris (a senior centurion).

EDCS-22400149.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For their most dutiful son, who lived 18 years, 3 months, and 22 days. Nasia Lycoris, his most unhappy mother.
EDCS-22400149.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her son who died at a young age.

EDCS-22400158.translation: Decimus Rupilius, son of Lucius, [---] willingly [and deservedly] fulfilled his vow to the Bona Dea Regina Triumphalis.
EDCS-22400158.summary: This is an inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to the goddess Bona Dea by a man whose name is partially lost.

EDCS-22400162.translation: Quintus Hortensius Hymnus willingly and with a glad heart fulfilled his vow to the Divine Nymphs, having been restored to health, and Cascellia Arethusa, with the permission of Titus Latinus Pandusa.
EDCS-22400162.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of vows to the Divine Nymphs by two individuals, one of whom was granted permission by another person.

EDCS-22400164.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, designated consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, by decree of the decurions and the consent of the people.
EDCS-22400164.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his titles and honors granted by local authorities.

EDCS-22400165.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, son of the deified Severus Pius, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, by decree of the decurions and the consent of the people.
EDCS-22400165.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Emperor Marcus Aurelius, detailing his extensive imperial titles and honors conferred by local officials.

EDCS-22400169.translation: The pontifices from the colony Iulia Sutrina, having been recorded in order: Gaius Vergilius Strabo, father; Publius Aemilius Valens, son of Publius; Publius Matrinius Sassula, father; Publius Portumius Gallus, son of Publius; Lucius Volturnius Gallus, son of Lucius; Lucius Rustius, son of Lucius; Lucius Lucretius Apulus, son of Caius; Quintus Herennius Labeo; Aulus Catius; Lucius Herennius, son of Caius; Publius Selius, son of Publius; Marcus Valerius Longus; Marcus Occius Ruso; Publius Alburius Mutus, son of Publius; Publius Matrinius Sassula; Lucius Pontius, son of Publius; Lucius Vibius, son of Publius; Quintus Mettius, son of Publius, in the place of Quintus Vibius Rosae; Lucius Clavius Macer, in the place of Quintus Mettius, son of Publius; Publius Siccius, son of Titus, in the place of Quintus Fulvius; Marcus Fabius Sabinus, in the place of Lucius Vibius, son of Publius; Marcus Vesidius Rufus, in the place of Sextus Praesens; Lucius Matrinius Miluvo; Lucius Pontius Aquila, in the place of Publius Cluatius, son of Lucius; Publius Sucius Algidienus, son of Publius, in the place of Lucius Horatius, son of Lucius; Caius Fabius Priscus, son of Caius, in the place of Caius Caristius Fronto; Quintus Titienus Bae[---], in the place of Caius Caristan[---]; Publius Umbricius Rufus, in the place of Lucius Plotius Cipoll[---]; Quintus Flavius Pollius, son of Quintus, in the place of Marcus Fabius, son of Caius [---]; Marcus Valerius Ferox, in the place of Lucius Farsullius Rufi; Lucius Octavius Tiro, in the place of Marcus Valerius Ferocis; Marcus Aponius Celsus, in the place of Publius Umbricius Rufus; Marcus Cattius Marcellus, in the place of Publius Calventius Iustus; Gnaeus Manlius Sacratus, in the place of Marcus Cattius Marcell[---]; Lucius Cornelius Priscus, in the place of Caius Cornelius Sicci.
EDCS-22400169.summary: This is a list of pontifices (priests) from the colony of Iulia Sutrina, with many names recorded as replacements for others, suggesting a significant administrative or religious body.

EDCS-22400172.translation: For Titus Egnatius Rufus, son of Titus, of the Voturia tribe, who served as quaestor, aedile, dictator, and aedile of Etruria. Egnatia Compse, freedwoman of Titus, made this for herself and her family. Titus Egnatius Rufus, son of Titus, lived for 2 years and 15 days.
EDCS-22400172.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Egnatius Rufus, a local magistrate, and a smaller inscription records the lifespan of a young Titus Egnatius Rufus, possibly his son.

EDCS-22400173.translation: By decree of the decurions and the people, a bronze statue was ordered to be erected to Caius Lepidius Victor, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, who served as IIvir iure dicundo, curator of public funds and public works, in honor of his meritorious service in repairing them. However, content with the honor, he erected it at his own expense and returned the contributions.
EDCS-22400173.summary: This inscription records that local officials voted to erect a statue to Caius Lepidius Victor for his service in repairing public works, but he chose to fund it himself and returned the collected money.

EDCS-22400176.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Patulcia Exoche, his wife, made this for Titus Valerius Victor, son of Titus, of the Velina tribe, a councilor of Sutrium, twice a second-rank magistrate with jurisdiction, a quinquennial official, curator of public funds, and a priest. She was his wife.
EDCS-22400176.summary: A funerary monument set up by a wife for her deceased husband, who held various civic and religious offices in Sutrium.

EDCS-22500177.translation: In the consulship of Tiberius Caesar for the third time and Germanicus Caesar for the second time. In the second consulship of Gnaeus Acceius Rufus, son of Gnaeus, of the Arnensis tribe, and Titus Lutatius Petillius, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe. Decrees: The aediculam and these statues were decreed for the sacrifice at the dedication, a victim on the birthday of Augustus on the 8th day before the Kalends of October. Two animals which have been accustomed to be sacrificed perpetually are to be sacrificed on the Kalends of October at the altar dedicated to the Divine Augustus. Likewise, on the birthday of Tiberius Caesar, perpetually acting councilors and the people were to dine, a cost that Quintus Cascellius Labeo promised to provide perpetually, in order that thanks be given for his generosity, and on that birthday, a bull was to be sacrificed annually. And so that on the birthdays of Augustus and Tiberius Caesar, before the councilors went to dine, their Genii were to be invited to the feast by incense and wine at the altar of the Divine Augustus. We took care to have the altar to the Divine Augustus made with our money. We also took care to have games held for six days from the Ides of August with our money. On the birthday of Augusta, we gave honeyed wine and small cakes to the women of the village at the Bona Dea with our money. Likewise, at the dedication of the statues of the Caesars and Augusta, we gave honeyed wine and small cakes to the councilors and the people in perpetuity, and we declared that we would provide them on the day of dedication, which day we will observe because it is more frequented each year, the 6th day before the Ides of March, on which day Tiberius Caesar, the pontifex maximus, was most happily created.
EDCS-22500177.summary: A decree outlining public religious observances, sacrifices, and feasts, funded by a benefactor, to commemorate imperial birthdays and events, likely in a municipium, with specific dates mentioned relating to Tiberius Caesar's pontificate and Augustus' birthday.

EDCS-22500188.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Ulpius Severinus, a cavalryman from the imperial escort, who lived [3] years. Lucius Ulpius Ursins, to his dearest brother, a veteran [?] of the Augustan [camps?].
EDCS-22500188.summary: A funerary monument for a cavalryman, likely from the imperial escort, set up by his brother, probably in the Roman army context.

EDCS-22500200.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Eutycia [Cos---] to her most sweet husband, Aulus Bibius Valerianus, who deserved well of her, who lived 70 years, 5 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-22500200.summary: A funerary epitaph for a devoted wife to her well-deserving husband, specifying his age at death.

EDCS-22500201.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To my most holy and most sweet and incomparable master, her husband, who deserved well of her, [---]citus made this.
EDCS-22500201.summary: A funerary monument erected by a wife to her deceased husband, described with strong terms of affection and respect.

EDCS-22500202.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Ponticus, freedman of the woman Caius Agrilius. Furia Calliope, to her most excellent husband.
EDCS-22500202.summary: A funerary epitaph for a husband, set up by his wife, identifying the husband as the freedman of a woman named Caius Agrilius.

EDCS-22500203.translation: Caius Cincius Faustus made this for himself and for Aelia Egloge, freedwoman of Marcus.
EDCS-22500203.summary: A funerary monument intended for the dedicator and his freedwoman partner, Aelia Egloge.

EDCS-22500204.translation: Curtia Helena, freedwoman of Postumus, to Theocrita, daughter of Perseus, her mother and father.
EDCS-22500204.summary: An epitaph where Curtia Helena, a freedwoman, commemorates her mother and father.

EDCS-22500211.translation: To Nero Caesar, son of Germanicus, grandson of Tiberius Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Augustus, imperial high priest, Augustan sodalist, quaestor. Quintus Novanius Salvius, son of Quintus, freedman. Gaius Culmilius Rufus, son of Gaius, freedman. Lucius Fulvius Docimus, son of Lucius, freedman. Lucius Furius Rectus, son of Lucius, freedman. Lucius Popillius Apollonius, son of Lucius, freedman. Lucius Furius Gemellus, son of Lucius, freedman. The six Augustan officials.
EDCS-22500211.summary: An inscription honoring Nero Caesar, detailing his lineage and imperial titles, with a list of six Augustales officials, likely associated with a public monument or dedication.

EDCS-22500212.translation: To Gaius Julius Julianus, son of Gaius, of the Palatina tribe, praetor, quaestor of the province of Baetica, admitted by the deified Hadrian among the aedilician officials, prefect of cavalry, tribune of soldiers, prefect of the fourth cohort, quinquennial official with jurisdiction in the municipium. [---]
EDCS-22500212.summary: An honorific inscription for Gaius Julius Julianus, detailing his distinguished career in civic and military service, including promotions and appointments under Hadrian.

EDCS-22500228.translation: To the divine shades. To Voconia Flora, Voconius Capito [erected this].
EDCS-22500228.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up for Voconia Flora by Voconius Capito, likely a relative.

EDCS-22500229.translation: To the divine shades. Volumnia Priscilla lived for 38 years, 5 months, and 5 days. Her parents Volumnia Euresis and Veturius Adventus [erected this].
EDCS-22500229.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Volumnia Priscilla, detailing her age at death and erected by her parents.

EDCS-22500231.translation: [Erected by a] herald. [To his] wife... for me... his guest... and forever... L... na.
EDCS-22500231.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription likely for a wife, set up by her husband and possibly mentioning a guest and a herald.

EDCS-22500239.text_edition: \n Publio Tullio Varronis filio Stellatina Varroni consuli auguri proconsuli provinciae Africae legato Augusti pro praetore Moesiae superioris curatori alvei Tiberis et riparum et cloacarum urbis praefecto aerari Saturnini proconsulis provinciae Baeticae ulterioris Hispaniae legato legionis XII fulminatae et VI victricis piae fidelis praetori aedili Ceriali quaestori urbis tribuno militum legionis XVI Flaviae Xviro stlitibus iudicandis praetori Etruriae quinquennali Tarquinis Publius Tullius Callistio posuit\n
EDCS-22500239.translation: Publius Tullius Callistio erected this for Publius Tullius Varro, son of Varro, of the Stellatina tribe, consul, augur, proconsul of the province of Africa, legate of the emperor with praetorian power in Upper Moesia, curator of the Tiber river, its banks, and the city's sewers, prefect of the treasury of Saturninus, proconsul of the province of Further Baetica in Hispania, legate of the XII Fulminata and VI Victrix legions (pious and faithful), praetor, aedile, Cerialis, quaestor of the city, military tribune of the XVI Flavia army, decemvir for judging lawsuits, quinquennial praetor of Etruria in Tarquinia.
EDCS-22500239.summary: This is a public dedication commemorating the extensive career of Publius Tullius Varro, listing his many prestigious offices and military commands, erected by Publius Tullius Callistio.

EDCS-22500240.text_edition: \n Lucio Dasumio Publi filio Stellatina Tullio Tusco consuli comiti Augusti auguri sodali Hadrianali sodali Antoniniano curatori operum publicorum legato pro praetore provinciarum Germaniae superioris et Pannoniae superioris praefecto aerarii Saturni praetori tribuno plebis legato provinciae Africae quaestori Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Pii tribuno militum legionis IIII Flaviae triumviro aere argento auro flando feriundo Publius Tullius Callistio posuit\n
EDCS-22500240.translation: Publius Tullius Callistio erected this for Lucius Dasumius Tuscus, son of Publius, of the Stellatina tribe, consul, companion of the emperor, augur, member of the Hadrianic college, member of the Antonine college, curator of public works, legate with proconsular authority of the provinces of Upper Germania and Upper Pannonia, prefect of the treasury of Saturn, praetor, tribune of the plebs, legate of the province of Africa, quaestor of Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, military tribune of the IIII Flavia army, triumvir for striking bronze, silver, and gold.
EDCS-22500240.summary: This is a public dedication honoring Lucius Dasumius Tuscus and enumerating his distinguished career in military, administrative, and religious roles, erected by Publius Tullius Callistio.

EDCS-22500242.translation: The council and citizens of Tarquinia and Gravisca [erected this] for Quintus Petronius Mela, a consular man, curator of the republic of Tarquinia and Gravisca, prefect of the grain dole, legate of the XXX Ulpia legion, curator of Pyrgi and Cereti, legate of the VII Augusta legion, praetor, tribune of the plebs, candidate for office, quaestor of the province of Narbonne, quaestor, member of the Augustan Claudian college, a Sexvir of the first cavalry turma, tribune with laticlavus of the I Minervia legion, decemvir for judging lawsuits, their excellent patron, because he favored the republic and restored the baths.
EDCS-22500242.summary: This is a public dedication by the city of Tarquinia and Gravisca to their patron, Quintus Petronius Mela, honoring him for his public service, which included restoring the baths.

EDCS-22500243.translation: For Domitia Melpis, a most distinguished woman, wife of Quintus Petronius Mela, a consular man. The council and citizens of Tarquinia [erected this] for their most worthy patroness.
EDCS-22500243.summary: This is a public dedication by the city of Tarquinia to Domitia Melpis, wife of Quintus Petronius Mela, honoring her as a distinguished patroness.

EDCS-22500244.translation: To the divine shades. To Titus Iulius Valens, a veteran of the V Macedonica legion, who served for 26 years. He lived for 50 years. Titus Iulius Fronto, his dearest brother, and Iulia Frontina and Iulia Valentina [erected this] for their most dutiful father.
EDCS-22500244.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Iulius Valens, a veteran of the V Macedonica legion, erected by his brother and daughters.

EDCS-22500246.translation: Aurelia, daughter of Lucius, an excellent woman, lived for 45 years. Lucius Tercenna, son of Publius, a magistrate for four years, [served as] flamen for four years.
EDCS-22500246.summary: This inscription records the death of Aurelia and provides information about the public service of Lucius Tercenna, likely her father or husband.

EDCS-22500248.translation: Lucius Colgius, son of Publius, magistrate for four years, lived for 59 years.
EDCS-22500248.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Colgius, a magistrate of his community, stating his age at death.

EDCS-22500249.translation: Cossutius, son of Publius, served as quaestor four times and held judicial office; he lived 74 years. Vibia Quarta, daughter of Gaius, wife of Cossutius, lived 56 years.
EDCS-22500249.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a couple, Cossutius and Vibia Quarta, dating to the Roman period, likely from a town where the quaestor held significant local power.

EDCS-22500250.translation: Publius Lotius Severus, son of Publius, served as quaestor four times and held judicial office; he was given a public funeral by decree of the decurions. He lived 58 years.
EDCS-22500250.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and burial of Publius Lotius Severus, a local magistrate, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500251.translation: Lucius Lucilius Celsus, son of Lucius, served as quaestor four times; he lived 67 years.
EDCS-22500251.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Lucius Lucilius Celsus, a local magistrate, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-22500252.translation: Marcus Mela, son of Quintus, served as quaestor four times and held judicial office; he lived 75 years.
EDCS-22500252.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Marcus Mela, a local magistrate, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-22500253.translation: Publius Octavius Albanus, quaestor, paid for the columns from his own funds.
EDCS-22500253.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a public building project, likely funded by Publius Octavius Albanus in his role as quaestor, during the Roman period.

EDCS-22500254.translation: Lucius Papirius Cognitus, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe, prefect of the engineers, served as quaestor four times and held judicial office; he was given a public funeral by decree of the decurions. He lived 41 years.
EDCS-22500254.summary: This funerary epitaph honors Lucius Papirius Cognitus, a man who held multiple local magistracies and military engineering roles, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500256.translation: Gaius Pilius Rufus, son of Gaius, of the Stellatina tribe, served as quaestor four times; he lived 57 years.
EDCS-22500256.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Gaius Pilius Rufus, a local magistrate, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-22500257.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Sevius Clementis, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe, an aruspex (diviner by entrails), lived 30 years. From the college of haruspices, 60 years. Curator of the treasury, twice quaestor, and also aedile. Gaius Sevius Celsus, to his dearest brother, by decree of the decurions, a public funeral.
EDCS-22500257.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Lucius Sevius Clementis, a haruspex who also held civic offices, and his brother Gaius Sevius Celsus arranged his public burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500258.translation: Publius Tuccius Postumus, son of Quintus, served as quaestor four times and held judicial office for a second time; he lived 75 years.
EDCS-22500258.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Publius Tuccius Postumus, a local magistrate who served multiple terms, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-22500259.translation: Gaius Vetilius, son of Quintus, served as quaestor four times and held judicial office, and as quinquennial magistrate; he paved [a road] from the crossroads for 10,000 paces at his own expense, and [paved] 3,000 paces with 200,000 sesterces which Gaius Coelius Valens, son of Gaius, [---] for the paving of the road from the Roman Gate to [---] which remained...
EDCS-22500259.summary: This inscription details public works funded by Gaius Vetilius, a local magistrate and quinquennial official, and Gaius Coelius Valens, concerning road construction, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500261.text_edition: \n Sextus Vettius Auli filius Stellatina Rufus vixit annos XLIV IIIIvir tribunus militum \n
EDCS-22500261.translation: Sextus Vettius Rufus, son of Aulus, of the Stellatina tribe, lived 44 years; he served as a quaestor and military tribune.
EDCS-22500261.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sextus Vettius Rufus, recording his lifespan and magistracies, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500262.translation: Aulus Volumnius Proculus lived 45 years; he served as a quaestor with judicial authority.
EDCS-22500262.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aulus Volumnius Proculus, noting his lifespan and his role as a magistrate, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500267.translation: To Lucius Papinius Lacer, son of Lucius, priest, lived 63 years.
EDCS-22500267.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Papinius Lacer, recording his lifespan and his role as a haruspex (diviner), likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500269.translation: Lucius Caesennius Sextus, freedman of Lucius, lived 67 years; he was a piper.
EDCS-22500269.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Caesennius Sextus, noting his lifespan and his profession as a piper, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500270.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Aburius Achoristus, recording his lifespan, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500271.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Acelius Capito, recording his lifespan, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500273.translation: To the departed spirits. Terentia Gemina made this for Publius Aelius Proculus, the virginwood.
EDCS-22500273.summary: A funerary dedication set up by Terentia Gemina for Publius Aelius Proculus, possibly indicating a relationship or a particular role of the deceased.

EDCS-22500276.translation: Publius Alina, son of Lucius, lived 65 years.
EDCS-22500276.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Alina, son of Lucius, recording his lifespan, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500608.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Alina Proculus, recording his short lifespan, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500621.translation: Caius Caenius Rufus, son of Caius, lived 57 years.
EDCS-22500621.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Caenius Rufus, recording his lifespan, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500623.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a woman named Caesennia, recording her age at death.

EDCS-22500624.translation: Caesennia Albana, daughter of Spurius, lived 22 years.
EDCS-22500624.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a woman named Caesennia Albana, recording her age at death.

EDCS-22500631.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Colgius Silvinus, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe, aged 5[?]; Colg[ia ... daughter of ...], Maxi[mi]lla.
EDCS-22500631.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely for Lucius Colgius Silvinus, with a damaged portion potentially naming another individual.

EDCS-22500633.translation: Cornelia Felix, freedwoman of the woman, lived 60 years.
EDCS-22500633.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Felix, a freedwoman, recording her age at death.

EDCS-22500642.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Felix, slave of our Caesar, lived 30 years. Satyrus, slave of our Caesar, made this for his dearest brother.
EDCS-22500642.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Satyrus for his brother Felix, both slaves of the emperor, recording Felix's age at death.

EDCS-22500648.translation: Graecinia Tertulla, daughter of Lucius, lived 32 years.
EDCS-22500648.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Graecinia Tertulla, recording her age at death.

EDCS-22500649.translation: Lucius Herennius Speratus, freedman of the woman, lived 40 years.
EDCS-22500649.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Herennius Speratus, a freedman, recording his age at death.

EDCS-22500652.translation: Hortensia Quarta, daughter of Publius, lived 60 years.
EDCS-22500652.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hortensia Quarta, recording her age at death.

EDCS-22500655.translation: Gaius Larcius Artemidorus, freedman of Decimus, aged 19 years.
EDCS-22500655.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Larcius Artemidorus, a freedman, recording his age at death.

EDCS-22500663.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcia Severa, recording her age at death.

EDCS-22500664.translation: Memmia Pollitta, daughter of Publius, lived 9 years.
EDCS-22500664.summary: This is a tombstone for a young girl named Memmia Pollitta, who died at the age of 9.

EDCS-22500665.translation: Gnaeus Mulvius Favor, son of Favor, lived 22 years.
EDCS-22500665.summary: This is a tombstone for a man named Gnaeus Mulvius Favor, who died at the age of 22.

EDCS-22500669.translation: Lucius Oppius Veturius Gallus lived 60 years.
EDCS-22500669.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Oppius Veturius Gallus, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-22500672.translation: Lucius Papius Celsus, son of Lucius, lived 37 years.
EDCS-22500672.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Papius Celsus, who died at the age of 37.

EDCS-22500675.translation: Pilia, daughter of Aulus, lived 65 years.
EDCS-22500675.summary: This is a tombstone for Pilia, who died at the age of 65.

EDCS-22500678.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Pompeius, who died at the age of 15.

EDCS-22500687.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Trebicius Rusticelianus, son of Marcus Scribonius Nicoma, and Marcus Farranius Nicomedes, grandson.
EDCS-22500687.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Marcus Scribonius Nicoma, with dedications from his son and grandson, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500688.translation: Sepuria Prima, freedwoman of Marcus, lived 68 years.
EDCS-22500688.summary: This is a tombstone for Sepuria Prima, a freedwoman, who died at the age of 68.

EDCS-22500691.translation: Quintus Sicinius Leo, son of Quintus, lived 54 years. Allia, wife of Quintus Sicinius Leo, lived 70 years.
EDCS-22500691.summary: This is a tombstone commemorating a married couple, Quintus Sicinius Leo and Allia, who died at the ages of 54 and 70 respectively.

EDCS-22500695.summary: This is a tombstone for Spurinnia Longa, who died at the age of 75.

EDCS-22500696.translation: Spurinnia Thannia, daughter of Lucius, lived 94 years.
EDCS-22500696.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Spurinnia Thannia.

EDCS-22500701.translation: Titia Iucunda, freedwoman of Sextus, lived 14 years.
EDCS-22500701.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Titia Iucunda.

EDCS-22500705.translation: Tuccia, daughter of Sextus, lived 21 years.
EDCS-22500705.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Tuccia.

EDCS-22500714.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Thallusa erected this for her well-deserving husband, Marcus Ulpius Metisco, freedman of Augustus.
EDCS-22500714.summary: This is a funerary dedication placed by Claudia Thallusa for her husband, Marcus Ulpius Metisco.

EDCS-22500719.translation: Claudius Severianus gave this gift to Apollo the Healer.
EDCS-22500719.summary: This is a dedication of a gift to the god Apollo Sanctus by Claudius Severianus.

EDCS-22500720.translation: Quintus Cassius Ianuarius gave this gift to Apollo and the Domitian Nymphs.
EDCS-22500720.summary: This is a dedication of a gift to Apollo and the Nymphs by Quintus Cassius Ianuarius.

EDCS-22500721.translation: Gavia Rhodine gave this silver cup weighing half a pound as a gift, having undertaken a vow to Apollo the Healer and the Nymphs.
EDCS-22500721.summary: This is a votive offering of a silver cup by Gavia Rhodine to Apollo and the Nymphs.

EDCS-22500723.translation: Quintus Licinius Nepos gave this gift to Apollo Silvanus and the Nymphs.
EDCS-22500723.summary: This is a dedication of a gift to Apollo Silvanus and the Nymphs by Quintus Licinius Nepos.

EDCS-22500726.translation: To the memory of Furia Asclepiades.
EDCS-22500726.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Furia Asclepiades.

EDCS-22500728.translation: Lucius Aiatius Phoebus, decurion of the Julian board of heralds under the consul, gave this gift to Apollo Silvanus, Asclepius, and the Nymphs, having undertaken a vow, along with his son Phoebianus.
EDCS-22500728.summary: This is a votive dedication to Apollo Silvanus, Asclepius, and the Nymphs by Lucius Aiatius Phoebus and his son Phoebianus.

EDCS-22500730.translation: Sextilius Attalus Obas to Apollo, in accordance with a dream, from Aphrodisias.
EDCS-22500730.summary: This is a dedication to Apollo by Sextilius Attalus Obas, likely from Aphrodisias, who dedicated the offering in accordance with a dream.

EDCS-22600308.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of death for a man named Valens.

EDCS-22600310.translation: Lucius Veturius, son of Lucius, lived 64 years.
EDCS-22600310.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name, filiation, and age of death for Lucius Veturius.

EDCS-22600312.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name and age of death for Volumnia Alcime.

EDCS-22600322.translation: Euticius the confessor was laid to rest on the eighth day before the Kalends of September, in peace.
EDCS-22600322.summary: A funerary inscription for Euticius, identified as a 'confessor', with his date of burial.

EDCS-22600326.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelius Fortunatus made this for his well-deserving friend, Canius Eutychus, a soldier of the third cohort of vigiles, of the century of Antoninus, a tesserarius, who served for 11 years and lived for 31 years.
EDCS-22600326.summary: A funerary monument erected by Aelius Fortunatus for his friend Canius Eutychus, a soldier in the vigiles, detailing his service and lifespan.

EDCS-22600327.translation: To the departed spirits. Nunniena Priscilla, a most unhappy mother, made this for her son, Publius Nunnienus Sabinianus, a soldier of the first cohort of vigiles.
EDCS-22600327.summary: A funerary monument erected by Nunniena Priscilla for her deceased son, Publius Nunnienus Sabinianus, a soldier in the vigiles.

EDCS-22600328.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Acutius Faustinus, soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, of the trireme of Castor, lived 56 years and served for 23 years. Genaeius Epulanus Pius made this. [3]
EDCS-22600328.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Acutius Faustinus, a sailor in the Misenum fleet, detailing his service and lifespan, with Genaeius Epulanus Pius as the maker.

EDCS-22600329.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Rufus made this for Marcus Antonius Aristo, a soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, who served for 24 years and lived for 35 years.
EDCS-22600329.summary: A funerary monument erected by Titus Flavius Rufus for Marcus Antonius Aristo, a sailor in the praetorian fleet, detailing his service and lifespan.

EDCS-22600330.translation: To the departed spirits. Aponius Maximus made this for his well-deserving Publius Artorius Pastor, born in Alexandria, a soldier from the praetorian fleet of Misenum, who served for 12 years and lived for 32 years.
EDCS-22600330.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Artorius Pastor, a soldier from Misenum born in Alexandria, detailing his service and lifespan, erected by Aponius Maximus.

EDCS-22600331.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Aiatitus Verus, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, a centurion of the quinquereme Dacico, served for 15 years, lived for 41 years. Erected by C. Valerius Maximus, optio.
EDCS-22600331.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Aiatitus Verus, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600332.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Caecilius Valens, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, serving on the trireme Salami(na), served for 8 years, lived for 31 years. Gaius Lucilius Valens, his body servant, erected this for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600332.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Caecilius Valens, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600333.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Carminius Provincialis, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, serving on the trireme Partico, lived 50 years and 3 months, served 32 years. [---]vionius [---] erected this for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600333.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Carminius Provincialis, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600335.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Clodius Naso, serving on the trireme Augusta, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Ravenna, lived 35 years, served 12 years. Erected by his heir, Publius Petilius Exoratus, for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600335.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Clodius Naso, a soldier of the Ravenna fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600336.translation: To the departed spirits. Dasumius Papus, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Ravenna, serving on the quinquereme Pado, of Dalmatian origin, lived 50 years, served 25 years. Erected by his heir Marius Mansuetus, for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600336.summary: This is a tombstone for Dasumius Papus, a Dalmatian soldier of the Ravenna fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600337.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Domitius Reginus, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Ravenna, a suboptio on the quinquereme Fortuna, served 18 years, lived 36 years. Secundus, his heir, erected this.
EDCS-22600337.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Domitius Reginus, a soldier and suboptio in the Ravenna fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600339.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Julius Saturninus, from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, served 16 years, lived 53 years. Gaius Julius Sabinianus erected this for his father, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600339.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Julius Saturninus, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600340.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Memmius Valens, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, of Bessian origin, in the century of Iulius Serenus, lived 30 years, served 8 years. Quintus Iulius Dolis, soldier of the same fleet and his heir, erected this for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600340.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Memmius Valens, a Bessian soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600341.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Petronius Maximianus, soldier of the Misenum fleet, serving on the liburna Clementia, of Egyptian origin, lived 30 years, served 10 years. Valerius Saturninus, soldier of the same fleet and his substitute heir, erected this for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600341.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Petronius Maximianus, an Egyptian soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600342.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. For Aulus Valerius Cassianus, of Bessian origin, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, served 15 years, lived 33 years. Didius Valerianus and Tarutius Victor, his heirs, erected this for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22600342.summary: This is a tombstone for Aulus Valerius Cassianus, a Bessian soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22600343.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Gaius Valerius Fronton, soldier of the praetorian fleet at Ravenna, who lived 37 years and served 18 years and 6 months. Marcus Sossius Quietus, from the same fleet, his heir, made this.
EDCS-22600343.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Valerius Fronton, a soldier in the Ravenna fleet, erected by his heir Marcus Sossius Quietus.

EDCS-22600344.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cassius Neo made this for his brother's son, Timaegenes.
EDCS-22600344.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Cassius Neo for his deceased nephew, Timaegenes.

EDCS-22600345.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia made this for her well-deserving husband, Salo Saulus.
EDCS-22600345.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Iulia for her deserving husband, Salo Saulus.

EDCS-22600346.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Licinia Italia made this for her well-deserving husband, Calisto, a slave of Caesar.
EDCS-22600346.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Licinia Italia for her deserving husband, Calisto, who was a slave of Caesar.

EDCS-22600347.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Licinius Repentinus made this for his well-deserving wife, Nonnia Colonicai, who lived 37 years.
EDCS-22600347.summary: This is a tombstone for Nonnia Colonicai, erected by her husband Licinius Repentinus, with her age at death specified.

EDCS-22600348.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Polychronia, by nationality Thyna, who lived 20 years and served 4 years and 4 months. Marcus Valerius Maximus, her owner, and Titus Flavius Capito, her fellow countryman, and Titus Eraulius Valens, her friend, made this for her, who deserved well.
EDCS-22600348.summary: This tombstone honors Polychronia, a Thynian woman who lived 20 years and served for 4 years and 4 months, and was erected by her owner, a fellow countryman, and a friend.

EDCS-22600349.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Primitivos, who lived 1 year...
EDCS-22600349.summary: This is a fragment of a tombstone for Primitivos, who died in his first year of life.

EDCS-22600356.translation: By order of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, under the supervision of Hebrus, freedman of the Emperor and procurator. Gaius Lucilius Pylades made this.
EDCS-22600356.summary: This inscription records a construction or dedication commissioned under Emperor Trajan and supervised by the procurator Hebrus, completed by Gaius Lucilius Pylades.

EDCS-22600357.translation: By order of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, under the supervision of Hebrus, freedman of the Emperor and procurator. Tuendus, a slave, made this.
EDCS-22600357.summary: This inscription records a construction or dedication commissioned under Emperor Trajan, supervised by the procurator Hebrus, and completed by a slave named Tuendus.

EDCS-22600358.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Persis, who deserved well, who lived 40 years. The vilicus (estate manager) of Pamphilus, dispensator of Augustus, made this.
EDCS-22600358.summary: This tombstone honors Persis, who lived 40 years, and was erected by the estate manager of Pamphilus, a dispensator (financial officer) of the Emperor.

EDCS-22600362.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Symphorus, freedman of the Emperor, and his son Symphorus made this for their most devoted wife Antistia Tecusa. She lived for 40 years.
EDCS-22600362.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a freedman and his son to a wife and mother, dated by the mention of the freedman's status.

EDCS-22600380.translation: Iulia Apronia, who lived for 30 years of her life and was married for 16 years, has fallen asleep in peace.
EDCS-22600380.summary: A funerary inscription for a woman named Iulia Apronia, noting her age at death and duration of marriage.

EDCS-22600384.text_edition: \n Iunoni Historiae Telephus et Priscus posuerunt dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-22600384.translation: Telephus and Priscus set up and dedicated this to Iuno Historia.
EDCS-22600384.summary: A dedication to the goddess Iuno Historia by two individuals, Telephus and Priscus.

EDCS-22600387.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus, the colony of Iulia Castronovo, devoted to his divine majesty and his greatness, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22600387.summary: A state dedication by the colony of Iulia Castronovo to Emperor Gallienus, likely during his reign.

EDCS-22600393.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Caius Laberius Proculus, soldier of the 9th praetorian cohort, under the centuria of Aufidius, served for 11 years.
EDCS-22600393.summary: A funerary inscription for Caius Laberius Proculus, a soldier in the praetorian guard, noting his service length.

EDCS-22600394.translation: Lucius Ateius Capito, son of Marcus, five-year duumvir, personally oversaw the construction of the curia, record office, stage, and lower seating area from private funds for the colonists of Castrum Novum; furthermore, by decree of the decurions, he oversaw the construction of the portico and apartments from his own funds and approved them.
EDCS-22600394.summary: A public works inscription detailing the building projects funded and overseen by Lucius Ateius Capito in Castrum Novum, likely in the 1st century AD based on his office.

EDCS-22600395.translation: To Lucius Ateius Capito, son of Marcus, five-year duumvir. Marcus Ligvius Rufus, son of [---], of the Sergia tribe.
EDCS-22600395.summary: An inscription honoring Lucius Ateius Capito, a local magistrate, with a dedication likely made by Marcus Ligvius Rufus.

EDCS-22600404.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Balbillus and Firma made this for their son Lucius Arruntius Heliodorus.
EDCS-22600404.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by parents to their son, Lucius Arruntius Heliodorus.

EDCS-22600408.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine [Augustus], 240 feet.
EDCS-22600408.summary: A measurement inscription related to Emperor Augustus, possibly a boundary marker or land survey.

EDCS-22600409.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the divine [Augustus], pontifex maximus, the senate and people of Caere.
EDCS-22600409.summary: A dedication from the town of Caere to Emperor Augustus, recognizing his religious and political authority.

EDCS-22600412.translation: To the Divine Drusilla, sister of [Gaius Caesar] Augustus Germanicus.
EDCS-22600412.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Diva Drusilla, sister of Caligula.

EDCS-22600414.translation: To Julia Augusta, daughter of Germanicus Caesar, Agrippina, [daughter of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus].
EDCS-22600414.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Julia Agrippina, daughter of Germanicus and wife of Claudius.

EDCS-22600417.translation: To the consul for the third time, with tribunician power. Sextus Vettius, the ninth and...
EDCS-22600417.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the honors conferred upon an individual, possibly Sextus Vettius, and mentioning a colleague named Cn. Po. during the reign of an emperor.

EDCS-22600428.translation: Vesbinus, freedman of Augustus, presented the Augustales with a decorated phretrium at his own expense, after receiving the site from the public treasury. This was recorded and verified in the pronaos of the Temple of Mars, based on a report ordered to be produced by Cuperius Hostilianus through the scribe Titus Rustius Lysiponus, which stated what is written below: In the consulship of Lucius Publilius Celsus II and Gaius Clodius Crispinus, on the Ides of April. In the dictatorship of Marcus Pontius Celsus and the aedileship of Gaius Suetonius Claudianus, prefect of the treasury, the daily record of the town of Caere, chapter VI, page XXVII. Marcus Pontius Celsus, dictator, and Gaius Suetonius Claudianus, decurions, convened in the temple of the gods, where Vesbinus, freedman of Augustus, requested that a public site be granted to him under the portico of the Sulpician basilica so that he could establish a phretrium for the Augustales. By unanimous consent of the decurions, the site he desired was granted to him, and it pleased all, including Curiatius Cosanus, the curator, that a letter be sent to the curia regarding this matter. The following were present: Pontius Celsus, dictator; Suetonius Claudianus, aedile in charge of justice; Marcus Lepidius Nepos, aedile of the grain supply; Pollius Blandus; Pescennius Flavianus; Pescennius Natalis; Pollius Callimus; Petronius Innocens; Sergius Proculus. Then on the following page, chapter I: Greetings from the magistrates and decurions to Curiatius Cosanus. On the Ides of August, we convened the decurions at your request. They were asked to grant you a public site in the corner of the portico of the basilica, as you promised to publicly adorn it as a phretrium for the Augustales, in accordance with the dignity of the town. Thanks were given to you by all. However, it was decided to write to you to see if you would also consent to this, as the site is not in use by the public and can yield no revenue. Page VIII, chapter I: Curiatius Cosanus to the magistrates and decurions of Caere, greetings. I should not only consent to your wishes but also congratulate you if someone adorns our republic. Therefore, I accede to your opinion, not as a curator, but as one of the order, since such honorable examples should be stimulated by honorable adornment. Dated the day before the Ides of September in Ameria. Done on the Ides of June, in the consulship of Quintus Ninnio Hasta and Publius Manilius Vopiscos. Dedicated on the Kalends of August, in the same consulship.
EDCS-22600428.summary: This lengthy inscription records the grant of public land to Vesbinus, a freedman of Augustus, for the construction of a phretrium (a meeting place or hall) for the Augustales (a college of priests) in the town of Caere, detailing the process of approval by local magistrates and decurions, with an estimated date in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22600429.translation: To Gaius Manlius, son of Gaius, perpetual censor, from his clients to their patron.
EDCS-22600429.summary: This inscription is a dedication from clients to their patron, Gaius Manlius, who held the office of perpetual censor.

EDCS-22600448.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription commemorating Tarquitius Gallus, son of Gaius.

EDCS-22600463.translation: Lucius Equitius Caecilianus Postimus, son of Gaius.
EDCS-22600463.summary: This is a simple funerary or commemorative inscription for Lucius Equitius Caecilianus Postimus.

EDCS-22600491.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or a memorial, mentioning an Aulus I[---] and Rufioni[---].

EDCS-22600492.translation: Gaius Sentius Chilo, freedman of Gaius.
EDCS-22600492.summary: This is a short inscription identifying Gaius Sentius Chilo, a freedman.

EDCS-22700159.translation: Licinia Fausta, freedwoman of Gaius, and Gaius Licinius Hilarus, freedman of Gaius, have set this up for themselves and their families.
EDCS-22700159.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument established by a freedwoman and a freedman for themselves and their families.

EDCS-22700169.translation: To Sol Invictus: Lucius Volumnius Horatianus, a member of the sodalitas Augustalis, urban praetor.
EDCS-22700169.summary: This is a dedication to the god Sol Invictus by Lucius Volumnius Horatianus, identified by his civic and religious roles, likely dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-22700174.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Britannicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, Imperator twice, Consul three times, Proconsul, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, the most strong, most fortunate, son of the Pontifex Maximus, Father of the Fatherland, grandson of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva; for his outstanding virtues, the decurions of the colony of Alsiensis gave and dedicated [this].
EDCS-22700174.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix by the decurions of the colony of Alsiensis, listing his extensive titles and honors, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22700175.translation: Marcus Herennius Rufus, son of Marcus, of the tribe Maecia, prefect for Capua and Cumae, quaestor.
EDCS-22700175.summary: This inscription records the name and offices of Marcus Herennius Rufus, an individual holding significant administrative positions in Capua and Cumae, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-22700176.text_edition: \n Publio Metilio Publi filio Claudia Secundo Pon consuli fratri Arvali legato Augusti pro praetore curatori operum locorumque publicorum legato Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae et exercitus Africani legato Augusti legionis XI Claudiae piae et fidelis praetori tribuno plebis quaestori Imperatoris Caesaris Nervae Traiani Augusti Germanici Dacici seviro equitum Romanorum tribuno militum legionis X geminae piae fidelis triumviro\n
EDCS-22700176.translation: To Publius Metilius Secundus, son of Publius, of the Claudia tribe, consul, brother, legate of the emperor with the rank of propraetor, curator of public works and places, legate of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus with the rank of propraetor of the Legion III Augusta and the African army, legate of the emperor, of Legion XI Claudia Pia Fidelis, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, member of the college of the equites Romani, tribune of soldiers of Legion X Gemina Pia Fidelis, triumvir.
EDCS-22700176.summary: This is a lengthy inscription detailing the distinguished career of Publius Metilius Secundus, a high-ranking military and administrative official, likely from the Hadrianic period.

EDCS-22700177.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Tiberius Claudius Maximus, trierarch of the fleet [...]
EDCS-22700177.summary: This is a tomb inscription dedicated to the spirits of the underworld for Tiberius Claudius Maximus, who held the naval rank of trierarch, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-22700178.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Titus Aelius Eutyches, procurator of our Augustus at the villa of Alsiensis. His heirs [set this up].
EDCS-22700178.summary: This is a tomb inscription dedicated to the spirits of the underworld for Titus Aelius Eutyches, a procurator of the emperor at the villa of Alsiensis, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-22700189.translation: Sacred to Fortuna: Lusia Galeria Rufina, daughter of Gaius, a most distinguished woman.
EDCS-22700189.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Fortuna by Lusia Galeria Rufina, identified as a 'clarissima femina', indicating her high social standing, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-22700190.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Ammon, optio of the Ravenna fleet, who lived 40 years, served 17 years. Circensius Fronto, trierarch. His heir made [this] for him, a well-deserving man.
