EDCS-22700190.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Ammon, an optio in the Ravenna fleet, detailing his age and service length, set up by his heir Circensius Fronto, a trierarch, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-22700191.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: [Ca]ius Iulius Alexander, optio of the praetorian fleet, trierarch of the ship Victoria, of Misenum by nationality, who served 9 years and lived 26 years and 2 months. Luccia Trofime [set this up] for her deserving husband.
EDCS-22700191.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Caius Iulius Alexander, an optio in the praetorian fleet from Misenum, detailing his service and lifespan, set up by his wife Luccia Trofime, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-22700192.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Lucius Valerius Papirianus, soldier of the praetorian fleet at Misenum, from the trireme Tigris, of Alexandrian nationality, who lived 45 years and served 22 years. Valeria Epitexeis, his freedwoman and heir, made [this] for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-22700192.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Lucius Valerius Papirianus, a soldier in the Misenum praetorian fleet, detailing his age and service, set up by his freedwoman and heir, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-22700209.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Decimus Iunius Karus, likely a tombstone inscription.

EDCS-22700211.summary: This inscription names Galeria Rufina, possibly a dedication or tombstone with grammatical errors.

EDCS-22700222.translation: To Vettia Soteris, my best friend.
EDCS-22700222.summary: This is a tombstone inscription dedicating the monument to Vettia Soteris, described as a beloved friend.

EDCS-22700223.translation: Anthemius Augustinus lived for thirty-five years; I, Candida Marta, made this for my virgin husband with whom I lived for 14 years. Laid to rest on the 15th day before the Kalends of May, he sleeps in peace.
EDCS-22700223.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Anthemius Augustinus, made by his wife Candida Marta, recording his age at death and the date of his repose.

EDCS-22700224.translation: To Turtura, my wife. Quirianus made this for her, well-deserving, who lived with me for 4 years and 10 months. She departed at the age of 17, sleeps in peace.
EDCS-22700224.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Turtura, wife of Quirianus, detailing her age at death and duration of marriage.

EDCS-22700227.text_edition: \n Lucius Gellius Luci libertus Zethus sibi et Gelliae Luci libertae Euporiae conleibertae sic suae\n
EDCS-22700227.translation: Lucius Gellius Zethus, freedman of Lucius, for himself and for Gellius Euporia, freedwoman of Lucius, his fellow freedwoman and wife.
EDCS-22700227.summary: This is an funerary inscription for Lucius Gellius Zethus and his fellow freedwoman and wife, Gellius Euporia.

EDCS-22700229.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Secundus, servant of our Emperor... [lacuna] ... [lacuna] ... [lacuna] ... funerary plot (HS P S S) ... [lacuna] ... for freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. In the front 21 feet, in the back 28 feet.
EDCS-22700229.summary: This is a funerary inscription that allocates a plot of land for the burial of freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, for Secundus, a servant of the emperor, and others.

EDCS-22700230.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Decimus Caecilius Achillaris... [lacuna] ... his freedmen... [lacuna] ... by his testament... [lacuna] ...
EDCS-22700230.summary: This is a funerary inscription that makes provisions for the burial of freedmen of Decimus Caecilius Achillaris, according to his will.

EDCS-22700234.translation: To Mulvia Tyche. Gnaeus Mulvius Juvenis and Ancharia Sabina, her children, made this for their most dutiful and most loving, well-deserving mother.
EDCS-22700234.summary: This is a tombstone inscription erected by Gnaeus Mulvius Juvenis and Ancharia Sabina for their mother, Mulvia Tyche.

EDCS-22700237.translation: To Gaius Tarcontius Maximus, son of Gaius; and to Pietas, freedman of Gaius; and to Tarcontia Primigenia, freedwoman of Gaius; and to my freedmen and freedwomen. Gaius Tarcontius Maximus, son of Gaius, lived 5 years, 2 months, and 19 days.
EDCS-22700237.summary: This tombstone inscription is for Gaius Tarcontius Maximus, dedicating the plot to him and other freedmen and freedwomen, and recording his age at death.

EDCS-22700238.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Terentia Asiatica, daughter of Publius. Publius Terentius Quietus, her foster-father, here lies the lifeless body of his beloved foster-daughter, whom the Fates submerged with a cruel death, for she had not yet completed her tenth year of life. And they have made my old age cruel and sad, for I shall always seek you, my foster-daughter Asiatica, and mournfully I shall always fashion your face for myself, and it will be my solace that I shall soon see you when, having completed my life, I am joined to your shades.
EDCS-22700238.summary: A tomb inscription for Terentia Asiatica, a young girl, lamenting her death at a young age and the grief of her foster-father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22700244.translation: Sacred to Castor and Pollux. Merula, duovir.
EDCS-22700244.summary: A dedication to the divine twins Castor and Pollux by Merula, who held the office of duovir, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-22700245.translation: To Jupiter Tonans and Hercules Musinus, Marcus Annius Eusch<e=I>mon gave this on the 11th day before the Kalends of October, in the consulship of Gaius Gallicio Torquatus and Publius Salvius Julianus.
EDCS-22700245.summary: A religious dedication to Jupiter and Hercules by Marcus Annius Eusch<e=I>mon, dated to 11 October during the consulship of Gallicio Torquatus and Salvius Julianus in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22700247.translation: Dedicated on the 6th day before the Nones of January, in the second consulship of Aemilianus and Aquilinus, with Publius Serg<o> Maximus and Marcus Lollio Sabinianus serving as quinquennial duovirs, under the supervision of Veientius Ianvarius, a bookseller of the arc<a=K> district. To Victory Augusta, sacred, restored after most ancient decay by the order of the people of Veii.
EDCS-22700247.summary: A dedication to Victory Augusta by the order of the people of Veii, noting its restoration and dating it to 6 January during the second consulship of Aemilianus and Aquilinus, with local officials named.

EDCS-22700248.translation: Sacred. Lucius Decimius Gamus, freedman of Lucius, VIvir Augustalis, took care to set this up at his own expense for the cost of the games. Dedicated in the time of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, by the seviri and seviral officials and others. During the consulship of Paullus Fabius Persicus and Lucius Vitellius. [---] Rufus, son of Lucius Mummius, son of Lucius. [---]
EDCS-22700248.summary: A dedication concerning the games, sponsored by Lucius Decimius Gamus, a VIvir Augustalis, during the consulship of Fabius Persicus and Vitellius, and mentioning Tiberius Caesar, dating it to the early Roman Empire.

EDCS-22700249.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine [Augustus], pontifex maximus, imperator, consul, etc., holder of tribunician power, father of the country. Quintus Numisius Thyrsus, freedman of Quintus. Marcus Numicius Acastus, freedman of the mulier Numicia. Lucius Postumius Eros Maior, freedman of Lucius. Lucius Messius Salvius, freedman of Lucius. Gaius Volumnius Bello, freedman of Gaius. Quintus Marius Stabilio, freedman of Quintus. The seviri Augustales, for the games.
EDCS-22700249.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Augustus and listing the names of several freedmen who served as seviri Augustales, likely related to the funding or organization of games, dating to the reign of Augustus.

EDCS-22700261.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 11th time, father of the country, designated consul for the 4th time.
EDCS-22700261.summary: A dedication honoring Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, listing his titles and powers, likely a formal inscription for a public monument.

EDCS-22700266.translation: To Marcus Herennius Picens, son of Marcus, consul. By the fellow citizens of the municipium of Augusti Veientis, the intramurani, as patron.
EDCS-22700266.summary: An honorary inscription by the citizens of Augusti Veientis (within the walls) to their patron Marcus Herennius Picens, who served as consul, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22700267.translation: [---] to Aescionius, son of Gaius Capella, duovir, military tribune, prefect of the fabrica. By the citizens living outside the walls and the Augustales, from contributions of bronze. [---] for the games in the orchestra.
EDCS-22700267.summary: An honorary inscription for Aescionius, a local magistrate and military officer, commissioned by the citizens living outside the walls and the Augustales, concerning games and funded by collected bronze, likely from a Roman provincial town.

EDCS-22700269.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Publius Caecilius Vallianus, by his fellow soldiers. He lived for 64 years.
EDCS-22700269.summary: A tombstone inscription for Publius Caecilius Vallianus, recording his service in the military and his lifespan, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22700756.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-22700756.translation: Marcus Tarquitius Saturninus, son of Titus, of the Tromentina tribe, prefect of the Scutata cohort, former first-rank centurion of the XXII Legion, military tribune of the III Legion, [and of the] XXII Legion, was chosen among the Curiati by Gnaeus Caesius Athictus, who restored and returned to public use at his own expense a statue vexed by ruin from the temple of Mars.
EDCS-22700756.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a statue of Mars by Gnaeus Caesius Athictus in honor of Marcus Tarquitius Saturninus, a military officer.

EDCS-22700760.translation: When the Centumviri of the municipality of Augustan Veii convened in Rome in the temple of Venus Genetrix, it was agreed by all, while the decree was being written, that in the meantime, by the authority of all, permission be granted to Gaius Julius Gelos, freedman of the deified Augustus, who at all times not only assisted the municipality of Veii with his counsel and favor, but also wished it to be celebrated at his own expense and through his son, that the most just honor be decreed to him, that he be counted among the Augustales as if he had held that honor, and that it be permitted for him to sit in his own bisellium among the Augustales at all public shows of our municipality and to attend all public banquets among the Centumviri; and it was also agreed that no tax be levied from him and his children by the municipality of Augustan Veii. This was done in the consulship of Gaetulicus and Calvisius Sabinus.
EDCS-22700760.summary: This is a decree by the Centumviri of Augustan Veii granting special honors and tax exemptions to Gaius Julius Gelos for his contributions to the municipality, issued during the consulship of Gaetulicus and Calvisius Sabinus.

EDCS-22700761.translation: Gelos, freedman of the deified Augustus, by decree of the Curiati, made and dedicated this in memory of his son after his death, on the 4th day before the Ides of May in the consulship of Titus Statilius Taurus and Publius Pomponius Secundus.
EDCS-22700761.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Gelos, a freedman of Augustus, in memory of his deceased son, dating to the consulship of Titus Statilius Taurus and Publius Pomponius Secundus.

EDCS-22700762.translation: To Gnaeus Caesius Athictus, chosen among the Curiati, honored with all offices, from the bronze collected, which all the councilmen contributed, in the orchestra during the games which Publius Memmius Apulus II and Gaius Poppaeus Priscus, the duovirs, held. Dedicated on the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Maximus and Glabrio, and under the duovirs Felix and Nemonius Silvanus, of the Veientines.
EDCS-22700762.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a bronze work in honor of Gnaeus Caesius Athictus, a prominent citizen, during public games in Veii, dated to the consulship of Maximus and Glabrio.

EDCS-22700763.translation: To Gnaeus Caesius Athictus, chosen among the Curiati, for his piety and munificence towards the divine house and the municipality of Augustan Veii, the centumviri, the seviri, the Augustales, and the intramural councilmen, from the bronze which was collected in the orchestra, during the games which Vergilius Cogitatus and Julius Senecio, the duovirs, held.
EDCS-22700763.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gnaeus Caesius Athictus by various civic bodies of Augustan Veii, including centumviri, seviri, and Augustales, for his piety and generosity, commemorating games held by duovirs Vergilius Cogitatus and Julius Senecio.

EDCS-22700764.translation: To Gnaeus Caesius Athictus, chosen among the Curiati, honored with all offices, by the Augustales of the municipality of Augustan Veii, from collected bronze, for the sake of honor.
EDCS-22700764.summary: This inscription records an honorary dedication to Gnaeus Caesius Athictus by the Augustales of Augustan Veii, funded by their collective contributions.

EDCS-22700766.translation: To Caesias Sabina, wife of Gnaeus Caesius Athictus, she alone of all women, gave a banquet to the mothers, sisters, and daughters of the Curiati and to the women of all orders of the townspeople, and on the days of the banquet and games, she gave her husband's bathhouse with free oil. (Dedicated by her) most pious sisters.
EDCS-22700766.summary: This inscription honors Caesias Sabina, wife of Gnaeus Caesius Athictus, for her generous hospitality towards the women of the community during public festivities.

EDCS-22700770.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a tombstone or dedication, to Publius Septimius Geta.

EDCS-22700774.translation: To the divine shades. Ulpia Martina, daughter, (erected this) to Marcus Ulpius Martialis, freedman of our Caesar Augustus, collector and banker.
EDCS-22700774.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Martialis, a collector and banker and freedman of Emperor Augustus, erected by his daughter Ulpia Martina.

EDCS-22700777.translation: In memory of Publius Cassius Atticus, son of Publius, a Salius, who lived 4 years, 2 months, 2 days. Publius Cassius Sabinus, son of Lucius, his most unhappy father, (erected this).
EDCS-22700777.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Cassius Atticus, a child who died at a young age, erected by his grieving father.

EDCS-22700778.translation: To Titus Flavius Salinator, son of Titus, of the Palatina tribe. Eubulus, his freedman, ordered this to be set up in his will to his most excellent and indulgent patron.
EDCS-22700778.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedman for his patron, Titus Flavius Salinator.

EDCS-22700779.translation: To Cassia Sabina, daughter of Publius, wife of Salinator. Eubulus, her freedman, ordered this to be set up in his will to his most excellent and indulgent patroness.
EDCS-22700779.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedman for his patroness, Cassia Sabina.

EDCS-22700780.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Fufius Salinator, son of Titus Flavius, Roman knight, lived 17 years and 3 months.
EDCS-22700780.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Fufius Salinator, son of Titus Flavius, a Roman knight, who died at age 17 years and 3 months.

EDCS-22700781.translation: To the departed spirits. Cassia Sabina, daughter of Publius, set this up to her foster-son, Titus Flavius Salutaris.
EDCS-22700781.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Cassia Sabina to her foster-son, Titus Flavius Salutaris.

EDCS-22700784.translation: To the departed spirits. To Caerellia Sevear, who lived 40 years, 5 months, and 25 days. Marcus Caerellius Iustus set this up for his most holy and deserving wife, with whom he lived 28 years, and Caerellia Severina, his daughter, for her dearest mother.
EDCS-22700784.summary: This is a funerary monument for Caerellia Sevear, erected by her husband Marcus Caerellius Iustus and daughter Caerellia Severina.

EDCS-22700789.translation: To the departed spirits. To his deserving husband Titus Flavius Demosthenes, freedman of Augustus. And to Tiberius Claudius Mucro and Marcus Ulpius Romanus, freedman of Augustus, his most dutiful sons. Claudia Danae, his mother, made this for herself, and for her freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-22700789.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Claudia Danae to her husband Titus Flavius Demosthenes and her sons Tiberius Claudius Mucro and Marcus Ulpius Romanus, and for herself and her household.

EDCS-22700790.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Artimicla, freedwoman of Lygia.

EDCS-22700792.translation: To the departed spirits. Manneia Candida made this for her deserving husband Marcus Manneius Callistus, who lived 75 years.
EDCS-22700792.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Manneius Callistus, erected by his wife Manneia Candida.

EDCS-22700793.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Munatius Felix, identified as 'father'.

EDCS-22700794.translation: To Marcus Saenius Marcellus. Tarquitia Prisca, his wife.
EDCS-22700794.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Tarquitia Prisca to her husband Marcus Saenius Marcellus.

EDCS-22700795.translation: Gaius Varius Zoticus, son of Gaius' freedman. Varia Hilara, daughter of Gaius' freedman. Gaius Varius Aeschimus, son of Gaius' freedman.
EDCS-22700795.summary: This inscription lists three freedmen and freedwomen who appear to be members of the same household or family, identified by their patron Gaius Varius.

EDCS-22700796.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Postumius Maxumus, son of Lucius, of the Veianti tribe.
EDCS-22700796.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Postumius Maxumus, likely a tombstone or memorial.

EDCS-22700797.translation: To Virgilia Romula, daughter of Marcus, an incomparable woman. Gaius Caesellius Latinus and his sons Caesellius Latinus and Romulus to their most holy wife and most loving mother.
EDCS-22700797.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication from a husband and sons to a deceased wife and mother, Virgilia Romula.

EDCS-22700798.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. To [son's name], son of Titus, of Serdica, a soldier in the Praetorian cohort, the Piae Vindicis Severianae, in the century of Ulpius [name], he served for 17 years, lived [number] years and 6 months and [number] days. His brother Servillus and Se[name] set this up for him, his well-deserving.
EDCS-22700798.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier of the Praetorian Guard, detailing his service and lifespan, set up by his brother.

EDCS-22700799.translation: Lucius Arruntius Hilario, freedman of Lucius, a cook, lived 40 years.
EDCS-22700799.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Arruntius Hilario, a cook and freedman, stating his age at death.

EDCS-22700802.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Cornelia Isidis, daughter of Gaius, who lived 8 years, 1 month, and 21 days, in a place donated by Cocceia Bassa. The parents made this for themselves, their descendants, and their kin.
EDCS-22700802.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Cornelia Isidis and indicates it was originally intended as a family burial plot.

EDCS-22700804.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Felicis, a deserving wife. Spes set this up with whom she lived 33 years and [number] months. [Name] made this statue. [Name] and Ronon, friend.
EDCS-22700804.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Felicis, set up by her partner Spes, and mentioning other individuals.

EDCS-22700805.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Furcilia Procilla, dearest, most holy, most devoted wife. Marcus Ulpius Perseus and his daughter Procilla.
EDCS-22700805.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Furcilia Procilla, dedicated by her husband and daughter.

EDCS-22700807.translation: To the Divine Manes. Tertia for herself and Agathopus, her [husband?].
EDCS-22700807.summary: This funerary inscription appears to be a double tombstone or memorial for Tertia and Agathopus, possibly husband and wife.

EDCS-22700816.translation: Lia Proculla, Annia Veneria, to the Bona Dea as a magistra, a silver mirror weighing [weight]. The same person dedicated it under the consulship of Antoninus Augustus.
EDCS-22700816.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a silver mirror to the Bona Dea by a magistra, with a possible indication of the consulship date.

EDCS-22700819.translation: Sepenatus, a magistrate, restored [this] from his own funds.
EDCS-22700819.summary: This is a funerary inscription mentioning a magistrate who paid for a restoration, likely of a public building or a monument.

EDCS-22700822.translation: To Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Consul V, Tribune of the Plebs XXXIV, the most excellent and just protector of the fatherland, for his health and safety, Aulus Fabius Fortunatus, viator of the consuls and praetors, first Augustalis, established [this] having undertaken a vow.
EDCS-22700822.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Tiberius by a prominent official, Aulus Fabius Fortunatus, likely dating to 31 AD.

EDCS-22700823.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus, Consul II, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune of the Plebs, Father of the Fatherland, Prince of the Senate, most courageous leader, and prince of all virtues, the allied people of Capena, under the praetor Caius Licinius Speratus, [dedicated this]. Dedicated on the 13th day before the Kalends of April, under the supervision of Publius Sextilius Prospectus, quinquennalis, and Lucius Mucianus, and Claudius, consuls.
EDCS-22700823.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Pertinax by the allied community of Capena, likely dating to his consulship in 192 AD.

EDCS-22700827.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Tribune of the Plebs, Proconsul, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, most courageous, most fortunate, Father of the Fatherland, grandson of Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan and the divine Nerva, the allied people of Capena, under the praetor Manilius Crescens, [dedicated this]. Dedicated on the 14th day before the Kalends of October, under Saturninus and Gallus, consuls.
EDCS-22700827.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Caracalla by the allied community of Capena, likely dating to 205 AD.

EDCS-22700834.translation: To the Divine Shades. Of Marcus Coelius Flavius Proculus, member of the board of fifteen for judging lawsuits, tribune with laticlavus of the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix, member of the board of six of the Roman cavalry, quaestor, candidate for tribune of the plebs, candidate for praetor, curator of the Republic of Aquinum.
EDCS-22700834.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Coelius Flavius Proculus, detailing his significant career progression through various military and political offices, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-22700837.translation: To the Divine Shades. Of Dai, freedman of Tiberius Claudius Augustus, scribe of the accounts of the patrimony of the Caesars. He lived 59 years. Servilia Aphro to her dearest husband. Servilia Aphro, most devoted.
EDCS-22700837.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Dai, a freedman responsible for imperial financial accounts, and his devoted wife Servilia Aphro, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-22700845.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Marcus Pontius Victorinus, son of Marcus, dearest father. By Marcus Pontius Victorinus, his son, veteran of the 9th Praetorian Cohort. He made [this].
EDCS-22700845.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a son for his father, Marcus Pontius Victorinus, a veteran of the Praetorian Guard, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-22700847.translation: Lictoria Chaerusa lived 15 years, 7 months, 5 days. Lucius Veturius Pudens, her husband, and Larcia Aucta, her mother, made this monument for her. [She bequeaths] the rose garden with the small vineyard, with her solarium, the fine boundary of the vineyard, and opposite it, the fishpond and the channel up to the threshing floor, and the threshing floor with buildings, and the storehouse, and the cistern in the solarium, and opposite it up to the reed bed, with the defined roads, and [it is estimated to contain] 7 jugera of land.
EDCS-22700847.summary: This is a funerary inscription and testament of Lictoria Chaerusa, detailing property bequeathed, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-22700885.translation: To Titus Flavius Flavianus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, aedile, quaestor designate, of the allied municipality of Capena. Titus Flavius Mythus, freedman of Augustus, and Flavia Diogis, his parents, made [this] for their most devoted son, and for themselves, and for their freedmen and freedwomen of both sexes, and their descendants, with a bath and buildings adjoining on both sides along the road, with an aqueduct from the Cutulenian estate and approximately 4 jugera of the Cutulenian land, as has been marked out; this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-22700885.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Flavius Flavianus, a local official in Capena, erected by his parents who also provided for their own future burial and specified land and water rights, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-22700888.translation: To the Divine Shades. Of Tiberius Mollius Clemens, sevirus, of the municipality of Capena. His sons and freedmen erected this altar.
EDCS-22700888.summary: This is an altar erected by the sons and freedmen of Tiberius Mollius Clemens, a member of the seviri, in the municipality of Capena, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-22700889.translation: To Lucius Pacatius Tyrannus, honored member of the guild of carpenters, from the townspeople of Capua, the allied municipality, with a collection made by the order of the same municipality, and by the Augustales, and by the villagers, likewise by the freedmen of Pacatius himself, because of his merits. The place was granted by decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the fourteenth day before the Kalends of October, when Junius Rusticus and Plautius Aquilinus were consuls. [---] games [---] [---] [---]
EDCS-22700889.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public space by the decurions and various groups of citizens to Lucius Pacatius Tyrannus in honor of his merits, likely for a public monument.

EDCS-22700891.text_edition: \n Marco Silio Epaphrodito patrono sevirum Augustalium magistro iuvenum iterum iuvenes Lucoferonenses patrono ob merita quod amphitheatrum coloniae Iuliae Felici Lucoferonensium sua pecunia fecit dedicavitque loco dato decreto decurionum honore contentus impendium remisit\n
EDCS-22700891.translation: To Marcus Silius Epaphroditus, patron, former leader of the Augustales, twice master of the youth, the young men of Lucoferia to their patron, because of his merits, for he built and dedicated the amphitheater of the colony Julia Felix of Lucoferia at his own expense, content with the honor granted by decree of the decurions, he remitted the cost.
EDCS-22700891.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated to Marcus Silius Epaphroditus by the youth of Lucoferia for his significant contribution of building an amphitheater at his own expense.

EDCS-22700893.text_edition: \n Tito Vennonio Titi filio Stellatina Aebutiano patrono et municipi coloniae Augustae Taurinorum equiti Romano equo publico iudici ex V decuriis selecto curatori rei publicae Albensium Pompeianorum Laurenti Lavinati pontifici eiusdem sacerdoti Munia Quinti filia Celerina uxor marito karissimo\n
EDCS-22700893.translation: To Titus Vennonius Aebutianus, son of Titus, of the Stellatina tribe, patron of the colony Augusta Taurinorum, Roman knight, who served with public horse, judge selected from the V decuries, curator of the public affairs of Alba Pompeia, Laurentum, and Lavinium, priest of the same, from Munia Celerina, daughter of Quintus, his wife, to her dearest husband.
EDCS-22700893.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her husband, Titus Vennonius Aebutianus, listing his numerous civic and religious honors.

EDCS-22700896.translation: Gaius Calpurnius Asclepiades from Prusa ad Olympum, physician, obtained the citizenship for seven communities for his parents, himself, and his brothers from the deified Trajan. Born on the third day before the Nones of March, in the thirteenth consulship of Domitian, on the same day as his wife Verona Chelidon, with whom he lived for 51 years. Approved for his studies and character by the most distinguished men, he sat with the magistrates of the Roman people, serving as a custodian of ballots in the urn of judges. He lived for 70 years.
EDCS-22700896.summary: This epitaph commemorates Gaius Calpurnius Asclepiades, a physician who obtained citizenship for several communities and served in various official capacities, likely from the late 1st to mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-22700901.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Volcius Hermes of the Palatina tribe, a dealer in ivory, a deserving man. Marcus Volcius Abascantus, his freedman, made [this] for him, [of the same family].
EDCS-22700901.summary: A funerary dedication for Marcus Volcius Hermes, an ivory merchant, made by his freedman, Marcus Volcius Abascantus.

EDCS-22700907.translation: To the Divine Shades. Quintus Apertius Fuscinianus, son of Gaius Apertius, of the Quirina tribe, lived for 4 years and 7 days. Gaius Apertius Fuscus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, and Terentia Daria, his parents, [made this] for their sweetest son.
EDCS-22700907.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Apertius Fuscinianus, who died in childhood, set up by his parents.

EDCS-22700908.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Marcus Arruntius Probus. Titus Flavius Diadumenus, freedman of Augustus, made [this] for his deserving friend.
EDCS-22700908.summary: A funerary dedication by Titus Flavius Diadumenus to his friend Marcus Arruntius Probus.

EDCS-22700916.translation: To Gaius Clodius Fabatus, my best husband. Atilia Marcella [says this]. My earthly body. His heavenly spirit, from which, returning to its seat, now we live there and Fabatus enjoys the eternal light above.
EDCS-22700916.summary: This epitaph, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE, is a poetic dedication by Atilia Marcella to her husband Gaius Clodius Fabatus, expressing her continued connection to him after his death.

EDCS-22700935.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lusena Staphyla, freedwoman of Titus.
EDCS-22700935.summary: A simple funerary dedication to Lusena Staphyla, a freedwoman.

EDCS-22700940.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Mitrasia Severa, daughter of Publius.
EDCS-22700940.summary: A funerary dedication to Mitrasia Severa, daughter of Publius.

EDCS-22700943.translation: To Eutycles, son of Lucius Pacatus, most excellent and dearest, who lived 7 years, 3 months, and 6 days, and to his most excellent and most indulgent partner, Pacatia Cameni, by permission of his most excellent and most indulgent patron, Lucius Pacatus Tyrannus.
EDCS-22700943.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a young boy named Eutycles and his partner Pacatia Cameni, likely from a tomb, referencing his patron and father.

EDCS-22700962.translation: Marcus Vitelius Theodorus, freedman of Marcus; Baebia Fausta; Marcus Vitelius Qursor, freedman of Marcus; Iulia Rufa, freedwoman of Caius. Traveler, come here and rest; you nod and deny, but you must return.
EDCS-22700962.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph with a rhyming verse intended to be read by passersby, urging them to rest and acknowledge the deceased.

EDCS-22700977.translation: Agrippa, with his sons, made this for his most chaste wife, Numisia Paula, who lived 46 years, 11 months, and 8 days, a well-deserving woman. Deposited on the 7th day before the Kalends of September, in peace.
EDCS-22700977.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Numisia Paula and was erected by her husband and sons, indicating her burial date.

EDCS-22700978.translation: Her father and brothers made this for Baleria Leoni, a well-deserving soul, most devoted, who lived 35 years, 5 months, and 8 hours. Deposited on the Kalends of July.
EDCS-22700978.summary: This inscription honors Baleria Leoni, detailing her lifespan and the relationship of the dedicants, and noting her burial date.

EDCS-22700980.translation: Under the consulship of Constantius Augustus II and Constans Augustus, on the Nones of December, Claudianus sleeps in peace.
EDCS-22700980.summary: This inscription marks the date of death and burial of Claudianus during the joint consulship of Constantius II and Constans.

EDCS-22700982.translation: Under the consulship of Leontius and Bonosus, on the 12th day before the Kalends of February, Leo sleeps in peace.
EDCS-22700982.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Leo under the consuls Leontius and Bonosus.

EDCS-22700983.translation: Under the consulship of Leontius and Bonosus, on the 8th day before the Kalends of February, Uranius sleeps in this place in peace.
EDCS-22700983.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Uranius under the consuls Leontius and Bonosus.

EDCS-22700984.translation: Deposited under the consulship of Leontius and Bonosus. Florentinus in peace, [recovering] his (body?).
EDCS-22700984.summary: This inscription indicates the burial of Florentinus during the consulship of Leontius and Bonosus, with a partial restoration suggesting a recovery or peace.

EDCS-22700985.translation: Under the consulship of Amantius and Albinus, on the 4th day before the Nones of January, the deposition of Aurelia rests in the sleep of peace.
EDCS-22700985.summary: This inscription marks the burial date of Aurelia during the consulship of Amantius and Albinus.

EDCS-22700986.translation: The deposition of Renatus on the day before the Kalends of October, under the consulship of Amantius and Albinus, in peace.
EDCS-22700986.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Renatus during the consulship of Amantius and Albinus.

EDCS-22700987.translation: The burial of Renatus on the day before the Nones of October, with Amantius and Albinus as consuls, in peace.
EDCS-22700987.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Renatus in the consular year of Amantius and Albinus.

EDCS-22700988.translation: The 11th day of June, who lived 7 years, 5 months, and eight days, after the consulship of Amantius and Albinus.
EDCS-22700988.summary: This inscription records the death date and lifespan of an individual, likely a child, with a reference to a consular period.

EDCS-22700998.translation: Saturninus lived approximately 75 years, on the March [date], in the 7th year of our lord Honorius Augustus.
EDCS-22700998.summary: This inscription records the approximate lifespan of Saturninus and a date during the reign of Emperor Honorius.

EDCS-22701001.translation: Maximina was buried on the 8th day before the Ides of January, in peace.
EDCS-22701001.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Maximina.

EDCS-22701004.translation: Latinus was buried on the 8th day before the Kalends of April, in peace.
EDCS-22701004.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Latinus.

EDCS-22701005.translation: On the 18th day before the Kalends of June, Setimina was buried in June.
EDCS-22701005.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Setimina, with a partial reconstruction of the month.

EDCS-22701006.translation: Verna was buried on the 3rd day before the Nones of August.
EDCS-22701006.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Verna.

EDCS-22701007.translation: Felicissimus was buried on the Kalends of October, in peace.
EDCS-22701007.summary: This inscription records the burial date of Felicissimus.

EDCS-22701008.translation: The burial of Exsuperius, well-deserving, on the day before the Nones of February.
EDCS-22701008.summary: This inscription records the burial of Exsuperius and notes his merit.

EDCS-22701009.translation: The burial of Iulianus [on the 9th day].
EDCS-22701009.summary: This inscription records the burial of Iulianus with a partially reconstructed date.

EDCS-22701010.translation: Burial of Antem[---] after the Ides of March, from Seberianes.
EDCS-22701010.summary: This inscription records the burial of a person named Antem[---] after the Ides of March.

EDCS-22701011.translation: The Nones of February, Viatris in peace.
EDCS-22701011.summary: This inscription records a person named Viatris being in peace on the Nones of February.

EDCS-22701012.translation: Buried on the third day before the Nones of October, Faustinus [---].
EDCS-22701012.summary: This inscription records the burial of Faustinus on the third day before the Nones of October.

EDCS-22701014.summary: This inscription records someone named Marcian[---]ae in peace.

EDCS-22701015.translation: Four days before the Kalends of May, Antonia in peace, with Christ.
EDCS-22701015.summary: This inscription records Antonia's burial in peace with Christ, four days before the Kalends of May.

EDCS-22701017.translation: Burial of Successus, the third Ides of July.
EDCS-22701017.summary: This inscription records the burial of Successus on the third Ides of July.

EDCS-22701019.translation: The Ides of December, received among the saints, Nominata.
EDCS-22701019.summary: This inscription records the reception of Nominata among the saints on the Ides of December.

EDCS-22701021.translation: Burial of Balicus the merchant, the fourth day before the Kalends of December, [by/for] Nevitta(?).
EDCS-22701021.summary: This inscription records the burial of the merchant Balicus on the fourth day before the Kalends of December.

EDCS-22701022.translation: Burial of Severa, the tenth day before the Kalends of December, after the consulship of Albinus Amantius; she lived [...].
EDCS-22701022.summary: This inscription records the burial of Severa, dating to after the consulship of Albinus Amantius, on the tenth day before the Kalends of December.

EDCS-22701023.translation: Burial of Leonis, on the Kalends of August.
EDCS-22701023.summary: This inscription records the burial of Leonis on the Kalends of August.

EDCS-22701025.translation: Deposited on the 13th day before the Kalends of April (March 20th).
EDCS-22701025.summary: This inscription marks the date of a deposition, likely a burial, in a Christian context.

EDCS-22701028.translation: Maxima, an honorable girl, who lived in peace for about 70 years, departed on the 3rd day before the Kalends of [August?].
EDCS-22701028.summary: This epitaph records the death of a young girl named Maxima, indicating her age and peaceful passing.

EDCS-22701029.translation: On the 6th day before the Kalends of September (August 27th), Beneritus the new servant in Christ.
EDCS-22701029.summary: This inscription records the name and clerical status of Beneritus in a Christian context, likely a memorial or dedication date.

EDCS-22701030.translation: Abundius, a holy presbyter martyr, deposited on the 7th day before the Ides of December (December 6th).
EDCS-22701030.summary: This inscription marks the deposition date of Abundius, identified as a holy presbyter and martyr, within a Christian context.

EDCS-23102413.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marco Postumio Sperato qui vixit annos III menses XI Postumi Aprilis et Spes alumno\n
EDCS-23102413.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Postumius Speratus, who lived 3 years and 11 months, [died]. Son of Postumia Aprilis and Spes, beloved foster-child.
EDCS-23102413.summary: This funerary inscription honors Marcus Postumius Speratus, detailing his age and parentage, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200972.translation: Imperator Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, rebuilt the Cassian Way, which had collapsed from age, from the borders of Chiusi to Florence, 76 miles from Florence.
EDCS-23200972.summary: This is an imperial dedication by Emperor Hadrian, recording the rebuilding of the Via Cassia and specifying its length, dating to his 7th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-23500213.translation: To the departed spirits. Munatius Hypnus to his most beloved wife, Munatia Aupausis.
EDCS-23500213.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication from Munatius Hypnus to his cherished wife, Munatia Aupausis.

EDCS-23800818.translation: Caius Raius Stachys, son of Gaius, grandson of Gaius, a sexvir, erected this to his most dutiful daughter, Raiia Iusta, who lived 32 years and 10 months.
EDCS-23800818.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life span of Raiia Iusta and commemorates her with a monument erected by her father, Caius Raius Stachys, a local magistrate.

EDCS-23800826.translation: To the departed spirits. [---]ippus, who lived [---]
EDCS-23800826.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to an individual whose name is partially preserved.

EDCS-24700128.text_edition: \n Aulus Salvius Auli filius Auli nepos Crispinus annorum LI heic conditus est gessit Ferenteis IIIIviratum quater summo supremo die cena et viscera municipibus\n
EDCS-24700128.translation: Aulus Salvius Crispinus, son of Aulus, grandson of Aulus, aged 51, lies buried here. He held the office of duovir in Ferentium four times. On the highest day, he provided a funeral feast and viscera to the townspeople.
EDCS-24700128.summary: This epitaph records the life and civic contributions of Aulus Salvius Crispinus, a four-time duovir of Ferentium, who also provided a funeral banquet for the citizens.

EDCS-24700147.text_edition: \n Lucius Nicidius Luci filius Sors scriba aedilium curulium\n
EDCS-24700147.translation: Lucius Nicidius, son of Lucius, clerk of the curule aediles.
EDCS-24700147.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Lucius Nicidius, who served as a clerk for the curule aediles.

EDCS-24700148.text_edition: \n Aulus Quintius Auli filius Tromentina Priscus gnatus Gestia\n
EDCS-24700148.translation: Aulus Quintius Priscus, son of Aulus, of the Tromentina tribe, grandson of Gestia.
EDCS-24700148.summary: This inscription records the name and tribal affiliation of Aulus Quintius Priscus, likely a citizen.

EDCS-24700149.translation: Of Quintus Aufidius, son of Gaius, of the Tromentina tribe, from Bucinae.
EDCS-24700149.summary: This inscription likely records ownership or dedication by Quintus Aufidius, identified by his name, father, tribe, and origin.

EDCS-24900044.summary: This inscription names Peto, a slave belonging to Fulvia, possibly on a grave marker or object.

EDCS-24900045.summary: This inscription is a direct address to someone named Octavian, perhaps a command or request.

EDCS-24900046.summary: This inscription names Peto and identifies a collar belonging to Octavian, possibly a pet's name.

EDCS-24900047.summary: This inscription describes a seat belonging to Octavius, possibly indicating ownership or a location.

EDCS-24900048.summary: This inscription is a simple designation of 'Caesar the Emperor', likely on a public monument or coin.

EDCS-24900049.summary: This inscription is a simple designation of 'Caesar the Emperor', likely on a public monument or coin.

EDCS-24900050.translation: Lucius Antoni, you have gone bald; Gaius Caesar's victory.
EDCS-24900050.summary: This inscription contrasts the aging of Lucius Antoni with the victory of Gaius Caesar, possibly a jibe or commentary.

EDCS-24900052.summary: This inscription records the title 'Emperor Rufus'.

EDCS-24900053.summary: This inscription records the title 'Emperor Rufus'.

EDCS-24900054.summary: This inscription records the title 'Emperor Rufus'.

EDCS-24900055.translation: Titus Etrus, primus pilus of the Fourth Legion
EDCS-24900055.summary: This inscription records the name and rank of Titus Etrus, primus pilus of the Fourth Legion.

EDCS-24900057.translation: Marcus Feridius, military tribune of the Eleventh Legion
EDCS-24900057.summary: This inscription records the name and rank of Marcus Feridius, military tribune of the Eleventh Legion.

EDCS-24900059.summary: This inscription refers to the Eleventh Legion and its connection to Julius Caesar.

EDCS-24900060.translation: The Twelfth Legion, Scaeva, primus pilus
EDCS-24900060.summary: This inscription records the name and rank of Scaeva, primus pilus of the Twelfth Legion.

EDCS-24900061.translation: The Twelfth Legion, Victrix; The Twelfth Legion, Victrix
EDCS-24900061.summary: This inscription records the name and epithet of the Twelfth Legion, 'Victrix', possibly as a dedication or unit identifier.

EDCS-24900068.summary: This inscription records the title 'Emperor Caesar'.

EDCS-24900071.translation: Eupor, son of Matrinus, looked favorably on the Ides of Quintilis, when Gnaeus Octavius and Gaius Curio were consuls.
EDCS-24900071.summary: This inscription is a dedication or record of an event that occurred on the Ides of Quintilis during the consulship of Gnaeus Octavius and Gaius Curio.

EDCS-26201104.translation: Lucius Volcacius Vicer, son of Lucius.
EDCS-26201104.summary: This is a tombstone inscription identifying an individual by name and filiation.

EDCS-26601005.text_edition: \n Caius Didius Titi filius Marcus Vettius Marci filius duomviri sic quinquennales statuas fornicesque decreto decurionum faciundum coeraverunt idemque probaverunt\n
EDCS-26601005.translation: Gaius Didius, son of Titus, and Marcus Vettius, son of Marcus, as quinquennial duovirs, supervised the construction of statues and arches by decree of the decurions, and they also approved them.
EDCS-26601005.summary: This inscription records the construction of public works, including statues and arches, by two quinquennial duovirs, likely in a Roman colony or municipality.

EDCS-26601008.summary: This is a tombstone inscription identifying an individual by name and filiation.

EDCS-26601011.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name and age of a deceased individual.

EDCS-26601015.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone inscription that identifies an individual by their filiation and father's name.

EDCS-27700299.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely records names and possibly affiliations or relationships.

EDCS-28300270.translation: Iulius Iuda made this for his well-deserving wife Iulia Maria, with whom he lived for 25 years.
EDCS-28300270.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name of the deceased, her husband, and the duration of their marriage.

EDCS-28600187.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae Cleopatrae filiae dulcissimae quae vixit nnos II menses IIII dies IIII Pontianus et Terti in pace\n
EDCS-28600187.translation: To the good memory of Cleopatra, my sweetest daughter, who lived 2 years, 4 months, and 4 days. Pontianus and Tertius in peace.
EDCS-28600187.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Cleopatra, set up by her parents, Pontianus and Tertius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29900183.text_edition: \n tio Luci filio Falerna Ceriali duoviro geniores\n
EDCS-29900183.translation: ...to his son Lucius Falernus Cerialis, duovir.
EDCS-29900183.summary: This fragmentary inscription refers to an individual named Lucius Falernus Cerialis, who held the office of duovir.

EDCS-29900205.translation: Cornib[us] Augusta testifies that the sacred rites of Diana have been received back and that the temple's path is open.
EDCS-29900205.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription related to the goddess Diana, possibly recording the restoration or reopening of her temple or rites.

EDCS-30400548.translation: Doliare ware from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, belonging to Lucius Munatius Crescens, under the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.
EDCS-30400548.summary: This inscription records doliare ware from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, identified as belonging to Lucius Munatius Crescens and dated by the consuls Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-30700140.translation: The Aurelii Hilarus and Gaiana, prefects of the station of the patrimony of our Augusti.
EDCS-30700140.summary: This inscription names Aurelius Hilarus and Aurelia Gaiana as prefects of a station related to the imperial patrimony, likely dating to the joint rule of two emperors.

EDCS-31600183.translation: Doliare ware from the Licinian estates of the servants of Myrtilus Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600183.summary: This inscription records doliare ware from the Licinian estates, associated with the slaves of Myrtilus Domitia Lucilla, and dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600222.translation: Doliare ware from the Licinian estates of Domitia Lucilla, belonging to Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, under the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600222.summary: This inscription records doliare ware from the Licinian estates of Domitia Lucilla, attributed to Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, and dated by the consuls Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31600223.translation: Doliare ware from the Licinian estates of the servants of Myrtilus Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600223.summary: This inscription records doliare ware from the Licinian estates, associated with the slaves of Myrtilus Domitia Lucilla, and dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700104.translation: Doliare ware from the workshops of our Caesar, belonging to Caius Calpetanius Hermetis, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700104.summary: This inscription records doliare ware from imperial workshops, belonging to Caius Calpetanius Hermetis, and dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31800016.summary: This inscription appears to be a tombstone or memorial for a person named Benignus, with a numerical notation possibly indicating an age or quantity.

EDCS-32301030.translation: Salarese ware from the estates of Arminius Cestianus, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-32301030.summary: This inscription records Salarese ware from the estates of Arminius Cestianus, dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-32800015.summary: This inscription likely records the consulship of a person named Sisenna, possibly as part of a larger list or dedication.

EDCS-32800185.translation: From the workshops of Domitia Domitiana Sulpicianis, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-32800185.summary: This inscription records items from the workshops of Domitia Domitiana Sulpicianis, dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-32800237.translation: From the kilns of Domitia Domitiana of Sulpician, under consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-32800237.summary: This inscription notes the origin of a product from the kilns of Domitia Domitiana, dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-32803791.translation: Titus Curtilius, son of Titus, [---] to Sulcania [---], to Curtilia [---], to Atilia(?).
EDCS-32803791.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription naming several individuals, likely related, with the primary name being Titus Curtilius.

EDCS-32803797.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Priscus, my incomparable husband, with whom [---] for 8 months. Aiedia(?) [---] to my eternal wife of our marriage made [---] to Felici, my incomparable husband, with whom he lived for 16 years and 4 months. Aerennia [---] daughter of Percinus, wife, made [---] to my eternal conjugal faithfulness. [---]AVIS[---]VIN[---] to her brother, dearest, the sister placed.
EDCS-32803797.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription dedicated to two individuals, Priscus and Felici, described as incomparable husbands, and a wife Aerennia, likely a mother, with the dedications being made by wives and a sister.

EDCS-32803798.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Maltinia Hellas, his deserving wife, Caius Pomponius Client placed; my sweet life while I could be with the living above; now pious and composed. May the earth be light for me. My wife speaks of how dear I was to her husband; he loved me alive and dead. [He] made a home for you. Do not grieve, wife, it had to be done. My fate hastened it. My fate willed this. Husband, I give you this final tribute as a gift, and not enough. You alone knew how to maintain faithfulness, a holy woman. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-32803798.summary: This epitaph is dedicated by Caius Pomponius Client to his wife Maltinia Hellas, expressing deep love and lament, and includes poetic verses about their life together and wishes for the deceased.

EDCS-32803800.translation: Gaius Bruttius, son of Quintus, of the Voltinia tribe, for himself and his family. 12 feet in front, 12 feet in the field.
EDCS-32803800.summary: This is a property boundary marker, indicating the dimensions of a plot of land purchased by Gaius Bruttius for his family.

EDCS-32803803.translation: [---] to Mario Januari, his deserving husband [---]EM [---].
EDCS-32803803.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to a husband named Mario Januari.

EDCS-32803814.translation: To the departed spirits. To Farsuleia Iucunda, daughter of Lucius, and to Annius Severus, her son, [---] months [---]ISIA [---] Lusius [placed it].
EDCS-32803814.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Lusius to Farsuleia Iucunda and her son Annius Severus, mentioning their ages in months.

EDCS-32803815.translation: [---] to Marcus Helvius Sabinus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, and Asiaticus [---].
EDCS-32803815.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely funerary, mentioning Marcus Helvius Sabinus and Asiaticus.

EDCS-32803816.translation: Caninia He[---], freedwoman of Lucius, for herself and her family. Lucius Caninius A[---], freedman of the woman. Lucius Caninius A[---], freedman of the woman. Lucius Caninius Ru[---], freedman of Lucius, of the woman.
EDCS-32803816.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caninia, a freedwoman, and mentions Lucius Caninius multiple times, likely as her freedman and possibly others related to him.

EDCS-32803817.translation: Lucius Vireius Bachius, freedman of Lucius, lives. Favonia Surisca, freedwoman of the woman, died. Nunnia Cleopatra, freedwoman of Caius, lives.
EDCS-32803817.summary: This is a funerary inscription, possibly part of a family tomb, recording the lives and statuses of Lucius Vireius Bachius, Favonia Surisca, and Nunnia Cleopatra, noting who is alive and who has died.

EDCS-32803818.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of the deceased, Acte.

EDCS-32803824.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Caesia Amphale to her well-deserving husband, Gaius Titinius Amerimnus.
EDCS-32803824.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife to her well-deserving husband.

EDCS-32803826.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Mustia Romana, [my wife]... Here I lie, Romana, torn from my husband while young, chaste, modest, beloved by my dear husband. The years I lived were seven and twenty; add nine months for the span in which I completed my life. You, sweet wife, always remember me. Lenturius Honoratus [erected this].
EDCS-32803826.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Lenturius Honoratus for his wife Mustia Romana, who died at the age of 27 years and 9 months.

EDCS-35200337.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Toria Primitiva made this for her well-deserving father, Gaius Persula Stablus.
EDCS-35200337.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a daughter for her father.

EDCS-35500648.translation: Gaius Firmius Gallus, son of Gaius, twice-elected magistrate, paved the road to Thorrena from the altar of Silvanus to the plot of Tlennasis at his own expense and laid the curbs. // Gaius Firmius Gallus, son of Gaius, twice-elected magistrate, paved the road to Thorrena from the altar of Silvanus to the plot of Tlennasis at his own expense and laid the curbs.
EDCS-35500648.summary: This is a public works inscription recording the paving of a road and the installation of curbs by a local magistrate named Gaius Firmius Gallus.

EDCS-36100084.translation: Doliare work from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Flavius? Probianus?, under the consulship of Pontianus and Atilianus.
EDCS-36100084.summary: This is a production record for doliare (earthenware jars or amphorae) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, dated by consuls.

EDCS-36100192.translation: Doliary work of Doryphorus for Domitia Publi filia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-36100192.summary: This is a production record for doliare (earthenware jars or amphorae) associated with Doryphorus and Domitia Lucilla, dated by consuls.

EDCS-36100301.translation: Doliary work of Doryphorus for Domitia Publi filia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-36100301.summary: This is a production record for doliare (earthenware jars or amphorae) associated with Doryphorus and Domitia Lucilla, dated by consuls.

EDCS-36100305.translation: Of Gnaeus Domitius Trophimus, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-36100305.summary: This inscription records the name of Gnaeus Domitius Trophimus and is dated by consuls, likely relating to a production or administrative record.

EDCS-36100318.translation: Doliary work of Dionysius for Domitia Publi filia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-36100318.summary: This is a production record for doliare (earthenware jars or amphorae) associated with Dionysius and Domitia Lucilla, dated by consuls.

EDCS-36900463.translation: To Attia Mulsula, daughter of Titus.
EDCS-36900463.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Attia Mulsula, daughter of Titus.

EDCS-37400030.translation: Lucius [---]rius, Lucius' freedman, Philodespotus; [---]us Mercurialis(?); Quarta; Glaphyra(?); [---]cessa. 20 feet in the front, in the field. This monument will not follow its heir.
EDCS-37400030.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription lists several names and provides dimensions for a plot of land, indicating it is a tomb with a stipulation regarding inheritance, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-37801307.translation: Minervinus, a runaway of Italicus, soldier of the twelfth urban cohort's standard-bearer.
EDCS-37801307.summary: This inscription identifies Minervinus as a runaway belonging to a soldier in an urban cohort.

EDCS-38301906.translation: From the workshops of Quintus Asinius Marcellus; tiling work of Caius Nunnidius Fortunatus, when Paetinus and Apronianus were consuls.
EDCS-38301906.summary: This inscription records tiling work by Caius Nunnidius Fortunatus, originating from the workshops of Quintus Asinius Marcellus, dated by the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus (likely 193 CE or 201 CE).

EDCS-39200599.translation: Here lies Caruventis, who enclosed all graces and reason within; most beloved by his parents. And this stone, thrice blessed, holding so much good.
EDCS-39200599.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram for Caruventis, praising his virtues and expressing deep affection from his parents, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-41700140.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, father of the country, Augustan [---] of Perusia.
EDCS-41700140.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, mentioning his titles and likely indicating a connection to the colony of Perusia, probably from his second consulship (242 CE).

EDCS-41700203.translation: Lucens(?), citizen, to Aurelius Probus. Emperor Augustus, Marcus Aurelius Laevus, proconsul within the Gallias, [---] of the Praetorian cohorts' legionary barracks, restored the walls on two sides.
EDCS-41700203.summary: This inscription records the restoration of walls of the Praetorian cohort barracks in Gaul by Marcus Aurelius Laevus, proconsul, likely during the reign of an emperor named Augustus (possibly Commodus or Septimius Severus).

EDCS-41800270.translation: Tiling work of Oppius Justus, when Paetinus and Apronianus were consuls.
EDCS-41800270.summary: This inscription records tiling work by Oppius Justus, dated by the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus (likely 193 CE or 201 CE).

EDCS-41800273.translation: Tiling work of Oppius Justus, when Paetinus and Apronianus were consuls.
EDCS-41800273.summary: This inscription records tiling work by Oppius Justus, dated by the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus (likely 193 CE or 201 CE).

EDCS-42300072.translation: From the estates of Quintus Servilius Pudentis, under the care of Hedy, the slave, when Hiberus and Sisenna were consuls.
EDCS-42300072.summary: This inscription records goods from the estates of Quintus Servilius Pudentis, managed by his slave Hedy, dated by the consulship of Hiberus and Sisenna (likely 205 CE or 216 CE).

EDCS-43400283.translation: Fructus of Veientus willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-43400283.summary: This is a votive dedication by Fructus, likely from a shrine or offering, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-44100311.translation: Of Sabinus Libo and Iulia Procla.
EDCS-44100311.summary: This inscription likely records the names of donors or owners of a property, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-45900134.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Prima, his wife, [placed this for] Titus Iulius Eytycanus, his well-deserving freedman.
EDCS-45900134.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedman named Titus Iulius Eytycanus, set up by his wife Prima, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-45900135.translation: Lucretia Laudice, a freedwoman, lived 65 years.
EDCS-45900135.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Lucretia Laudice, a freedwoman, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-45900136.translation: Pomponia Posilla, daughter of Gaius, lived 20 years.
EDCS-45900136.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Pomponia Posilla, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-45900183.translation: Gaius Vergilius Diomedes, freedman of the freedmen, lived 63 years.
EDCS-45900183.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Gaius Vergilius Diomedes, a freedman, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-46600936.translation: Parents made [this] for their most sweet daughter Mariae Restitutae, who lived 5 years, 8 months, and 25 days.
EDCS-46600936.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Mariae Restitutae, set up by her parents, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-46600938.translation: Ticrinus and A[...], for their son T[...], lived 11 years, [X] months, on the Ides of June. Peace [be with you?].
EDCS-46600938.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a son named T., set up by Ticrinus and A., dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-46600949.translation: This is a burial place, enclosed by a wall. Plaetoria Hermais made it from her funds for herself and for her patron Marcus Plaetorius Firmus, her most excellent and most indulgent husband, and for her son Marcus Plaetorius Saturninus, most dutiful, and for her freedman Marcus Plaetorius Successus, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-46600949.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription detailing the burial arrangements and beneficiaries made by Plaetoria Hermais for her family and freedmen, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-47001247.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a dedication or ownership marker, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-47001248.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the nomen of Lucius Plotius Porcius.

EDCS-47001252.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the nomen of Lucius Plotius Porcius.

EDCS-47001253.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the nomen of Lucius Plotius Porcius.

EDCS-47400396.translation: Aulus Cutius, son of Aulus, of the Tromentina tribe, born in Pisentia, from Hastia.
EDCS-47400396.summary: This is a personal funerary inscription identifying Aulus Cutius, his father, tribe, and place of origin.

EDCS-47500003.translation: Of Lucius Tussanius, son of Lucius, from Tarentum.
EDCS-47500003.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name and origin of Lucius Tussanius.

EDCS-47900109.translation: Of Caius Fulvius Plautianus, praetorian prefect, clarissimus vir, consul twice. To...?
EDCS-47900109.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Caius Fulvius Plautianus, a high-ranking official, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-47900139.translation: By the authority of Quintus Iunius Rusticus, urban prefect.
EDCS-47900139.summary: This inscription records an action taken under the authority of Quintus Iunius Rusticus, the urban prefect.

EDCS-47900152.translation: Of Felix, son of Marcus Iunius Homullus.
EDCS-47900152.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name of Felix and his father, Marcus Iunius Homullus.

EDCS-47900405.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning Marcus Antonius, identified as imperator.

EDCS-47900407.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the name Fulvia and a lacuna.

EDCS-47900409.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to record the name of Gaius Caesar, possibly on a monument dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-47900410.translation: Hail, Octavius, may you be well
EDCS-47900410.summary: This is a short, salutatory inscription mentioning the name Octavius, likely a personal dedication or greeting.

EDCS-47900411.summary: This inscription identifies Octavius and associates him with the Tenth Legion Equestris, possibly indicating military service.

EDCS-47900416.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication to Apidus, identified as a primus pilus (first spear, a high-ranking centurion).

EDCS-47900418.translation: Of Apidus, of the first spear of the Sixth Legion
EDCS-47900418.summary: This inscription names Apidus, a primus pilus (first spear, a high-ranking centurion) of the Sixth Legion.

EDCS-47900421.translation: Marcus Aninius of the Seventh Legion
EDCS-47900421.summary: This inscription records the name Marcus Aninius and his service in the Seventh Legion.

EDCS-47900422.translation: The Eleventh Legion, [serving] the Divine Julius
EDCS-47900422.summary: This inscription refers to the Eleventh Legion and its association with Julius Caesar (Divus Iulius).

EDCS-47900429.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription beginning with the name Decimus Tad(ius), with the remainder illegible.

EDCS-47900448.summary: This inscription seems to be a dedication to Faustinus, mentioning a vilicus (an overseer or steward).

EDCS-47900451.summary: This inscription begins with the name Icarus, followed by illegible text.

EDCS-47900470.translation: For Apolaustus, freedman of Quintus Planus Truttedius Pi.
EDCS-47900470.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Apolaustus, a freedman of Quintus Planus Truttedius Pi.

EDCS-47900482.translation: For Dracontis, freedman of Quintus Planus Truttedius Pi.
EDCS-47900482.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Dracontis, a freedman of Quintus Planus Truttedius Pi.

EDCS-47900759.translation: By authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, urban prefect.
EDCS-47900759.summary: This inscription records an action taken by Quintus Junius Rusticus, urban prefect, likely related to a legal or administrative matter.

EDCS-47900760.translation: By authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, urban prefect, for four pounds.
EDCS-47900760.summary: This inscription documents an authorization by Quintus Junius Rusticus, urban prefect, concerning a quantity of four pounds, possibly for payment or assessment.

EDCS-47900761.translation: By authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, for four pounds.
EDCS-47900761.summary: This inscription indicates an authorization by Quintus Junius Rusticus regarding a quantity of four pounds.

EDCS-47900762.translation: By authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, urban prefect, for three ounces and eight scruples.
EDCS-47900762.summary: This inscription records an authorization by Quintus Junius Rusticus, urban prefect, for a specific weight of three ounces and eight scruples.

EDCS-47900806.translation: For Callidorus, freedman of Quintus Calvisius Sabinus, a distinguished man.
EDCS-47900806.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Callidorus, a freedman of the distinguished Quintus Calvisius Sabinus.

EDCS-47900860.translation: Of Sextus Sabucius Sabinus, a distinguished man.
EDCS-47900860.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or memorial made by or for Sextus Sabucius Sabinus, a distinguished man.

EDCS-47900877.summary: This is a dedication or funerary marker for Veturia Gratilla.

EDCS-47901017.translation: To Hercules Augustus, may you always be victorious.
EDCS-47901017.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Hercules Augustus.

EDCS-48000292.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Fadius Secundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48000920.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Munatius Adiectus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48000993.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Munatius Adiectus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48500216.translation: Publius Sulpicius Mundus, son of Publius, quaestor, aedile, praetor, proconsul, curator of roads, legatus Augusti pro praetore, by his will, through the arbitration of Lucius Aufustius.
EDCS-48500216.summary: This inscription records the career of Publius Sulpicius Mundus and indicates a public work or endowment made in accordance with his will, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400958.translation: Cornelia Murra, freedwoman of (a woman), made this for herself and her family.
EDCS-51400958.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Murra, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400959.translation: For the health of Tiberius Caesar, son of the deified Augustus, grandson of the deified Julius, Augustus, pontifex maximus, consul V, imperator VIII, with tribunician power XXXVI, and of the nephews, of Gaius Caesar Germanicus, son of Germanicus, quaestor, and of Tiberius Caesar, son of Drusus, (they dedicated) a quercus crown of 10 pounds of gold and a gilded chair with ornaments, from their own money. They dedicated and paid their vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina. Marcus Tarquitius Priscus, haruspex of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, IIIIvir iure dicundo, prefect of the soldiers, and Etruscus, son of [Marcus Tarquitius Priscus], IIIIvir iure dicundo, tribune of the soldiers, and prefect of the soldiers, and on the same day of dedication, they gave a feast to the decurions and a cake and mulsum to the plebs. Aurelius Gloriosus, laudabilis puer, master, and curator, in the 12th indiction, restored the bath, which was of private ownership of the citizens of Tarquinii, consumed by long neglect and the decay of time, with his own money, under the administration of Projecticius, his most excellent father, and imminent Serbanus, an honorable man, and returned it for public use, with Cethegus, a most excellent man, as consul.
EDCS-51400959.summary: This extensive inscription details the dedication of offerings and a feast for the imperial family and citizens, along with the restoration of a public bath in Tarquinii, likely during the reign of Tiberius or later.

EDCS-51400960.translation: To Hercules and the Unconquered Springs. Gaius Sulpicius Liscus of the Sequani, freedman, paid his vow willingly and deservedly on the day he descended.
EDCS-51400960.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicating offerings to Hercules and springs, likely by a freedman, possibly related to a healing ritual, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51900474.translation: To the deified Marcus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus, Sarmaticus, Germanicus, Maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power VII, imperator IIII.
EDCS-51900474.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the deified Emperor Marcus Aurelius, likely part of a monument erected during the reign of his son Commodus.

EDCS-52200603.translation: Pottery work from the estates of Lucilla. Caricus Felix made (this) under consuls Pontianus and Atilianus.
EDCS-52200603.summary: This is a production inscription for pottery, indicating the producer, source of materials, and the consuls of the year, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-52602768.translation: To Popilia Severina, who lived 28 years, 5 months, and 19 days, and to Tettenia Severina, with whom he lived 32 years. Gaius Popilius Festus made (this) for his well-deserving daughters and wife.
EDCS-52602768.summary: This is a funerary monument for Popilia Severina and Tettenia Severina, set up by Gaius Popilius Festus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-53200228.translation: Names of the guild of carpenters: patrons Quintus Nunnius Montanus, Quintus Valerius Proculus, Quintus Valerius Proculus the son, [Fabricus Festus], [Terentius Genialis], Quintus Pompeius Festus, [Fisidus Proculus], Tiberius Claudius Felix, [Sextus Laberius Lupus], [Cassius Festus], [Volumnius Tacitus], Tiberius Claudius Symphor, [Aquilius Eglectus], [Numisius Cornelianus], [Numisius Tacitus, father of the guild, holder of a bisellium]. Decurions: Aulus Octavius Faustus, Lucius Aninius Faustus, Marcus Vipsanius Polybius, Marcus Livius Aptus, Publius Aquillius Soterichus, Titus Flavius Proculus, Quintus Albatius Corinthus the haruspex, Quintus Albatius Verna the scribe, Lucius Tettius Glycon the doctor, Lucius Tettius Apollonius the doctor, Gaius Julius Italicus, Gaius Julius Matho. [Tiberius] ..., [ Titus] ..., [Marcus] ..., [Sextus] ..., [Sextus] ..., [Publius] ..., [Gaius A...] ..., [Titus C...] ..., [Marcus C...] ..., Eur[...], [Gaius Ca...] ..., [Publius Sa...] ..., [Gaius Vi...].
EDCS-53200228.summary: This is a list of names, likely members and officials, of the guild of carpenters, possibly including patrons and decurions, with some individuals holding special statuses like 'father of the guild' or 'haruspex'.

EDCS-55702079.translation: To the divine shades. Ianuaria made this for her brother Ianuarius. He lived 18 years and ... days.
EDCS-55702079.summary: A funerary dedication from a sister to her deceased brother, providing his name and age at death.

EDCS-56500176.translation: Of Marcus Flavius Salvensis, son of Quintus.
EDCS-56500176.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication or ownership marking for an individual named Marcus Flavius Salvensis.

EDCS-56500181.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, possibly part of a funerary monument or dedication.

EDCS-56800020.text_edition: \n ------] tia Pro Baebia Athicte Valeria Sabina Terentia Tacita Cornelia Polla Grata \n
EDCS-56800020.translation: Pro Baebia Athicte, Valeria Sabina, Terentia Tacita, Cornelia Polla Grata.
EDCS-56800020.summary: A list of names, likely women, possibly associated with a funerary context or a donation.

EDCS-57100003.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief pontiff, in his second tribunician power, consul, father of the country, our best and greatest prince, the federated people of Capena, devoted to his divine power and majesty.
EDCS-57100003.summary: A dedication from the federated people of Capena to Emperor Gordian III, recognizing his titles and authority, likely on a public building or monument.

EDCS-58300003.translation: To Lucius Marcius Aprion, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe, who held the office of aedile twice and duumvir, and was responsible for the alimentary program, a place was granted by decree of the decurions and the seviri Augustales (both men and women) of Feroniae and the people of the colony Iulia Felix Lucus Feroniae. Dedicated on the Kalends of January in the consulship of Gaius Erucius Quintus Sosius Falcone, with Quintus Vibius Paris in charge.
EDCS-58300003.summary: A dedication granting a public burial plot to Lucius Marcius Aprion of Lucus Feroniae, honoring his public service including alimentary provisions, and dating the grant.

EDCS-59900018.summary: A very fragmentary inscription with the name of Marcus Antonius, likely an emperor.

EDCS-59900019.summary: A fragmentary inscription identifying 'Caesar' as Emperor, possibly referring to a specific ruler.

EDCS-59900022.summary: A fragmentary inscription referring to an emperor named Rufus.

EDCS-59900023.summary: This inscription records the name Rufus, identified as an emperor, likely on a building or monument.

EDCS-59900024.translation: Titus Etruscus, primus pilus of the Fourth Legion
EDCS-59900024.summary: This inscription identifies Titus Etruscus, a primus pilus (centurion of the first cohort) of the Fourth Legion, possibly on a tombstone or military dedication.

EDCS-59900025.translation: Apidius, primus pilus of the Sixth Legion
EDCS-59900025.summary: This inscription names Apidius, a primus pilus of the Sixth Legion, likely on a military dedication or tombstone.

EDCS-59900026.summary: This inscription records the name Apidius, identified as a primus pilus, probably on a tombstone or military monument.

EDCS-59900027.summary: This inscription names Apidius as a primus pilus, likely on a tombstone or military dedication.

EDCS-59900028.summary: This inscription contains the abbreviated names Gaius Po[...] and Ul[...], possibly a personal dedication or identification.

EDCS-59900029.summary: This inscription refers to Felix, identified as Emperor Caesar, likely on a monument or official document.

EDCS-59900030.summary: This inscription names Felix as Emperor Caesar, likely on a monument or official document.

EDCS-59900040.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus, possibly with a reference to Augustus, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-60000051.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To the most loving father, Ducenus Severianus. Severinus and Dionysias, to one who deserved well.
EDCS-60000051.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the Dis Manibus, dedicating a tomb to Ducenus Severianus by his children Severinus and Dionysias, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-60000052.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Apollinaris, six-man official of Sulpicius Festus.
EDCS-60000052.summary: A funerary dedication to Sulpicius Festus by Apollinaris, who held a priestly or civic office.

EDCS-60500016.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Erotianus made this for her brother P. Aelius Hermippus, dearest brother. He lived for 4 years, 7 months, and 14 days.
EDCS-60500016.summary: A funerary dedication for a young boy, Erotianus, by his brother, detailing his age at death.
