EDCS-60700083.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Caenii Iulianus and Caelianus, his sons, made this for their father Barbula, son of L. Caenius, a man of rare innocence, and for his well-deserving wife. [Agathemeris?], his wife.
EDCS-60700083.summary: A funerary dedication by sons for their father and mother, praising the father's character.

EDCS-60700137.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XII imperator VI consul V pater patriae viam novam Traianam a Volsiniis ad fines Clusinorum fecit XVII ddomini nnnostri Constantius et Maximianus Auggusti et Diocletianus et Maximianus nniores Auggusti et Severus et Maximinus nobbilissimi Caessares et milia XVII Bono generis humani creati Impperatores ddomini nnostri Constantini sic <et Licinius> perpetui semper Augusti XVII\n
EDCS-60700137.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the divine Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 12th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the new Trajanic road from Volsinii to the borders of Clusium. In the 17th year of our Lords Constantius and Maximianus Augusti and Diocletian and Maximianus the Younger Augusti and Severus and Maximinus, most noble Caesars, and in the 17th year of our Lords Constantinus and Licinius, perpetual always Augusti, for the benefit of mankind.
EDCS-60700137.summary: An inscription dedicating a new road constructed under various emperors, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE, and commissioned by Emperor Trajan.

EDCS-60800046.translation: To Gaius Marcius, son of Lucius Marcius, Rusticus. [---] Marcia [---] [---]
EDCS-60800046.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication for Gaius Marcius Rusticus, with additional partially legible names.

EDCS-61300030.translation: For Tullia Marcilla, daughter of Publius, of the Quentinia gens, Rufina, for Rufia Procula, a most distinguished woman.
EDCS-61300030.summary: An epitaph for Tullia Marcilla Rufina, identified by multiple names and familial/tribal affiliations, honored as a distinguished woman.

EDCS-61300046.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Aemilia Fortunata made this for her well-deserving husband, Lucius Antonius Ampiatus.
EDCS-61300046.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife for her husband, Lucius Antonius Ampiatus.

EDCS-61300047.translation: To the shades of the underworld. For Nicephorus, slave of Lucius Aemilius Callimachus, his fellow colleague, who deserved well. They made this. He lived for about 35 years.
EDCS-61300047.summary: A funerary dedication for Nicephorus, a slave and colleague, by his associates, mentioning his age at death.

EDCS-61300051.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Marcus Efficius Fortunatus made this for himself and for his dearest wife Lucatia Tyche, who lived with me for 30 years, and also for my sons and their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. In front 10 feet, in the field 20 feet.
EDCS-61300051.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Efficius Fortunatus, his wife, children, and freedmen, with land dimensions for a tomb complex.

EDCS-61300055.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Masculinius Titianus the younger made this for his most beloved parents.
EDCS-61300055.summary: A funerary dedication by a son, Masculinius Titianus the younger, for his parents.

EDCS-61300056.translation: To Masculinius Titianus. Aemilia Afrodite [made this] for her incomparable husband, who lived 44 years, 11 months, and 17 days.
EDCS-61300056.summary: A funerary epitaph from an unknown location, dedicated by a wife to her husband, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-61900004.translation: Gnaeus Laberius, son of Aulus, of the Pomptina tribe. To Aulus Haprnius, son of Aulus.
EDCS-61900004.summary: A funerary epitaph from Ostia, recording a dedication from Gnaeus Laberius to Aulus Haprnius, likely from the 1st century BCE to 1st century CE.

EDCS-63800143.summary: A fragmentary inscription from an unknown location, likely a funerary epitaph, identifying a servant of Clodius Lunensis, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-64000155.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Porcia Hyle made this for her dearest husband, Lucius Laberius Felix, freedman of Lucius, an oil merchant.
EDCS-64000155.summary: A funerary epitaph from Ostia, dedicated by a wife to her husband, an oil merchant, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-64400236.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Here lies Iulius Clemensianus, who lived 73 years. He made [this] with his wife, with whom he lived 43 years. He departed in peace on the 12th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-64400236.summary: A funerary epitaph from an unknown location, recording the lifespan and death date of Iulius Clemensianus and his wife, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-64400251.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sextus Murrius Paezon [made this] for his most dutiful father, Sextus Murrius Pru[nic]us.
EDCS-64400251.summary: A funerary epitaph from an unknown location, recording a dedication from a son to his father, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-64400252.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Meclo Constitutus [made this] for his deserving son, Gaius Vessius Valens, the magistrate.
EDCS-64400252.summary: A funerary epitaph from an unknown location, recording a dedication from a father to his son, who held the office of magistrate, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-64400253.summary: A funerary epitaph from an unknown location, recording the lifespan of Lucius Hirrius Doryphorus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-64400254.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Larcia Verulla, who lived 13 years, 3 months, and 7 days. Her parents [made this] for their most dutiful daughter, who deserved well.
EDCS-64400254.summary: A funerary epitaph from an unknown location, dedicated by parents to their young daughter, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-64400255.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Pullius Servandus [made this] for his wife, Rutilia Felicula, who deserved well of him.
EDCS-64400255.summary: A funerary epitaph from an unknown location, dedicated by a husband to his wife, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-64400531.translation: To the departed spirits. Thrania Hermione made this for her well-deserving husband, Tiberius Claudius Amandus.
EDCS-64400531.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife for her husband, Tiberius Claudius Amandus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400532.translation: To the departed spirits. Thrania Hermione made this for her most dutiful, well-deserving son, Marcus Aponius Amandus.
EDCS-64400532.summary: A funerary dedication by a mother, Thrania Hermione, for her son, Marcus Aponius Amandus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400533.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Virasius Venusius made this for his most dutiful brother, Publius Virasius Hermogenes.
EDCS-64400533.summary: A funerary dedication by a brother, Publius Virasius Venusius, for his sibling, Publius Virasius Hermogenes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400534.translation: To the departed spirits. Made for Primigenia, well-deserving.
EDCS-64400534.summary: A funerary dedication for Primigenia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500129.translation: Publius Eppius Eureticus made this, while alive, for himself.
EDCS-64500129.summary: A funerary or sepulchral dedication by Publius Eppius Eureticus for himself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500132.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianvs, the Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, chief pontiff, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-64500132.summary: An honorific inscription for Emperor Maximian, recording his titles and honors, likely from the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd to early 4th century CE).

EDCS-64500134.translation: To the departed spirits. [---]misi, [---] Nonas, for himself and [---]ori his [---]
EDCS-64500134.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication, possibly for the deceased and a surviving individual, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500365.translation: Caelia Rufina made this for her foster-daughter, who lived 16 years.
EDCS-64500365.summary: A funerary dedication by Caelia Rufina for her foster-daughter, aged 16, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500379.translation: Lucius Tettius Fortunatus made this, while alive, for me(?) and for Tettia Felici, his contubernalis.
EDCS-64500379.summary: A funerary or sepulchral dedication by Lucius Tettius Fortunatus for himself and his female companion (contubernalis), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500384.translation: Sacred to Augustus's spring. Aulus Vibius Aper gave this offering.
EDCS-64500384.summary: A dedication to a sacred spring named 'Augustus's spring' by Aulus Vibius Aper, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500501.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Caius Sulpicius Serenus, his well-deserving wife Sulpicia Hilaritas made this.
EDCS-64500501.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife to her husband, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-64900862.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. His wife made this for Ulpius Candido. To Candidus the African.
EDCS-64900862.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife to her husband, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-64901012.translation: freedman of Ianius... freedman of Marcus... Chrestus... freedman of Marcus... freedwoman... five years.
EDCS-64901012.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription possibly recording the names of freedmen and their ages, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65400061.summary: A very brief inscription, possibly a dedication or ownership mark, mentioning two individuals, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65800122.translation: When Gnaeus Domitius Trophimus was consul with Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-65800122.summary: A dating formula mentioning the consuls Gnaeus Domitius Trophimus, Paetinus, and Apronianus, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-66200012.translation: Lucius Aveius Attianus, son of Lucius, made this for his mother Sulpicia Polla, daughter of Sextus, and for his wife Valeria, daughter of Publius.
EDCS-66200012.summary: A funerary monument erected by a son for his mother and wife, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-67000014.translation: Marcus Antonius Neanthus, Augustalis, made this of his own accord and at his own expense, by decree of the decurions, free of charge.
EDCS-67000014.summary: An inscription recording an honor bestowed upon Marcus Antonius Neanthus, an Augustalis, who funded a public work himself, likely from an Italian town during the Imperial period.

EDCS-67000017.summary: A dedication or maker's inscription by Marcus Hortorius Thalamus, possibly related to a building or artwork, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-67200476.translation: adlected... and... Callistus... and... Crescens... and... Capito and... Diadumenus... and... Philippus... and...anthus... and...nius adlected... mens adlected... verus adlected...mus and... Treptus... and...nus and... Epaphroditus... Glaphyr adlected... ens and...hodus... genius adlected...ena and... Silvius... and...eus and... Euhemer... Marcellus adlected... Publius Aufidius... Lucius Pomentinus and Publius Rubrius... Publius Veturus rus and Publius Annius Fortunatus, Publius Annius Annianus adlected, Gaius Paetieus Hilarianus and Titus Flavius Primitivos, Lucius Numisius Delphicus adlected, Aulus Egrilius Sabinus and Gaius Julius Fortunatus, Gnaeus Apuleius Verus and Marcus Farranius Hermes, Gnaeus Turpilius Agathopus adlected, Gnaeus Turpilius Gnesius junior adlected, Marcus Aemilius... Publius Pompon... Quintus Patr... Titus Valerius Agathanus adlected, Quintus Cannus... and Sextus Sestius Pyramus, Lucius Sentius Hermes and Lucius Aristius Amemptus, Lucius Aristius Thallus adlected, Sextus Apuleius Veratius Decrius Auctus quinquennales, Quintus Valerius Zosimus and Gaius Popilius Phoebus, Titus Carvilius Olympicus adlected.
EDCS-67200476.summary: A very fragmentary inscription listing numerous names, many marked as 'adlected' (selected or appointed to a role), possibly related to a civic or administrative body, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-67200479.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Euphrates, Grania Asia.
EDCS-67200479.summary: A fragmented funerary dedication mentioning two individuals, possibly a couple or family members, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-68000013.translation: To the Divine Shades. Tyrannus, slave of Gaius Sulpicius Festus, to his beloved fellow slave, well-deserving.
EDCS-68000013.summary: This funerary epitaph, dating to the Roman Imperial period, records the devotion of a slave to his deceased partner.

EDCS-69500150.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Ulpius Decrianus the elder made this for Marcus Ulpius Decrianus.
EDCS-69500150.summary: This Roman funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, records a father setting up a tomb for his son with the same name.

EDCS-70300306.translation: The entrance to the gymnasium of the young men of Lucoferia, along with all its roofing tiles and beams, was constructed from his own funds by the donor for his fellow young men. [---] He supervised the gymnasium for the young men under the curatorship of the esteemed Pompeianus and Geminius Felix, on the 21st day before the Kalends of May, in the consulship of Sabinus II and Anullinus.
EDCS-70300306.summary: This inscription from the Roman Imperial period records the construction of a gymnasium entrance by a benefactor for the local youth, including details of the officials and consuls.

EDCS-70700156.translation: Titus Flavius Euphrosynus, son of Titus, from the Arnensian tribe, a sevir Augustalis, aedile, quaestor of provisions, and duovir, during the consulship of Petronius Mamertinus and Tineius Rufus, with Quintus Vibius Paris and Publius Licinius Valentinus as curators.
EDCS-70700156.summary: This Roman inscription from the Imperial period lists the various magistracies held by Titus Flavius Euphrosynus, noting his service as duovir during a specific consulship.

EDCS-70700157.translation: Publius Aelius Aphrodisius, son of Publius, duovir with aedilician power, quaestor of provisions, and one of the Seviri Augustales, remitted the expenses after receiving the honour, for the women of Feron, the people, during the consulship of Lucius Bruttii Crispinus and Lucius Roscio Aelianus.
EDCS-70700157.summary: This inscription from the Roman Imperial period records Publius Aelius Aphrodisius, a local magistrate, remitting costs associated with an honour granted to him by the town of Feron.

EDCS-70800042.summary: This fragmentary Roman funerary epitaph records the name of a child, Cosconius, and their age at death.

EDCS-70800044.translation: Fisevia, daughter of Tiberius, lived 80 years.
EDCS-70800044.summary: This Roman funerary epitaph records the name and age at death of Fisevia, daughter of Tiberius.

EDCS-70800127.translation: To the Divine Shades. [---] Ulpius, of the Legion(?), who lived 45 years, 6 months, and 11 days. Ulpius Philomathes(?) and Ulpia Aprotes(?) made this monument for their dearest father. This monument shall not bind the heir.
EDCS-70800127.summary: This Roman funerary epitaph, likely from the Imperial period, records the death details of Ulpius and commemorates him with a monument that does not pass to his heir.

EDCS-70800225.translation: Publius Antistius Eros, freedman of Publius.
EDCS-70800225.summary: This Roman inscription from the Imperial period records the name and relationship (freedman) of Publius Antistius Eros.

EDCS-70800233.summary: This Roman funerary epitaph records the name of Lollia Procula and her age at death.

EDCS-70800234.translation: To Clodius Feliximus, who lived 65 years. Capr(i)ola made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-70800234.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Capr(i)ola to her husband Clodius Feliximus.

EDCS-70800316.summary: This dedication is made to the goddess Ceres.

EDCS-70900765.summary: This funerary monument is for [3]inia Hillaria.

EDCS-70900834.summary: This funerary monument is for Vettia Pieris.

EDCS-70900844.summary: This funerary monument is for Albia Fortuna.

EDCS-70900845.translation: Of Lucius Tussanius Niger, son of Sextus, from Clustumina.
EDCS-70900845.summary: This is a funerary monument for Lucius Tussanius Niger.

EDCS-70900997.translation: Sacred to Victoria. Lucius Perperna, son of Quintus; Gaius Terentius, son of Aulus; Gaius Gavius, son of Gaius Ru[...], the three men in charge. Sacred to the August Emperors' Concord.
EDCS-70900997.summary: This dedication likely records a group of officials dedicating something to Victoria and the Concord of the Emperors.

EDCS-70901075.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Gaius Fuficius Trophimus, freedman of a woman.
EDCS-70901075.summary: This funerary monument is dedicated to Gaius Fuficius Trophimus.

EDCS-71000443.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, grandson of the deified Severus, son of the deified Augustus Antoninus Pius, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, father of the country. The public community of Hebanus, devoted to his divine majesty and power, placed this and saw to its erection by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-71000443.summary: This is a dedication by the community of Hebanus to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, recording his titles and dignities.

EDCS-71000459.translation: On the Nones of April(?), roses completed. Iulius Epagatus, C[3]nius Ful[vus](?), [3]ius [3], Cominius Faust[us] fulfilled their vow willingly(?). On the twelfth day before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-71000459.summary: This inscription records individuals fulfilling a vow, possibly related to a floral offering, on specific dates.

EDCS-71000461.translation: Greetings? To Secundus, son of B...
EDCS-71000461.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication or greeting, possibly to a person named Secundus.

EDCS-71000464.translation: Gaius Antistius Naso, son of Gaius, and Apate, freedwoman of a woman (named Fabia), wife of Dionysius, freedman of Faustus, a doctor, made this.
EDCS-71000464.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Apate for her husband Dionysius, who was a freedman and a doctor, commissioned by Gaius Antistius Naso.

EDCS-71000468.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription bearing the name and filiation of Lucius Larcius, son of Marcus.

EDCS-71000469.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name and father of Quintus Purpuro.

EDCS-71000472.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription bearing the name and filiation of Publius Mituleius, son of Marcus.

EDCS-71000473.translation: Titus Hatilius Philod[---], freedman of Caius.
EDCS-71000473.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription for Titus Hatilius Philod[---], a freedman of Caius.

EDCS-71000474.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription bearing the name Lucius Manlius.

EDCS-71000476.translation: M. Hilarus, son of C. Caecilius...
EDCS-71000476.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording a person named Hilarus, son of C. Caecilius.

EDCS-71000477.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription bearing the name Marcus Caecilius Marcasus(?).

EDCS-71000679.translation: Emperor Hadrianus Augustus to the Pyrgenses.
EDCS-71000679.summary: This inscription records a decree or dedication by Emperor Hadrian to the people of Pyrgi.

EDCS-71000740.translation: To [---], son of Marcus, of the Horatia tribe, [---] quaestor [---] by testament, by the arbitration of Philadespotus [---] from 50,000 sesterces.
EDCS-71000740.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a bequest from a man who died leaving a sum of money for a public official.

EDCS-71100136.translation: To the Great and Unconquered Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian, the Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune for the third time, Consul for the second time, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, the council and people of Luna, devoted to his divine majesty and authority.
EDCS-71100136.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, likely set up by the local community of Luna, dating to his third tribunician power and second consulship.

EDCS-71100137.translation: To Marcus Turtellius Rufus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe.
EDCS-71100137.summary: This is a dedication to a man named Marcus Turtellius Rufus.

EDCS-71200439.translation: To Mars. Titus Julius Verecundus, a veteran of the First Minervia Legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-71200439.summary: This is a votive inscription to Mars by a veteran of the First Minervia Legion.

EDCS-71200440.summary: This is a very brief votive inscription to Hercules.

EDCS-71300029.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Marcus Ulpius Epaphroditus, freedman of Augustus, procurator of the private treasury, procurator of the Tiburtine estates, [---].
EDCS-71300029.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Epaphroditus, a freedman of Augustus who held procuratorial posts.

EDCS-71300032.translation: To Gaius Rufius Marcellinus, most distinguished boy, grandson of [---] and of the Rufii, [---].
EDCS-71300032.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Gaius Rufius Marcellinus, whose maternal grandparents were from the Rufii family.

EDCS-71300035.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Virasius Venusius made this for his most dutiful and deserving brother, Publius Virasius Hermogenes.
EDCS-71300035.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Publius Virasius Venusius for his brother Publius Virasius Hermogenes.

EDCS-71300036.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Made for Primigenia, who deserved it.
EDCS-71300036.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Primigenia.

EDCS-71400197.translation: Aulus [---] Optati [---] son of Lucius Rustius.
EDCS-71400197.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or a funerary marker, mentioning individuals named Aulus, Optatus, and Lucius Rustius.

EDCS-72600217.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely mentioning the genitive of a Roman personal name, Publius Cornelius.

EDCS-72600224.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely mentioning the Roman personal name, Publius Cornelius.

EDCS-72600303.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely mentioning the genitive of a Roman personal name, Marcus Perennius.

EDCS-72600304.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely mentioning the genitive of a Roman personal name, Marcus Perennius.

EDCS-72600308.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely mentioning the genitive of a Roman personal name, Publius Cornelius.

EDCS-72600311.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely mentioning the genitive of a Roman personal name, Marcus Perennius.

EDCS-72900062.translation: Sextus Valerius Flaccus, son of Gaius; Gaius Herennius Ganymedes, freedman of Gaius; Sextus Sextius, freedman of Marcus; the three men, sacred to the Tutelary Hercules.
EDCS-72900062.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by three officials (IIIviri) to the god Hercules Tutanus, likely dating to the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-72900063.translation: [Unknown person], son of Gaius, Pontifex; Aulus Marcius Rentio, freedman of Aulus; Publius Vettius Antiocus, freedman of Publius; the three men, sacred to Mercury.
EDCS-72900063.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by three officials (IIIviri), including a Pontifex, to the god Mercury, likely dating to the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-72900064.translation: Lucius Firmius Pollio, son of Lucius, of the Clustumina tribe, quinquennalis, tribune of the soldiers of the 12th Gallic Legion and the Fourth Legion.
EDCS-72900064.summary: This inscription records the career of Lucius Firmius Pollio, who held civic office (quinquennalis) and military positions, likely from a colonia in the late 1st century BC or early 1st century AD.

EDCS-73800097.translation: To the spirits of the departed; to Faustinus, Fausticus, Festinus, and Festus, who deserved well.
EDCS-73800097.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring at least four individuals, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74000094.translation: Lucius Aelius Crassus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-74000094.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner or subject of a monument.

EDCS-74000095.translation: Lucius Avillius Brocchus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-74000095.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner or subject of a monument.

EDCS-74700531.translation: To the divine spirits. To Aufidia Fortunata, for her well-deserving freedwoman and wife. Lucius Aufidius Iustus.
EDCS-74700531.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a husband to his deceased wife.

EDCS-74700533.translation: Aulus Cosconius Custurna, son of Aulus, of the tribe Tromentina.
EDCS-74700533.summary: This is a personal name with tribal affiliation, likely an owner or subject of a monument.

EDCS-74700559.translation: Flavia Epictesis dedicated this gift to Fortuna.
EDCS-74700559.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Fortuna by Flavia Epictesis.

EDCS-75200393.translation: To the divine spirits. To Publius Aquillius Restitutus, a well-deserving fellow student. Marcus Ulpius Maximianus. Aquilia Felicitas, sister, made this for her most devoted brother.
EDCS-75200393.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a colleague and a sister for Publius Aquillius Restitutus.

EDCS-75600261.translation: To Junia Calliena, a most distinguished woman.
EDCS-75600261.summary: This is an epitaph honoring Junia Calliena, likely a woman of high social standing.

EDCS-76000156.translation: Lucius Campatius Tobrio, son of Lucius.
EDCS-76000156.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner or subject of a monument.

EDCS-76000158.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner or subject of a monument.

EDCS-76400076.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Hostilius Maximus and Hostilia Flora set up this monument to Tiberius Claudius Hilarion, who lived 50 years, for his well-deserved merit.
EDCS-76400076.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Hilarion, set up by his heirs, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-76800109.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sextus Vismatius Corinthus set up this monument to Sextus Vismatius Romanianus, who lived 3 years, 8 months, and 3 days. [and] Primigenia [---].
EDCS-76800109.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Sextus Vismatius Romanianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76900119.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Adinna Severus and Adinnia Maxima set up this monument to their most devoted father, Adinna Maximus, who lived 42 years, 7 months, and 5 days, for his well-deserved merit.
EDCS-76900119.summary: A funerary epitaph for Adinna Maximus, set up by his children, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-77700166.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Damio, freedman of Marcus Aburius [and of a woman, name illegible].
EDCS-77700166.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Damio, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-77700185.translation: Aurelia Agapes, his wife, and their sons set up this monument to Aurelius He[r]eneus, who lived 71 years and 4 days, a deacon for 5 years, and a presbyter for 30 years and 8 months, for his well-deserved merit.
EDCS-77700185.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Hereneus, a Christian cleric, set up by his wife and sons, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-78200024.translation: In the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar IV and Lucius Vitellius III.
EDCS-78200024.summary: A dating formula referencing the consuls Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus and Lucius Vitellius, likely from the year 47 CE.

EDCS-78200041.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The council of the Rusellani, devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-78200041.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Constantius I, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE, commissioned by the local council of the Rusellani.

EDCS-79300009.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Petronius Dignus, centurion of the XXII Primigenia legion, set up this monument to his excellent freedman, Publius Petronius Sosthenes.
EDCS-79300009.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Petronius Sosthenes, a freedman, set up by his patron, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-80300117.summary: A fragmentary inscription with personal names, possibly an ownership mark or a dedication, of uncertain date.

EDCS-80300120.summary: A fragmentary inscription with personal names, possibly an ownership mark or a dedication, of uncertain date.

EDCS-80600047.summary: This is a gravestone or personal monument inscribed with the name Lucius Primus.

EDCS-80600057.summary: This is a gravestone or personal monument inscribed with the name Gaius Numerius Restitutus.

EDCS-81800031.translation: To Gaius Clodius, son of Lucius, of the Pomptina tribe.
EDCS-81800031.summary: This is a dedication or epitaph for Gaius Clodius, son of Lucius, mentioning his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-81800059.translation: Quintus Fabius Eutychianus, patron and perpetual quinquennial, dedicated at his own expense to the virtue of the body of the college of dendrophori of the Carians quarter.
EDCS-81800059.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Quintus Fabius Eutychianus, a patron and official, to the dendrophori college in the Carians quarter.

EDCS-82400015.summary: This is a gravestone or personal monument inscribed with the name Sextus Gavi Celer.

EDCS-82900057.translation: Gaius Autronius, Marcus Roscius, son of Marcus, Gaius Baebius, freedman of Gaius, Gaius Praeconius, masters, to Tiberinus Mercurius, to Jupiter.
EDCS-82900057.summary: This fragmented inscription records the names of officials (magistri) and dedications to the deities Mercurius and Jupiter, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.
